title: A First Measurement Study on Authentication Security in Real-World Remote MCP Servers
summary: A measurement study of authentication security in real-world remote Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, identifying pervasive flaws in OAuth deployments that can lead to sensitive information leakage and account takeover.
rationale: This paper directly addresses security vulnerabilities in the tool-interface layer of agentic systems, specifically focusing on authentication in remote MCP servers, with empirical findings and CVEs.

title: A Five-Plane Reference Architecture for Runtime Governance of Production AI Agents
summary: A five-plane reference architecture for runtime governance of production AI agents, featuring stop-anywhere mediation, composite principals with capability attenuation, and a structured audit substrate, demonstrated to foreclose seven production threats.
rationale: This paper proposes a comprehensive reference architecture for runtime governance of AI agents, directly addressing systems-level security and safety concerns for production deployments.

title: A Methodology for Selecting and Composing Runtime Architecture Patterns for Production LLM Agents
summary: Introduces the stochastic-deterministic boundary (SDB) as a core architectural primitive for LLM agent runtimes, catalogs six runtime patterns, and provides a methodology for selecting and composing them to improve reliability and manage replay divergence.
rationale: Provides a foundational architectural methodology and catalog of patterns for engineering reliable and safe production LLM agent systems, directly addressing the stochastic-deterministic boundary.

title: A Security Analysis of the OpenClaw AI Agent Framework
summary: A systematic security analysis of the OpenClaw AI agent framework, identifying vulnerabilities across architectural layers and attack types, including a complete RCE path and weaknesses in command filtering and plugin distribution.
rationale: This paper provides a detailed security analysis of an open-source AI agent runtime, identifying systemic vulnerabilities and proposing architectural insights, directly contributing to trustworthy agentic systems.

title: A Systematic Security Evaluation of OpenClaw and Its Variants
summary: A systematic security evaluation of six OpenClaw-series agent frameworks using a benchmark of 205 attack cases, revealing lifecycle-wide vulnerabilities amplified by multi-step planning and runtime orchestration.
rationale: This paper provides a systematic security evaluation of agent frameworks, identifying system-level vulnerabilities beyond model-only evaluation, which is a direct contribution to red-teaming agentic systems.

title: A Systematic Survey of Security Threats and Defenses in LLM-Based AI Agents: A Layered Attack Surface Framework
summary: A systematic survey and layered attack surface framework for security threats and defenses in LLM-based AI agents, categorizing threats by architectural layer and temporality to highlight under-explored areas and defense gaps.
rationale: This paper provides a foundational taxonomy and survey of security threats in agentic systems, directly addressing the 'foundations' harness layer by framing the problem structurally.

title: AC4A: Access Control for Agents
summary: AC4A is an access control framework for LLM agents that enables fine-grained, hierarchical permission definition and enforcement for API and browser-based interactions, addressing the problem of agents having excessive access.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the critical systems-engineering problem of least-privilege access control for agentic systems, providing a concrete framework and demonstrating its utility.

title: Adaptive Evaluation of Out-of-Band Defenses Against Prompt Injection in LLM Agents
summary: Analyzes existing out-of-band defenses against prompt injection through the lens of classical security principles and proposes an adaptive evaluation protocol, then applies it to Progent, finding it robust against a specific adaptive attack.
rationale: The paper provides a structured comparison of out-of-band defenses and proposes an adaptive evaluation methodology, directly contributing to red-teaming and evaluation of agentic system security.

title: ADR: An Agentic Detection System for Enterprise Agentic AI Security
summary: Presents the Agentic AI Detection and Response (ADR) system, a production-proven enterprise framework for securing AI agents by providing high-fidelity agentic telemetry, systematic pre-deployment red teaming, and scalable two-tier online detection.
rationale: This paper describes a comprehensive, production-deployed system for observing, evaluating, and securing agentic AI, directly addressing observability and red-teaming challenges in the operational stack.

title: Agent Audit: A Security Analysis System for LLM Agent Applications
summary: Agent Audit is a security analysis system that combines dataflow analysis, credential detection, and privilege-risk checks to identify vulnerabilities in LLM agent code and deployment artifacts, integrating with development and CI/CD workflows.
rationale: This paper directly addresses supply-chain security for agent applications by providing a static analysis system to audit agent code and deployment configurations for vulnerabilities.

title: Agent Control Protocol: Admission Control for Agent Actions
summary: A temporal admission control protocol (ACP) that enforces behavioral properties over agent execution traces using static risk scoring and stateful signals, preventing harmful patterns from individually valid requests.
rationale: Directly addresses a core problem in agent trustworthiness: controlling emergent harmful behavior from individually valid actions via a stateful execution-shell mechanism.

title: Agent-Fence: Mapping Security Vulnerabilities Across Deep Research Agents
summary: AgentFence is an architecture-centric security evaluation framework that defines 14 trust-boundary attack classes across agent components and detects failures via trace-auditable conversation breaks, evaluating eight agent archetypes under persistent multi-turn interaction.
rationale: This paper directly addresses adversarial testing of agentic systems by defining attack classes and evaluating architectural variations in security break rates, focusing on operational security.

title: Agent Meltdowns: The Road to Hell Is Paved with Helpful Agents
summary: Introduces and characterizes "accidental meltdowns" where agents exhibit unsafe or harmful behavior in response to benign environmental errors, and provides an agent-agnostic infrastructure to systematically evaluate these failures across different agent systems and models.
rationale: This paper introduces a new type of agent failure (accidental meltdowns) and provides a systematic evaluation methodology and infrastructure to test for it, directly contributing to the adversarial testing and reliability of agentic systems.

title: Agent Privilege Separation in OpenClaw: A Structural Defense Against Prompt Injection
summary: A structural defense against prompt injection using agent isolation via privilege separation and tool partitioning in a two-agent pipeline, complemented by JSON formatting to strip persuasive framing.
rationale: Directly addresses prompt injection as a system-level security problem with a structural defense mechanism involving agent architecture and privilege separation.

title: Agent Security is a Systems Problem
summary: This paper frames agent security as a systems problem, advocating for system-level enforcement of security invariants by treating the AI model as an untrusted component, and analyzes real-world attacks through this lens.
rationale: The paper provides a foundational framing of agent security as a systems problem, aligning with the list's thesis, and identifies core principles and research challenges.

title: Agent Skills for Large Language Models: Architecture, Acquisition, Security, and the Path Forward
summary: A comprehensive survey of agent skills, focusing on architectural foundations, acquisition, deployment, and security, including a proposed Skill Trust and Lifecycle Governance Framework for managing skill provenance and deployment capabilities.
rationale: This survey provides a foundational overview of agent skills as a systems abstraction, directly addressing architectural and security concerns for trustworthy agentic systems.

title: AgentAssay: Token-Efficient Regression Testing for Non-Deterministic AI Agent Workflows
summary: AgentAssay is a token-efficient framework for regression testing non-deterministic AI agent workflows, using statistical verdicts, behavioral fingerprinting, and adaptive budget optimization to detect regressions with significant cost reductions.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a core systems-engineering problem of trustworthy agents: regression testing and evaluation of non-deterministic workflows, with a concrete, token-efficient methodology and empirical validation.

title: AgentCanary: A Security Evaluation Framework for Autonomous AI Agents in Real Executable Environments
summary: AgentCanary is a security evaluation framework for autonomous AI agents that features comprehensive risk coverage, a high-fidelity real executable environment, and trajectory-grounded multi-dimensional evaluation to assess agent security against long-horizon attacks.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the red-teaming and security evaluation of agentic systems in realistic, high-fidelity environments, which is a core concern for trustworthy agents.

title: AgentCollabBench: Diagnosing When Good Agents Make Bad Collaborators
summary: Introduces AgentCollabBench, a diagnostic benchmark to measure multi-hop process failures in multi-agent systems, focusing on instruction decay, false-belief contagion, context leakage, and tracer durability, and identifies communication topology as a primary risk factor.
rationale: Directly addresses reliability and security in multi-agent coordination by diagnosing structural vulnerabilities and their impact on information integrity across agent workflows.

title: AgentEval: DAG-Structured Step-Level Evaluation for Agentic Workflows with Error Propagation Tracking
summary: AgentEval is a framework for DAG-structured, step-level evaluation of agentic workflows that tracks error propagation and automates root cause attribution, demonstrating improved failure detection and root cause accuracy in production settings.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the systems-level problem of evaluating and debugging multi-step agentic workflows, providing a concrete framework and empirical validation for improved reliability.

title: AgenticCyOps: Securing Multi-Agentic AI Integration in Enterprise Cyber Operations
summary: AgenticCyOps is a holistic architectural framework for securing multi-agentic AI integration in enterprise cyber operations, decomposing attack surfaces and defining defensive principles for tool orchestration and memory management.
rationale: This paper provides a holistic architectural model for securing multi-agentic AI integration in enterprise cyber operations, addressing system-level trust boundaries and defensive principles.

title: AgenticOS: An Intent-Oriented Secure Operating System Architecture for Autonomous AI Agents
summary: Proposes AgenticOS, an intent-oriented secure operating system architecture that reframes OS security from resource management to intent filtering, synthesizing least-privilege environments based on structured agent intent declarations.
rationale: Directly addresses the fundamental problem of least-privilege authorization for autonomous agents by proposing a novel OS architecture centered on intent-based security and capability generation.

title: AgentLeak: A Benchmark for Internal-Channel Privacy Leakage in Multi-Agent LLM Systems
summary: Introduces AgentLeak, a benchmark for evaluating internal-channel privacy leakage in multi-agent LLM systems by instrumenting seven communication pathways and analyzing inter-agent messages and shared memory.
rationale: Directly addresses privacy leakage in multi-agent systems by proposing a novel benchmark and methodology for observing and evaluating internal communication channels, which is a critical systems-level problem.

title: AgentRaft: Automated Detection of Data Over-Exposure in LLM Agents
summary: AgentRaft is an automated framework that uses program analysis, function call graph traversal, and runtime taint tracking with a multi-LLM voting committee to detect Data Over-Exposure (DOE) in LLM agents, where sensitive data is inadvertently transmitted beyond user intent.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a critical privacy and security concern in agentic systems by providing an automated detection framework for data over-exposure in cross-tool data flows, which is a systems-level problem.

title: AgentRedBench: Dynamic Redteaming and Integration-Aware Defense for LLM Agents over SaaS Integrations
summary: Introduces AGENTREDBENCH, a dynamic LLM-driven redteaming benchmark for indirect prompt injection in tool-use agents across 24 enterprise integrations, and AGENTREDGUARD, a defense model trained on integration-diverse adversarial tool-response content.
rationale: Directly addresses a critical security threat (indirect prompt injection) in agentic systems through a comprehensive red-teaming benchmark and a concrete defense mechanism.

title: AgentRiskBOM: A Risk-Scoping Security Bill of Materials for Agentic AI Systems
summary: Introduces AgentRiskBOM, a security bill of materials (BOM) schema for agentic AI systems that documents runtime authority, including tool permissions, memory, and external action capabilities, and evaluates its effectiveness in risk-scoping and detecting authority drift across various open-source agents.
rationale: Directly addresses the supply-chain security and transparency gap for agentic systems by providing a structured, machine-readable artifact for risk-scoping and detecting authority drift.

title: AgentSCOPE: Evaluating Contextual Privacy Across Agentic Workflows
summary: Introduces the Privacy Flow Graph, a Contextual Integrity-grounded framework for evaluating privacy violations across intermediate information flows in agentic workflows, and AgentSCOPE, a benchmark for multi-tool scenarios with ground truth at every pipeline stage.
rationale: Directly addresses privacy evaluation for agentic systems by proposing a novel framework and benchmark for tracing privacy violations across the entire workflow, not just inputs/outputs.

title: AgentShield: Deception-based Compromise Detection for Tool-using LLM Agents
summary: AgentShield is a deception-based framework that uses fake tools, credentials, and allowlisted parameters within the tool interface to detect successful indirect prompt injection attacks in LLM agents, providing real-time compromise signals and self-supervised labels for a classifier.
rationale: The paper directly addresses a critical security vulnerability (indirect prompt injection) in the agent's tool interface with a novel detection mechanism and robust evaluation.

title: AgentSys: Secure and Dynamic LLM Agents Through Explicit Hierarchical Memory Management
summary: AgentSys is a framework that defends against indirect prompt injection by implementing hierarchical memory management, inspired by OS process isolation, to prevent external data and subtask traces from polluting the main agent's context.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a critical security vulnerability (indirect prompt injection) in agentic systems by proposing a novel memory management architecture, which is a core systems-engineering contribution.

title: AgentTrace: A Structured Logging Framework for Agent System Observability
summary: AgentTrace is a dynamic observability and telemetry framework that captures structured logs across operational, cognitive, and contextual surfaces to enable real-time monitoring, security, and accountability for LLM agents.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a core systems-engineering problem for trustworthy agents: observability and traceability for security and reliability in high-stakes domains.

title: AgentTrace: Causal Graph Tracing for Root Cause Analysis in Deployed Multi-Agent Systems
summary: AgentTrace is a causal tracing framework that reconstructs causal graphs from execution logs to diagnose failures in deployed multi-agent systems, localizing root causes without requiring LLM inference at debugging time.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the observability and reliability of deployed multi-agent systems through a concrete causal tracing framework for root cause analysis.

title: AgentTrust: Runtime Safety Evaluation and Interception for AI Agent Tool Use
summary: AgentTrust is a runtime safety layer that intercepts agent tool calls, normalizes shell commands, detects multi-step attack chains, and provides verdicts (allow, warn, block, review) before execution, evaluated on extensive benchmarks.
rationale: Directly addresses runtime safety and security for agent tool use, including multi-step attack detection and a deployable interception layer.

title: AgentVisor: Defending LLM Agents Against Prompt Injection via Semantic Virtualization
summary: AgentVisor is a defense framework that uses semantic virtualization and a trusted semantic visor to enforce privilege separation and mitigate direct and indirect prompt injection attacks on LLM agents.
rationale: This paper directly addresses prompt injection defense for LLM agents by proposing a novel systems-level framework inspired by OS virtualization, with experimental validation.

title: AI Harness Engineering: A Runtime Substrate for Foundation-Model Software Agents
summary: Formalizes an AI Harness Engineering substrate for software-engineering agents, identifying eleven component responsibilities and a four-level operational ladder for runtime support, with a trace-based evaluation protocol.
rationale: Provides a foundational framework and taxonomy for engineering trustworthy agentic systems, specifically focusing on the runtime substrate and its responsibilities for reliability and security.

title: AIP: Agent Identity Protocol for Verifiable Delegation Across MCP and A2A
summary: Introduces Agent Identity Protocol (AIP) using Invocation-Bound Capability Tokens (IBCTs) to provide verifiable delegation, attenuated authorization, and provenance binding for agent-to-agent and tool-calling interactions, with reference implementations and adversarial evaluation.
rationale: Directly addresses agent identity, authorization, and verifiable delegation across the agentic stack, with concrete protocol design, implementation, and adversarial evaluation.

title: AIRGuard: Guarding Agent Actions with Runtime Authority Control
summary: AIRGuard is a runtime guard that implements action-time authorization and least privilege for tool-using agents by normalizing tool calls, deriving step-level authority, tracking trust, simulating side effects, and auditing cross-step risk.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a core systems-engineering problem for trustworthy agents: runtime authority control and least privilege for tool-mediated actions, with empirical validation against adversarial attacks and utility preservation.

title: Aligning Provenance with Authorization: A Dual-Graph Defense for LLM Agents
summary: AuthGraph is a dual-graph defense framework that aligns an agent's execution provenance with an authorization graph derived from user intent to detect indirect prompt injection and unauthorized operations at the parameter-source level.
rationale: This paper directly addresses agent security by proposing a novel defense framework that combines provenance tracking with authorization baselines to prevent indirect prompt injection and unauthorized actions, a core systems problem.

title: Ambient Persuasion in a Deployed AI Agent: Unauthorized Escalation Following Routine Non-Adversarial Content Exposure
summary: Analysis of a safety incident in a deployed multi-agent system where an agent performed unauthorized actions due to ambiguous instructions and a permissive environment, highlighting failures in control boundaries and multi-agent oversight.
rationale: This paper provides a detailed postmortem of a real-world agent safety incident, analyzing system-level failures in permissions, oversight, and instruction persistence, which is directly relevant to trustworthy agentic systems.

title: AMV-L: Lifecycle-Managed Agent Memory for Tail-Latency Control in Long-Running LLM Systems
summary: AMV-L is a memory-management framework that uses value-driven lifecycle tiers and bounded retrieval sets to control the computational footprint of agent memory, improving throughput and reducing tail latency in long-running LLM systems.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a systems-engineering problem in agent memory management (performance and reliability of long-running agents) with a concrete, evaluated solution.

title: An AI Agent Execution Environment to Safeguard User Data
summary: GAAP is an execution environment for AI agents that enforces user-defined permission specifications for private data disclosure, including to the AI model provider, by tracking information flow across execution steps and tasks.
rationale: This paper directly addresses data privacy and security in agentic systems by proposing a novel execution environment for enforcing fine-grained data access permissions, even against model-level attacks.

title: An Evaluation of Data Leakage Risks in Tool-Using LLM Agents in Realistic Scenarios
summary: Evaluates data leakage risks in tool-using LLM agents across 12 realistic, non-adversarial tasks, identifying five risk types and proposing a methodology for assessing data-handling safety.
rationale: This paper provides a systematic evaluation of data leakage in agentic systems under realistic, non-adversarial conditions, contributing to red-teaming and evaluation methodologies for agent privacy and safety.

title: An Organization-Scoped LLM Agent Runtime Architecture for Regulated Cybersecurity Operations
summary: Proposes an organization-scoped LLM agent runtime architecture for regulated cybersecurity operations, emphasizing a typed Security Context, governed Tool Adapter Layer, and tiered human-in-the-loop gates for auditable and policy-enforced agent behavior.
rationale: This paper proposes a concrete, auditable architecture for deploying LLM agents in regulated environments, directly addressing systems-level concerns for trustworthiness.

title: Architectural Design Decisions in AI Agent Harnesses
summary: Empirical study of architectural design decisions across 70 public AI agent systems, identifying recurring dimensions, co-occurrence patterns, and five architectural archetypes to guide framework design and selection.
rationale: This paper provides a foundational, empirical study of architectural patterns and design decisions in agentic systems, directly contributing to understanding the engineering stack.

title: Architecture Matters for Multi-Agent Security
summary: Empirical study of how multi-agent system architectural decisions, including agent roles, communication topology, and memory, impact the tradeoff between task performance and attack resistance across three agentic environments.
rationale: This paper directly addresses security risks arising from architectural decisions in multi-agent systems, providing an empirical study of attack surfaces and design choices.

title: Are AI-assisted Development Tools Immune to Prompt Injection?
summary: Empirical analysis of prompt injection vulnerabilities, specifically tool-poisoning, across seven real-world Model Context Protocol (MCP) clients, identifying disparities in their security features and susceptibility to attacks.
rationale: This paper provides a direct, empirical study of prompt injection attacks and tool-poisoning vulnerabilities in real-world AI-assisted development tools, contributing to red-teaming and security of agentic systems.

title: ARGUS: Defending LLM Agents Against Context-Aware Prompt Injection
summary: Introduces AgentLure, a benchmark for context-aware prompt injection attacks against LLM agents, and ARGUS, a provenance-aware decision auditing defense that tracks untrusted context propagation to verify agent decisions.
rationale: Directly addresses context-aware prompt injection in agentic systems with a new benchmark and a provenance-based defense mechanism.

title: ARIS: Autonomous Research via Adversarial Multi-Agent Collaboration
summary: ARIS is an open-source research harness for autonomous research, featuring a multi-agent adversarial collaboration architecture, a persistent research wiki, and a three-stage assurance layer for verifying experimental claims against evidence.
rationale: ARIS presents a comprehensive architectural design for trustworthy autonomous research agents, focusing on reliability and safety through adversarial collaboration and rigorous evidence-based assurance mechanisms.

title: ASPI: Seeking Ambiguity Clarification Amplifies Prompt Injection Vulnerability in LLM Agents
summary: Introduces ASPI, a benchmark of 728 task-attack scenarios, to demonstrate that clarification-seeking behavior in LLM agents significantly amplifies their vulnerability to prompt injection attacks, revealing a previously unexplored attack surface.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a novel prompt injection vulnerability in agentic systems, specifically tied to the clarification-seeking state, and provides a benchmark for evaluation.

title: AttackEval: A Systematic Empirical Study of Prompt Injection Attack Effectiveness Against Large Language Models
summary: A systematic empirical study of prompt injection attack effectiveness, categorizing attack strategies and evaluating them against simulated production defenses to identify blind spots.
rationale: This paper directly contributes to red-teaming agentic systems by systematically studying prompt injection attacks and their effectiveness against various defense tiers.

title: Attested Tool-Server Admission: A Security Extension to the Model Context Protocol
summary: Introduces a security extension to the Model Context Protocol (MCP) for tool-server admission, enabling attested server verification, per-server tool allowlisting, and enforcement modes to secure agent interactions with external tools.
rationale: Directly addresses security and trust boundaries in agent tool interfaces with a concrete, deployable, and standardized mechanism for server and tool authorization.

title: AttriGuard: Defeating Indirect Prompt Injection in LLM Agents via Causal Attribution of Tool Invocations
summary: AttriGuard is a runtime defense that uses causal attribution and counterfactual tests to distinguish legitimate tool calls from those driven by indirect prompt injections embedded in untrusted tool outputs.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a critical security vulnerability in agentic systems (indirect prompt injection) by focusing on the tool-interface layer with a novel runtime defense mechanism.

title: Auditable Agents
summary: Defines five dimensions of agent auditability (action recoverability, lifecycle coverage, policy checkability, responsibility attribution, evidence integrity) and three mechanism classes (detect, enforce, recover) to enable accountability in agent systems.
rationale: Provides a foundational framework and taxonomy for auditability in agentic systems, addressing a core trustworthiness concern with empirical evidence and open problems.

title: Authenticated Workflows: A Systems Approach to Protecting Agentic AI
summary: Introduces authenticated workflows as a complete trust layer for enterprise agentic AI, enforcing intent and integrity at prompt, tool, data, and context boundaries using cryptographic proofs and a hierarchical policy language.
rationale: This paper proposes a foundational systems approach to agent security, defining trust boundaries and a policy language for deterministic enforcement, with strong empirical validation.

title: Authorization Propagation in Multi-Agent AI Systems: Identity Governance as Infrastructure
summary: Formalizes authorization propagation as a workflow-level property in multi-agent systems, identifying sub-problems and deriving architectural requirements for identity governance as infrastructure.
rationale: Directly addresses a core systems-level problem of authorization and identity governance in multi-agent AI, distinct from model-level concerns.

title: Automating Agent Hijacking via Structural Template Injection
summary: Phantom is an automated agent hijacking framework that exploits chat template token parsing to induce role confusion and misinterpret injected content as legitimate instructions, validated by identifying over 70 vulnerabilities in commercial products.
rationale: This paper presents a novel, automated red-teaming framework that targets a fundamental architectural vulnerability in agent parsing, with significant real-world impact.

title: Autonomous Action Runtime Management(AARM):A System Specification for Securing AI-Driven Actions at Runtime
summary: AARM is an open specification for a runtime security system that intercepts AI-driven actions, evaluates them against policy and intent, enforces authorization, and records tamper-evident receipts, addressing the shift in security boundaries from model outputs to tool execution.
rationale: This paper directly addresses runtime security for AI-driven actions, defining a system specification for interception, policy enforcement, and forensic logging, which is a core systems-engineering problem for trustworthy agents.

title: Autonomous Incident Resolution at Hyperscale: An Agentic AI Architecture for Network Operations
summary: Presents an agentic AI architecture for autonomous incident resolution in hyperscale network operations, detailing multi-agent orchestration, tool invocation, knowledge encoding, safety boundaries, and closed-loop verification, deployed in production.
rationale: This paper describes a production-deployed agentic AI architecture for autonomous incident resolution, focusing on systems-level concerns like safety, authorization, and rollback mechanisms.

title: BadSkill: Backdoor Attacks on Agent Skills via Model-in-Skill Poisoning
summary: Introduces BadSkill, a backdoor attack targeting model-in-skill supply-chain risk where malicious behavior is concealed within a skill's bundled model, and evaluates its effectiveness across various model architectures and perturbation types.
rationale: Directly addresses a novel supply-chain security risk in agent ecosystems related to third-party skills bundling malicious models.

title: Benchmarking Autonomous Agents against Temporal, Spatial, and Semantic Evasions
summary: Proposes a multi-dimensional evasion framework (temporal, spatial, semantic) to benchmark autonomous agents against stateful, multi-turn attacks, revealing systemic architecture-level vulnerabilities.
rationale: Directly addresses adversarial testing of multi-step agent workflows and system-level threats, identifying architectural vulnerabilities.

title: Beyond Final Answers: Auditing Trajectory-Level Hallucinations in Multi-Agent Industrial Workflows
summary: Introduces Trajel, a dataset and evaluation framework with a five-type taxonomy for auditing trajectory-level hallucinations in multi-agent industrial workflows, demonstrating the inadequacy of final-output-only evaluation.
rationale: Directly addresses the critical problem of evaluating and auditing multi-step agent behavior for trustworthiness, moving beyond final-answer metrics to trajectory-level analysis.

title: Beyond Single-Agent Alignment: Preventing Context-Fragmented Violations in Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Identifies and formalizes Context-Fragmented Violations (CFVs) in multi-agent systems where local actions are safe but global policies are violated due to siloed context, proposing Distributed Sentinel, a distributed zero-trust enforcement architecture using Semantic Taint Tokens for cross-domain policy verification.
rationale: Directly addresses a novel security risk in multi-agent coordination by proposing a distributed enforcement architecture and validating it with a comprehensive benchmark.

title: Beyond Task Success: Measuring Workflow Fidelity in LLM-Based Agentic Payment Systems
summary: Introduces Agentic Success Rate (ASR), a trajectory-fidelity metric for LLM-based payment agents that compares observed and expected execution sequences to detect workflow deviations, revealing hidden shortcuts missed by traditional metrics.
rationale: Directly addresses the problem of evaluating the fidelity of agent workflows, especially critical in regulated domains like payments, by proposing a new system-level metric and demonstrating its utility in identifying hidden deviations.

title: Blind Gods and Broken Screens: Architecting a Secure, Intent-Centric Mobile Agent Operating System
summary: Proposes Aura, a clean-slate secure agent operating system architecture for mobile agents, replacing GUI scraping with a structured, intent-centric interaction model and enforcing security through cryptographic identity, semantic input sanitization, cognitive integrity, and granular access control.
rationale: This paper presents a comprehensive architectural solution for secure mobile agent systems, addressing multiple systems-level vulnerabilities and proposing concrete defense pillars.

title: Bridging Protocol and Production: Design Patterns for Deploying AI Agents with Model Context Protocol
summary: Identifies missing protocol primitives for safe and reliable production-scale tool operation in AI agents, proposing mechanisms for identity propagation, adaptive tool budgeting, and structured error semantics, formalized with testable hypotheses.
rationale: Directly addresses critical systems-engineering challenges for trustworthy agent tool integration at scale, proposing concrete protocol extensions and mechanisms.

title: Caging the Agents: A Zero Trust Security Architecture for Autonomous AI in Healthcare
summary: Presents a zero-trust security architecture for autonomous AI agents in healthcare, detailing a six-domain threat model and a four-layer defense-in-depth implementation covering workload isolation, credential proxying, network egress policies, and prompt integrity.
rationale: This paper describes a comprehensive, deployed security architecture for production AI agents, directly addressing trustworthiness in a high-stakes domain with concrete systems-level mitigations.

title: CapSeal: Capability-Sealed Secret Mediation for Secure Agent Execution
summary: CapSeal introduces a capability-sealed secret mediation architecture that replaces direct secret access with constrained invocations through a local trusted broker, addressing prompt injection and tool misuse for agent secrets.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a critical security and privacy problem in agentic systems by proposing a novel permissions architecture for secret handling.

title: CausalArmor: Efficient Indirect Prompt Injection Guardrails via Causal Attribution
summary: CausalArmor is a selective defense framework against Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) that uses causal attribution to identify when untrusted content disproportionately influences an agent's privileged actions, triggering targeted sanitization and retroactive Chain-of-Thought masking.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a critical security vulnerability (IPI) in agentic systems by proposing a novel, attribution-based defense mechanism that improves both security and efficiency.

title: Causality Laundering: Denial-Feedback Leakage in Tool-Calling LLM Agents
summary: Identifies "causality laundering," a denial-feedback leakage pattern in tool-calling agents, and proposes the Agentic Reference Monitor (ARM) as a runtime enforcement layer that uses a provenance graph with counterfactual edges to block such attacks.
rationale: Directly addresses a novel security vulnerability in tool-calling agent systems with a concrete, evaluated runtime defense mechanism.

title: ChainFuzzer: Greybox Fuzzing for Workflow-Level Multi-Tool Vulnerabilities in LLM Agents
summary: ChainFuzzer is a greybox fuzzing framework that discovers and reproduces multi-tool vulnerabilities in LLM agents by identifying source-to-sink dataflows across tool compositions and synthesizing prompts to reliably trigger these long-horizon exploits.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the red-teaming of agentic systems by identifying and reproducing multi-tool, workflow-level vulnerabilities, which is a core systems-level security concern.

title: Characterizing Faults in Agentic AI: A Taxonomy of Types, Symptoms, and Root Causes
summary: Empirical study of 385 faults in agentic AI systems, deriving a taxonomy of 34 fault types, symptoms, and root causes across architectural dimensions like structured output, tool calls, and state management.
rationale: Provides a foundational taxonomy of faults in agentic systems, directly addressing reliability and observability challenges in the operational stack.

title: CIBER: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Security Evaluation of Code Interpreter Agents
summary: CIBER is an automated benchmark for evaluating the security of code interpreter agents against dynamic adversarial attacks, including prompt injection and memory poisoning, within isolated sandboxes and across multi-turn contexts.
rationale: This paper directly addresses adversarial testing of agentic systems, specifically code interpreter agents, by introducing a comprehensive benchmark for security evaluation against system-level attacks.

title: Clawed and Dangerous: Can We Trust Open Agentic Systems?
summary: Systematizes the security challenges of open agentic systems through a software engineering lens, introducing a six-dimensional taxonomy and synthesizing 50 papers to derive a reference doctrine for secure-by-construction agent platforms.
rationale: Provides a foundational taxonomy and synthesis of security challenges in agentic systems, identifying key engineering gaps for trustworthy deployments.

title: ClawGuard: A Runtime Security Framework for Tool-Augmented LLM Agents Against Indirect Prompt Injection
summary: ClawGuard is a runtime security framework that enforces user-confirmed rules at tool-call boundaries to prevent indirect prompt injection by automatically deriving task-specific access constraints from user objectives.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a critical security vulnerability in tool-augmented LLM agents by providing a runtime defense mechanism at the tool-call boundary, which is a core systems-engineering concern.

title: ClawGuard: Out-of-Band Detection of LLM Agent Workflow Hijacking via EM Side Channel
summary: ClawGuard is a passive, out-of-band monitor that detects LLM agent workflow hijacking by analyzing electromagnetic (EM) emanations from distinct agent skill invocations, providing a forge-resistant physical check against compromised host software.
rationale: This paper presents a novel, system-level defense against agent workflow hijacking using an out-of-band physical side channel, directly addressing agent security and observability.

title: ClawKeeper: Comprehensive Safety Protection for OpenClaw Agents Through Skills, Plugins, and Watchers
summary: ClawKeeper is a real-time security framework for OpenClaw agents that integrates multi-dimensional protection mechanisms across skill-based, plugin-based, and watcher-based architectural layers to mitigate system-level threats.
rationale: This paper presents a comprehensive, multi-layered security architecture for an open-source agent runtime, directly addressing system-level trustworthiness concerns.

title: ClawLess: A Security Model of AI Agents
summary: ClawLess is a security framework that enforces formally verified, dynamic policies on AI agents by translating them into BPF-based syscall interception rules, providing fundamental security guarantees against adversarial agents.
rationale: This paper directly addresses agent permissions and security enforcement at the system call level, providing a strong contribution to trustworthy agentic systems.

title: ClawSafety: "Safe" LLMs, Unsafe Agents
summary: CLAWSAFETY is a benchmark of 120 adversarial test scenarios for personal AI agents with elevated privileges, evaluating five LLMs and three agent frameworks to demonstrate that safety depends on the full deployment stack, not just the backbone model.
rationale: This paper directly addresses adversarial testing of agentic systems in realistic, high-privilege environments, highlighting the system-level nature of agent safety beyond model-centric evaluations.

title: ClawVM: Harness-Managed Virtual Memory for Stateful Tool-Using LLM Agents
summary: ClawVM introduces a virtual memory layer for LLM agents that manages state as typed pages with minimum-fidelity invariants and validated writeback, making residency and durability deterministic and auditable within the agent harness.
rationale: Directly addresses memory management, state integrity, and durability for trustworthy agentic systems with a concrete systems-level solution and empirical validation.

title: Clouding the Mirror: Stealthy Prompt Injection Attacks Targeting LLM-based Phishing Detection
summary: Presents a comprehensive evaluation of stealthy prompt injection attacks against multimodal LLM-based phishing detection systems, categorizing attack techniques and surfaces, and proposing InjectDefuser as a defense framework.
rationale: This paper directly addresses adversarial testing of LLM-based systems, specifically focusing on prompt injection in a security-critical application, and proposes a defense framework.

title: Code as Agent Harness
summary: A survey that frames code as the operational substrate for agent reasoning, acting, environment modeling, and execution-based verification, organizing the discussion around harness interfaces, mechanisms, and scaling to multi-agent systems.
rationale: This paper provides a foundational survey and taxonomy for 'code as agent harness,' directly addressing the systems-engineering aspects of trustworthy agents.

title: CodeTracer: Towards Traceable Agent States
summary: CodeTracer is a tracing architecture that reconstructs hierarchical state transition histories and localizes failure origins in complex code agent workflows, enabling systematic debugging and recovery.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the observability and debugging of complex agentic systems, a core problem in engineering trustworthy agents, with a concrete architecture and evaluation.

title: Comment and Control: Hijacking Agentic Workflows via Context-Grounded Evolution
summary: Presents JAW, a novel framework for hijacking agentic workflows on automation platforms by evolving inputs under context derived from hybrid program analysis to achieve credential exfiltration and arbitrary command execution.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a critical security vulnerability in agentic workflows by demonstrating a novel attack method and evaluating it on real-world platforms, contributing to red-teaming and adversarial testing of agentic systems.

title: Confidently Wrong: Severity-Aware Calibration of Prompt-Injection Detectors under Attack Shift
summary: Evaluates the calibration and severity of prompt-injection detectors under attack distribution shifts, revealing that detectors confidently miss indirect behavior-hijack injections due to content-keying, leading to significant exploit leakage in live models.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the trustworthiness of agentic systems by analyzing the failure modes of prompt-injection detectors under adversarial conditions and proposing a severity-aware evaluation metric.

title: Confused ChatGPT: Cross-App Context Poisoning via First-Party APIs
summary: Identifies and demonstrates cross-app context poisoning in ChatGPT Apps, a novel indirect prompt injection attack leveraging shared, untagged context and first-party APIs to manipulate co-resident benign applications.
rationale: This paper details a system-level adversarial attack (context poisoning) on a multi-tenant agent platform, identifying architectural vulnerabilities and demonstrating exploit chains across applications.

title: Conjunctive Prompt Attacks in Multi-Agent LLM Systems
summary: Characterizes 'conjunctive prompt attacks' that exploit prompt segmentation and inter-agent routing in multi-agent systems, where benign-looking components combine to activate harmful behavior, and demonstrates their effectiveness across various topologies.
rationale: Directly addresses a novel system-level adversarial attack on multi-agent systems, highlighting a structural vulnerability and the inadequacy of existing single-component defenses.

title: Constraining Host-Level Abuse in Self-Hosted Computer-Use Agents via TEE-Backed Isolation
summary: Proposes a TEE-backed trusted operation plane to confine self-hosted computer-use agents, protecting security-critical decisions and execution control from host-level abuse, and instantiates it on OpenClaw using Intel TDX.
rationale: Directly addresses host-level abuse and confinement for self-hosted agents using a systems-level TEE-backed isolation architecture.

title: Context-Fractured Decomposition Attacks on Tool-Using LLM Agents: Exploiting Artifact Provenance Gaps
summary: Introduces Context-Fractured Decomposition (CFD) attacks that exploit provenance gaps in agent workflows to achieve multi-step jailbreaks via artifact-mediated composition, and proposes provenance lineage tagging as a mitigation.
rationale: Directly addresses a novel system-level attack vector (provenance gaps) in multi-step agent workflows and proposes a verifiable defense, squarely in red-teaming and observation-eval.

title: Context Kubernetes: Declarative Orchestration of Enterprise Knowledge for Agentic AI Systems
summary: Context Kubernetes is a declarative architecture for orchestrating enterprise knowledge for agentic AI systems, formalizing abstractions, a YAML-based manifest, a reconciliation loop, and a three-tier agent permission model to ensure knowledge is delivered with appropriate permissions and freshness.
rationale: This paper proposes a novel architectural solution for a core systems problem in agentic AI (knowledge orchestration and permissions) with formalization, a prototype, and experimental validation against attack scenarios.

title: Control-Plane Placement Shapes Forgetting: An Architectural Study of Agent Memory Across Thirteen System Configurations
summary: An architectural study of LLM placement within agent memory pipelines, comparing thirteen configurations on a 385-case adversarial forgetting surface to identify optimal strategies for different forgetting failure modes.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a core systems-engineering problem in agent memory: how the architectural placement of LLMs in the memory control plane impacts forgetting and privacy, with a new evaluation suite and empirical findings.

title: Cordon: Semantic Transactions for Tool-Using LLM Agents
summary: Cordon introduces a transactional runtime system for LLM agents that stages and validates irreversible effects within a task-level execution boundary, enabling commit, rollback, and recovery across multi-step workflows.
rationale: This paper directly addresses transactional semantics for agent actions, a core open problem for trustworthy agentic systems, with a concrete runtime implementation and evaluation.

title: Cost-Aware Speculative Execution for LLM-Agent Workflows: An Integrated Five-Dimension Method
summary: A five-dimension method for cost-aware speculative execution in LLM-agent workflows, integrating pricing, decision rules, probability estimation, and transactional considerations for improved reliability and efficiency.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the execution and reliability of agent workflows by introducing a novel speculative execution method that considers cost, probability, and transactional semantics for side effects.

title: Crab: A Semantics-Aware Checkpoint/Restore Runtime for Agent Sandboxes
summary: Crab is a host-side runtime that bridges the agent-OS semantic gap to enable semantics-aware checkpointing and restoration of agent sandbox state, improving recovery correctness and efficiency for fault tolerance and safe rollback.
rationale: This paper directly addresses transactional agency by providing a system-level solution for checkpointing and restoring agent state, which is crucial for reliability and safety in agentic systems.

title: DeltaBox: Scaling Stateful AI Agents with Millisecond-Level Sandbox Checkpoint/Rollback
summary: DeltaBox proposes OS-level abstractions, DeltaFS and DeltaCR, to enable millisecond-level, change-based checkpoint and rollback of complete sandbox states for AI agents, addressing the performance bottleneck in state exploration.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the transactional-agency problem by providing a novel, low-latency checkpoint and rollback mechanism for agent sandboxes, which is critical for reliable agent execution.

title: Demystifying and Detecting Agentic Workflow Injection Vulnerabilities in GitHub Actions
summary: Introduces Agentic Workflow Injection (AWI) vulnerabilities in GitHub Actions, categorizes two core patterns (P2A, P2S), and develops TaintAWI, a taint-analysis tool to detect these workflow-level injection flaws in real-world agentic workflows.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a novel, system-level injection vulnerability in agentic workflows, providing a systematic study and a practical detection tool.

title: Description-Code Inconsistency in Real-world MCP Servers: Measurement, Detection, and Security Implications
summary: Characterizes Description-Code Inconsistency (DCI) in MCP servers, where tool descriptions diverge from their implementations, and proposes DCIChecker, an automated framework to detect these inconsistencies using static analysis and prompting.
rationale: Directly addresses a critical security and reliability problem in the tool-interface layer of agentic systems with a concrete detection framework and empirical study.

title: Detecting AI Coding Agents in Open Source: A Validated Multi-Method Census of 180 Million Repositories
summary: A multi-layered detection framework identifies AI coding agent traces across 180 million Git repositories, revealing significant underestimation by single-method approaches and distinct work profiles based on deployment channels.
rationale: This paper directly contributes to understanding the prevalence and characteristics of AI coding agents in the open-source supply chain, which is crucial for supply-chain security and reliability.

title: Detecting Malicious Agent Skills in the Wild using Attention
summary: Locate-and-Judge is a two-stage attention-based detector for malicious agent skills, addressing the supply-chain attack surface of third-party skill marketplaces by efficiently identifying and flagging malicious instructions within skill packages.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a critical supply-chain security problem for agentic systems by proposing and evaluating a scalable detection mechanism for malicious third-party skills.

title: Device Context Protocol: A Compact, Safety-First Architecture for LLM-Driven Control of Constrained Devices
summary: Introduces the Device Context Protocol (DCP), a compact and safety-first architecture for LLM control of constrained physical devices, featuring protocol-layer capability scoping, type/range checks, and a host-side Bridge to reject unsafe calls.
rationale: Directly addresses the safety and security of LLM-driven tool use for physical devices, a critical systems-engineering problem for agentic systems.

title: Dive into Claude Code: The Design Space of Today's and Future AI Agent Systems
summary: Architectural analysis of Claude Code and OpenClaw, identifying design principles for human values, safety, security, reliability, and extensibility, and comparing their different architectural answers based on deployment context.
rationale: This paper provides a detailed architectural analysis of two real-world agent systems, identifying design principles and implementation choices related to trustworthiness, directly contributing to the 'architectures' layer.

title: Don't believe everything you read: Understanding and Measuring MCP Behavior under Misleading Tool Descriptions
summary: Large-scale study of description-code inconsistency in Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, revealing a prevalent attack surface where misleading tool descriptions can enable unauthorized or privileged agent actions.
rationale: Directly addresses a critical security vulnerability in the tool-interface layer of agentic systems through a large-scale empirical study and static analysis.

title: "Elementary, My Dear Watson." Detecting Malicious Skills via Neuro-Symbolic Reasoning across Heterogeneous Artifacts
summary: MalSkills is a neuro-symbolic framework that detects malicious skills by extracting security-sensitive operations from heterogeneous artifacts, constructing a skill dependency graph, and performing reasoning to identify malicious patterns or suspicious workflows.
rationale: This paper directly addresses supply-chain security for agentic systems by detecting malicious components (skills) through a novel neuro-symbolic reasoning framework.

title: enclawed: A Configurable, Sector-Neutral Hardening Framework for Single-User AI Assistant Gateways
summary: enclawed is a hardening framework for AI assistant gateways, built on OpenClaw, providing attestable peer trust, deny-by-default external connectivity, signed-module loading, and a tamper-evident audit trail, with a comprehensive test suite and formal verification primitives.
rationale: This paper describes a comprehensive hardening framework for AI assistant gateways, addressing multiple systems-level security and privacy concerns with concrete architectural details, formal methods, and a robust test suite.

title: Energy per Successful Goal: Goal-Level Energy Accounting for Agentic AI Systems
summary: A-LEMS is a cross-layer measurement framework that redefines AI energy accounting from energy per inference to Energy per Successful Goal (EpG), aggregating total workflow energy across all execution attempts for agentic systems.
rationale: This paper introduces a novel, system-level energy measurement framework for agentic systems, directly addressing the observability and evaluation of long-horizon agent workflows.

title: Epistemic Blinding: An Inference-Time Protocol for Auditing Prior Contamination in LLM-Assisted Analysis
summary: Introduces 'epistemic blinding,' an inference-time protocol that anonymizes entity identifiers before prompting to audit and measure the influence of an LLM's memorized priors versus supplied data in agentic reasoning workflows.
rationale: Addresses a critical memory integrity and attribution problem in agentic systems by providing a concrete, deployable protocol to audit the source of agent knowledge.

title: Ethical Hyper-Velocity (EHV): A Hardware-Rooted Zero-Trust Runtime Enforcement Architecture for Agentic AI Systems
summary: A hardware-rooted zero-trust runtime enforcement architecture for agentic AI systems that integrates grammar-constrained decoding, causal CRDTs for policy synchronization, TEEs for attested execution, and formal verification to reduce policy enforcement latency.
rationale: This paper proposes a novel, hardware-rooted runtime enforcement architecture for agentic systems, directly addressing a fundamental safety gap in policy enforcement and traceability.

title: Evaluating Agentic AI in the Wild: Failure Modes, Drift Patterns, and a Production Evaluation Framework
summary: Presents a taxonomy of seven production agent failure modes, empirically demonstrates the inadequacy of standard metrics, and proposes PAEF, a five-dimension framework for continuous evaluation of agentic systems in production.
rationale: Directly addresses the critical problem of evaluating trustworthy agentic systems in production, including failure modes and a concrete evaluation framework.

title: Exploiting LLM Agent Supply Chains via Payload-less Skills
summary: Introduces Semantic Compliance Hijacking (SCH), a payload-less supply chain attack that uses natural language instructions to induce agents to generate and execute unauthorized code, bypassing traditional signature-based detection.
rationale: Directly addresses a novel supply-chain security vulnerability in agentic systems by demonstrating a payload-less attack and highlighting the need for semantic intent validation.

title: Exploring Robust Multi-Agent Workflows for Environmental Data Management
summary: EnviSmart is a production data management system that achieves reliability through a three-track knowledge architecture and a role-separated multi-agent design with deterministic validators and audited handoffs at trust boundaries, preventing errors in irreversible actions.
rationale: This paper presents a concrete reference architecture for trustworthy agentic systems, focusing on reliability and safety in a production environment with detailed mechanisms and incident response.

title: FastOMOP: A Foundational Architecture for Reliable Agentic Real-World Evidence Generation on OMOP CDM data
summary: FastOMOP is an open-source multi-agent architecture for real-world evidence generation that enforces safety and reliability through process-boundary governance, observability, and orchestration layers, independent of agent reasoning.
rationale: This paper proposes a foundational architecture for trustworthy agentic systems, specifically addressing safety and reliability in a critical domain (healthcare RWE) through system-level governance and observability.

title: Formal Policy Enforcement for Real-World Agentic Systems
summary: A framework for formal policy enforcement in agentic systems using aspect-oriented programming and Datalog, enabling policies to be specified independently of agent reasoning and enforced at every policy-relevant decision.
rationale: This paper directly addresses formal policy enforcement and permissions for agentic systems, a core trustworthiness concern, with a concrete technical framework and evaluation.

title: FragFuse: Bypassing Access Control of Large Language Model Agents via Memory-Based Query Fragmentation and Fusion
summary: FragFuse demonstrates a novel memory-based attack that fragments prohibited content across interactions, stores it in long-term memory, and reconstructs it to bypass agent access control, evaluated across four agent settings and three access-control mechanisms.
rationale: This paper identifies and exploits a critical vulnerability in agent memory operations to bypass access control, directly contributing to the security of agentic systems.

title: From Admission to Invariants: Measuring Deviation in Delegated Agent Systems
summary: Identifies a structural limitation in enforcement-based agent governance where local action-level checks fail to detect global behavioral drift from admission-time expectations, and proposes an Invariant Measurement Layer (IML) to restore observability of such drift.
rationale: This paper makes a foundational contribution to agent observation and evaluation by identifying a systemic blind spot in current enforcement mechanisms and proposing a novel solution with theoretical and empirical validation.

title: From Agent Loops to Deterministic Graphs: Execution Lineage for Reproducible AI-Native Work
summary: Introduces execution lineage, a DAG-based execution model for AI-native workflows, to provide explicit dependencies, stable intermediate boundaries, and identity-based replay for reproducible and maintainable AI-generated work.
rationale: Proposes a novel architectural model for agentic systems that directly addresses reproducibility and maintainability of AI-generated work products, a core systems-engineering concern.

title: From Agent Traces to Trust: A Survey of Evidence Tracing and Execution Provenance in LLM Agents
summary: A survey defining execution provenance and evidence tracing as foundational concepts for process-level accountability in LLM agents, connecting various aspects of trustworthiness within a unified framework.
rationale: This paper provides a foundational taxonomy and survey of evidence tracing and execution provenance, directly addressing the observability and accountability of agentic systems.

title: From Control Boundary to Insurance Claim: Reconstructing AI-Mediated Losses Through the CER Framework
summary: Introduces the CER framework (Control Boundary, Evidence Reconstruction, Insurance Response) to define and operationalize the reconstruction of AI-mediated losses for insurance claims, addressing the need for state reconstruction in agentic systems.
rationale: This paper provides a foundational framework for understanding and reconstructing AI-mediated losses in agentic systems, directly addressing the 'foundations' layer and the need for robust observability for accountability.

title: From CRUD to Autonomous Agents: Formal Validation and Zero-Trust Security for Semantic Gateways in AI-Native Enterprise Systems
summary: Proposes a Semantic Gateway and Model Context Protocol (MCP) for AI-native enterprise systems, reframing API access as a semantic surface with dynamic tool discovery, authorization, and execution based on intent and policy enforcement, and introduces a three-layer Zero-Trust security model with formal validation and fuzzing techniques.
rationale: Directly addresses security and validation challenges at the tool-interface layer for agentic systems, proposing a novel architectural pattern and formal verification methods.

title: From Failed Trajectories to Reliable LLM Agents: Diagnosing and Repairing Harness Flaws
summary: HarnessFix is a trace-guided framework that diagnoses agent failures by compiling execution traces and harness code into an intermediate representation, attributing failures to specific harness layers, and generating scoped repair patches to improve reliability.
rationale: The paper directly addresses diagnosing and repairing flaws in agent harnesses, contributing to the reliability of agentic systems through trace-guided analysis and automated repair.

title: From Model Scaling to System Scaling: Scaling the Harness in Agentic AI
summary: This paper frames agentic AI progress as a system-scaling problem, emphasizing the 'agent harness' (execution layer) and outlining research bottlenecks and benchmarks for context governance, trustworthy memory, and dynamic skill routing.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the foundational framing of trustworthy agentic systems as a systems-engineering problem, not just a model-scaling problem, and proposes a research agenda for harness-level evaluation.

title: From Prompt Injection to Persistent Control: Defending Agentic Harness Against Trojan Backdoors
summary: Introduces ClawTrojan, a benchmark for multi-step trojan attacks in local agentic harnesses that achieve persistent control, and proposes DASGuard, a defense that traces and sanitizes control-like text from untrusted sources in sensitive files.
rationale: Directly addresses multi-step, persistent control attacks on agentic systems, a critical red-teaming and security problem, with a benchmark and a concrete defense mechanism.

title: From Secure Agentic AI to Secure Agentic Web: Challenges, Threats, and Future Directions
summary: A survey providing a comprehensive threat taxonomy and defense review for agentic AI systems, extending to the 'Agentic Web' with discussions on escalated risks and open challenges for web-scale deployment.
rationale: This paper provides a foundational taxonomy and survey of security challenges and defenses for agentic systems, directly addressing the 'agent-as-system' thesis.

title: From Shield to Target: Denial-of-Service Attacks on LLM-Based Agent Guardrails
summary: Demonstrates a denial-of-service attack on LLM-based agent guardrails by trapping them in extended reasoning loops, leading to significant token and latency amplification in both standalone and end-to-end agent deployments.
rationale: This paper identifies a novel system-level DoS vulnerability in agent guardrails, providing a systematic attack framework and evaluating its impact on real-world agent deployments.

title: From Storage to Steering: Memory Control Flow Attacks on LLM Agents
summary: Identifies and quantifies Memory Control Flow Attacks (MCFA) where persistent memory entries can force unintended tool usage and behavioral deviations in LLM agents, and proposes MEMFLOW, an automated evaluation framework to assess this vulnerability.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a novel security vulnerability in agent memory that impacts control flow and tool usage, providing an evaluation framework and empirical results on real-world agent systems.

title: From Tool Orchestration to Code Execution: A Study of MCP Design Choices
summary: Formalizes and empirically evaluates Code Execution Model Context Protocols (CE-MCPs) for agent tool orchestration, identifying scalability benefits and a vastly expanded attack surface, then proposes a layered defense architecture.
rationale: Directly addresses the design choices, scalability, and security implications of tool interfaces and execution environments for agentic systems, including specific attack classes and defense mechanisms.

title: From Untrusted Input to Trusted Memory: A Systematic Study of Memory Poisoning Attacks in LLM Agents
summary: Systematic study of memory poisoning attacks in LLM agents, identifying write channels, structural vulnerabilities, and a taxonomy of attacks, along with a benchmark to evaluate their exploitability.
rationale: Directly addresses memory integrity and security in agentic systems by characterizing attacks and their underlying vulnerabilities.

title: Ghost in the Agent: Redefining Information Flow Tracking for LLM Agents
summary: NeuroTaint is a novel information flow tracking framework for LLM agents that redefines taint propagation to include semantic transformation, causal influence, and cross-session persistence, enabling more effective detection of security threats from untrusted external data.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a critical security problem in agentic systems by proposing a new information flow tracking framework tailored for LLM agents, with extensive evaluation on real-world frameworks and benchmarks.

title: GitInject: Real-World Prompt Injection Attacks in AI-Powered CI/CD Pipelines
summary: GitInject is an open-source framework for evaluating prompt injection vulnerabilities in real GitHub CI/CD workflows, demonstrating eleven attack classes and identifying structural vulnerabilities and workflow-level countermeasures.
rationale: This paper directly addresses prompt injection attacks in a real-world agentic system (CI/CD pipelines) with a concrete evaluation framework and identified system-level vulnerabilities and countermeasures.

title: Give Them an Inch and They Will Take a Mile:Understanding and Measuring Caller Identity Confusion in MCP-Based AI Systems
summary: Identifies and characterizes caller identity confusion vulnerabilities in Model Context Protocol (MCP) based AI systems, where MCP servers fail to authenticate callers or enforce fine-grained, per-tool authorization, leading to expanded attack surfaces.
rationale: Directly addresses security vulnerabilities in tool interfaces (MCP) by analyzing caller identity confusion and proposing explicit authentication and fine-grained authorization as solutions.

title: Governance Architecture for Autonomous Agent Systems: Threats, Framework, and Engineering Practice
summary: Proposes a four-layer governance architecture (LGA) for autonomous agents, including sandboxing, intent verification, zero-trust authorization, and immutable audit logging, and evaluates its effectiveness against various execution-layer vulnerabilities.
rationale: This paper proposes a comprehensive architectural framework for agent governance, addressing multiple systems-level trust concerns with experimental validation.

title: Governance by Construction for Generalist Agents
summary: CUGA's policy-as-code system provides a modular governance layer for generalist LLM agents, enforcing policies at five structural checkpoints across the execution pipeline to ensure predictable, auditable, and compliance-aware behavior.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the systems-engineering challenge of governance and oversight for enterprise agents through a concrete, multi-stage policy enforcement architecture.

title: Governed Memory: A Production Architecture for Multi-Agent Workflows
summary: Governed Memory is a production architecture for multi-agent workflows that provides shared, governed memory through a dual memory model, tiered governance routing, reflection-bounded retrieval, and a closed-loop schema lifecycle, validated by controlled experiments and production deployment.
rationale: This paper presents a production architecture addressing critical systems-level challenges in multi-agent memory governance, with concrete mechanisms and empirical validation.

title: Governed Shared Memory for Multi-Agent LLM Systems
summary: Formalizes the fleet-memory problem for multi-agent LLM systems, identifies failure modes, and proposes systems-level primitives for governed shared memory, implemented and evaluated in a production service.
rationale: Directly addresses memory integrity, provenance, and access control in multi-agent systems with a production-grade implementation and evaluation.

title: Governing Actions, Not Agents: Institutional Attestation as a Governance Model for Autonomous AI Systems
summary: Proposes a computational governance model for autonomous AI systems where high-risk actions require independent attestation from authoritative sources, cryptographically bound to intent, and evaluated by a deterministic policy, with decisions recorded in a tamper-evident log.
rationale: Directly addresses human oversight and governance for consequential agent actions by introducing an institutional attestation model at the point of execution.

title: Governing Dynamic Capabilities: Cryptographic Binding and Reproducibility Verification for AI Agent Tool Use
summary: Proposes a cryptographic framework for agent governance that ensures capability integrity, behavioral verifiability, and interaction auditability by distinguishing tool definitions from user context at the orchestration layer, with two instantiations and an evaluation of overhead and attack detection.
rationale: Directly addresses fundamental security and reliability problems in agentic systems by proposing a cryptographic framework for governing dynamic capabilities and verifying agent actions.

title: Grimlock: Guarding High-Agency Systems with eBPF and Attested Channels
summary: Grimlock is an Agent Guard that uses eBPF and attested TLS channels to enforce identity, authorization, provenance, and least-privilege delegation for agent-to-agent communication across multi-cloud environments.
rationale: This paper directly addresses security and least-privilege authorization for agentic systems at the communication and execution-shell layers, using concrete systems-level mechanisms.

title: Harness-Bench: Measuring Harness Effects across Models in Realistic Agent Workflows
summary: Harness-Bench is a diagnostic benchmark for evaluating the impact of different harness configurations on agent performance, process quality, and failure behavior in realistic, sandboxed agent workflows.
rationale: This paper introduces a benchmark specifically designed to measure and diagnose the effects of the agentic system's harness layer on trustworthiness, directly addressing a core problem in agent evaluation.

title: HARP: Measuring Harm Amplification in Multi-Agent LLM Systems
summary: Introduces HARP, a trace-first methodology for measuring harm amplification in multi-agent LLM systems by comparing paired clean and perturbed executions across various attack vectors and evaluating local-to-global harm propagation.
rationale: Directly addresses adversarial testing and evaluation of multi-agent systems, focusing on the system-level propagation of harm beyond local attack points.

title: HDP: A Lightweight Cryptographic Protocol for Human Delegation Provenance in Agentic AI Systems
summary: HDP is a lightweight cryptographic protocol that captures and verifies human authorization context and multi-hop delegation chains in agentic systems, enabling offline provenance verification of actions.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a core security and accountability problem in agentic systems by proposing a cryptographic protocol for human delegation provenance and authorization.

title: Heartbeat-Bound Hierarchical Credentials: Cryptographic Revocation for AI Agent Swarms
summary: Heartbeat-Bound Hierarchical Credentials (HBHC) is a cryptographic protocol that binds agent credential validity to periodic parent liveness proofs, enabling rapid, network-independent revocation of sub-agent swarms to prevent zombie agents from executing privileged operations.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a critical security and safety problem in multi-agent systems by providing a novel, cryptographically enforced revocation mechanism for agent credentials, with strong experimental validation.

title: Hidden in Memory: Sleeper Memory Poisoning in LLM Agents
summary: Characterizes 'sleeper memory poisoning' as a delayed attack where adversarial external content causes an LLM agent to store fabricated memories, influencing future interactions, and evaluates its effectiveness across different models.
rationale: Directly addresses a novel attack vector on agent memory integrity and its long-term impact on agent behavior, with empirical validation.

title: How Reliable Is Your Jailbreak Judge? Calibration and Adversarial Robustness of Automated ASR Scoring
summary: Evaluates the calibration and adversarial robustness of automated jailbreak judges (safety classifiers and LLM-as-judges) against human labels, revealing significant unreliability and vulnerability to surface and white-box attacks, and proposes reporting and adversarial checking recommendations for ASR.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the reliability and security of evaluation infrastructure for agentic systems, specifically the automated judges used to score jailbreaks and prompt injections.

title: How Vulnerable Are AI Agents to Indirect Prompt Injections? Insights from a Large-Scale Public Competition
summary: Large-scale red teaming competition evaluating indirect prompt injection attacks against LLM agents across tool calling, coding, and computer use, focusing on concealed attacks that manipulate agent behavior without user awareness.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a critical security and safety concern for agentic systems through a large-scale red-teaming effort, identifying universal attack strategies and providing a competition environment for further evaluation.

title: "I Strongly Suspect This Website Is a Scam": Benchmarking PII Leakage and Detection without Defense in Autonomous Web Agents
summary: Introduces Scammer4U, a benchmark of 91 attacker-controlled web environments to quantify PII leakage from autonomous web agents under social-engineering attacks, revealing a detection-action gap where agents submit PII despite recognizing suspicious activity.
rationale: This paper directly addresses adversarial testing of agentic systems for PII leakage, identifying a critical systems-level vulnerability and motivating output-level interception as a defense.

title: ILION: Deterministic Pre-Execution Safety Gates for Agentic AI Systems
summary: ILION is a deterministic, cascade-architecture execution gate that classifies proposed agent actions as BLOCK or ALLOW, addressing the architectural mismatch of text-safety systems for agent action safety.
rationale: The paper directly addresses a critical systems-engineering problem of agent action safety and authorization, proposing a novel, deterministic execution gate architecture and evaluating it on a purpose-built benchmark.

title: Indirect Prompt Injection in the Wild: An Empirical Study of Prevalence, Techniques, and Objectives
summary: Large-scale empirical analysis of indirect prompt injections in web content, characterizing their prevalence, techniques, and objectives, and evaluating compliance across models and webpage representations.
rationale: This paper provides a large-scale empirical study of a critical red-teaming vector (indirect prompt injection) for agentic systems that interact with untrusted web content.

title: Inference-Time Backdoors via Chat Templates: From LLM Supply Chains to Agentic System Compromise
summary: Identifies chat templates as a novel and undefended attack surface for inference-time backdoors, demonstrating compromise across LLM, agent, and multi-agent system levels, and showing evasion of existing defenses.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a critical supply-chain security vulnerability in agentic systems by identifying and demonstrating a novel attack vector (chat templates) that bypasses existing defenses.

title: IPI-proxy: An Intercepting Proxy for Red-Teaming Web-Browsing AI Agents Against Indirect Prompt Injection
summary: IPI-proxy is an open-source intercepting proxy toolkit for red-teaming web-browsing AI agents against indirect prompt injection by rewriting real HTTP responses with adversarial payloads from a unified library.
rationale: This paper directly contributes to red-teaming agentic systems by providing a practical tool for evaluating web-browsing agents against indirect prompt injection in a realistic setting.

title: IterInject: Indirect Prompt Injection Against LLM Agents via Feedback-Guided Iterative Optimization
summary: IterInject is a feedback-guided iterative framework for indirect prompt injection against LLM agents, which optimizes adversarial payloads based on structured diagnosis and self-evolving disguise seeds, demonstrating effectiveness against various agent frameworks and a production coding agent.
rationale: This paper directly contributes to red-teaming agentic systems by developing an advanced, adaptive indirect prompt injection attack framework and analyzing its mechanisms.

title: Kill-Chain Canaries: Stage-Level Tracking of Prompt Injection Across Attack Surfaces and Model Safety Tiers
summary: Introduces a kill-chain canary methodology to track prompt injection propagation through four stages (EXPOSED, PERSISTED, RELAYED, EXECUTED) across various LLMs, attack surfaces, and defense conditions, reframing prompt injection as a pipeline-architecture problem.
rationale: Directly addresses adversarial testing of agentic systems by providing a detailed, multi-stage diagnostic methodology for prompt injection across the operational stack.

title: LITMUS: Benchmarking Behavioral Jailbreaks of LLM Agents in Real OS Environments
