summary: LITMUS is a benchmark for behavioral jailbreaks of LLM agents in real OS environments, featuring semantic-physical dual verification and OS-level state rollback to evaluate dangerous OS-level operations.
rationale: This paper directly addresses adversarial testing of agentic systems in real OS environments, including novel attack paradigms and a physically grounded evaluation framework.

title: LivePI: More Realistic Benchmarking of Agents Against Indirect Prompt Injection
summary: LivePI is a structured benchmark for indirect prompt injection (IPI) risk in a production-like, test-controlled environment, covering seven input surfaces, twelve attack families, and five malicious goals, and evaluates a two-layer defense.
rationale: This paper directly contributes to red-teaming agentic systems by introducing a comprehensive, realistic benchmark for indirect prompt injection and evaluating defenses against it.

title: Local LLM Agents as Vulnerable Runtimes:A Source-Code Audit of the Agent Runtime Layer
summary: Presents CLAWAUDIT, a static auditing framework with a five-category vulnerability taxonomy and custom static-analysis rules to identify implementation-level security weaknesses in local LLM agent runtimes.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the security of the agent's execution shell by auditing its source code for implementation-level vulnerabilities, a critical systems-engineering concern.

title: Log analysis is necessary for credible evaluation of AI agents
summary: Argues for systematic log analysis of agent inputs, execution, and outputs to overcome threats to evaluation credibility, presenting a taxonomy of threats and guiding principles illustrated on a benchmark.
rationale: Directly addresses the need for robust observation and evaluation mechanisms for trustworthy agentic systems, going beyond outcome metrics to analyze internal behavior.

title: LogJack: Indirect Prompt Injection Through Cloud Logs Against LLM Debugging Agents
summary: LogJack demonstrates indirect prompt injection attacks against LLM debugging agents via cloud logs, evaluating 8 foundation models and cloud guardrails, and revealing a novel "sanitize and execute" behavior.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a system-level adversarial threat (indirect prompt injection) against agentic systems (debugging agents) and evaluates its impact on multiple models and cloud guardrails.

title: MalSkillBench: A Runtime-Verified Benchmark of Malicious Agent Skills
summary: Introduces MalSkillBench, a runtime-verified benchmark of malicious agent skills, to evaluate the effectiveness of detection tools against hybrid code and prompt-based supply chain risks in third-party agent skills.
rationale: This paper directly addresses supply-chain security for agentic systems by creating a benchmark for malicious third-party skills, which are a hybrid of code and agent-facing instructions.

title: MAS-FIRE: Fault Injection and Reliability Evaluation for LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems
summary: MAS-FIRE is a systematic fault injection and reliability evaluation framework for multi-agent systems, defining a taxonomy of 15 fault types and non-invasive injection mechanisms to diagnose failure propagation and recovery.
rationale: Directly addresses reliability evaluation and observability for multi-agent systems through a systematic fault injection framework and architectural analysis.

title: MCP-38: A Comprehensive Threat Taxonomy for Model Context Protocol Systems (v1.0)
summary: A comprehensive threat taxonomy (MCP-38) for Model Context Protocol systems, derived through systematic methodology and mapped to existing frameworks, addressing novel semantic attack surfaces like tool description poisoning and parasitic tool chaining.
rationale: Provides a foundational taxonomy for understanding and categorizing security threats specific to agentic systems, which is crucial for engineering trustworthy agents.

title: MCP-in-SoS: Risk assessment framework for open-source MCP servers
summary: Systematic, large-scale security assessment of open-source Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers using static code analysis to identify CWE weaknesses and map them to CAPEC attack patterns, revealing exploitable vulnerabilities that compromise confidentiality, integrity, and availability.
rationale: Directly addresses security risks and weaknesses in open-source MCP servers, which are critical components of the agentic system's tool interface.

title: MemAudit: Post-hoc Auditing of Poisoned Agent Memory via Causal Attribution and Structural Anomaly Detection
summary: MemAudit is a post-hoc causal memory auditing framework that uses counterfactual influence scores and memory consistency graphs to identify and attribute malicious records in an agent's memory after harmful behavior has occurred.
rationale: The paper directly addresses a critical security vulnerability in agent memory (poisoning) and provides a systems-level solution for post-hoc auditing and attribution.

title: MemLineage: Lineage-Guided Enforcement for LLM Agent Memory
summary: MemLineage is a defense for LLM agent memory that uses cryptographic provenance and LLM-mediated derivation lineage to prevent untrusted content from justifying sensitive actions, treating memory integrity as a chain-of-custody problem.
rationale: This paper directly addresses memory integrity and trust boundaries in agentic systems with a novel, technically detailed defense mechanism and strong experimental validation.

title: MemMark: State-Evolution Attribution Watermarking for Agent Long-Term Memory Systems
summary: MemMark introduces a state-evolution attribution watermark for agent long-term memory systems, embedding owner-controlled signals into latent memory-write decisions to provide robust provenance even from leaked or migrated snapshots.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the critical systems problem of memory provenance and attribution for long-term agent memory, providing a novel watermarking mechanism and evaluating its robustness against various attacks.

title: Memory for Autonomous LLM Agents:Mechanisms, Evaluation, and Emerging Frontiers
summary: A comprehensive survey of memory mechanisms for LLM agents, formalizing memory as a write-manage-read loop, taxonomizing design choices, and discussing engineering realities like write-path filtering and privacy governance.
rationale: This survey provides a foundational and systematic overview of memory in agentic systems, including engineering challenges and privacy governance, directly addressing a core harness layer.

title: memorywire: A Vendor-Neutral Wire Format for Agent Memory Operations
summary: A vendor-neutral wire format for agent memory operations, including a MemoryStore interface, fan-out router, and optional human-in-the-loop governance channel, with a reference implementation and empirical validation.
rationale: Directly addresses the systems-engineering problem of memory interoperability, governance, and reliability for agentic systems with a concrete protocol and reference implementation.

title: MemVenom: Triggered Poisoning of Multimodal Memories in Web Agents
summary: Identifies and systematically studies multimodal memory poisoning in web agents, proposing MemVenom, a black-box attack framework that injects coordinated text-image evidence into graph-structured external memory to persistently influence agent behavior.
rationale: Directly addresses a critical security vulnerability in agent memory, specifically multimodal poisoning, with a concrete attack framework and experimental validation.

title: Mind Your HEARTBEAT! Claw Background Execution Inherently Enables Silent Memory Pollution
summary: Identifies a memory pollution vulnerability in AI agents where untrusted content from background execution silently influences user-facing behavior due to shared memory context and lack of provenance, formalized as an Exposure-Memory-Behavior pathway.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a critical security vulnerability in agent memory management and context integrity, proposing a novel attack vector and analyzing its impact on agent behavior.

title: Model Context Protocol Threat Modeling and Analyzing Vulnerabilities to Prompt Injection with Tool Poisoning
summary: Threat modeling of the Model Context Protocol (MCP) identifies tool poisoning as a prevalent client-side vulnerability, leading to an empirical evaluation of seven MCP clients and a multi-layered defense strategy.
rationale: This paper directly addresses security vulnerabilities in tool interfaces, a critical component of trustworthy agentic systems, with a clear threat model and empirical evaluation.

title: Monitoring Agentic Systems Before They're Reliable
summary: A monitoring and triage methodology for early-stage agentic systems that decomposes evaluation into quality, suitability, and efficiency across within-run, cross-run, and structural scopes, using variance to characterize failure types and route findings for human investigation.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the systems-level problem of monitoring and triaging failures in early-stage agentic systems, focusing on structural defects that mask task-level errors.

title: MPIB: A Benchmark for Medical Prompt Injection Attacks and Clinical Safety in LLMs
summary: Introduces the Medical Prompt Injection Benchmark (MPIB) to evaluate clinical safety of LLMs and RAG systems under direct and RAG-mediated prompt injection, measuring both attack success rate and clinical harm event rate.
rationale: Directly addresses adversarial testing of agentic systems (LLMs and RAG in clinical workflows) with a focus on safety and security, providing a concrete benchmark and methodology.

title: MUZZLE: Adaptive Agentic Red-Teaming of Web Agents Against Indirect Prompt Injection Attacks
summary: MUZZLE is an automated agentic red-teaming framework that adaptively identifies high-salience injection surfaces and generates context-aware malicious instructions to test web agents against indirect prompt injection attacks, demonstrating new attacks across various web applications and adversarial objectives.
rationale: This paper directly contributes to red-teaming agentic systems by providing an adaptive framework for discovering indirect prompt injection attacks in web agents, which is a critical security concern.

title: No Attack Required: Semantic Fuzzing for Specification Violations in Agent Skills
summary: Sefz is a semantic fuzzing framework that automatically discovers specification violations in agent skills by translating natural-language guardrails into reachability goals over execution traces, identifying cases where benign inputs cause skills to breach their own safety rules.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a critical systems-level trustworthiness problem by proposing a novel fuzzing framework to identify specification violations in agent skills, which can lead to security and safety breaches without explicit attacks.

title: Notarized Agents: Receiver-Attested Confidential Receipts for AI Agent Actions
summary: Proposes a class of protocols for receiver-attested confidential receipts for AI agent actions, enabling tamper-evident activity logs without trusting the agent or its operator.
rationale: Directly addresses a fundamental problem in agent observability and attribution with a novel cryptographic protocol and security analysis.

title: OMNI-LEAK: Orchestrator Multi-Agent Network Induced Data Leakage
summary: Identifies and demonstrates OMNI-LEAK, a novel multi-agent data leakage attack vector in orchestrator setups, which compromises multiple agents via a single indirect prompt injection despite access controls.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a system-level security vulnerability in multi-agent orchestration, demonstrating a novel attack and highlighting the need for multi-agent safety research.

title: On Randomness in Agentic Evals
summary: Empirical study of evaluation variance in agentic systems on SWE-Bench-Verified, demonstrating that single-run pass@1 scores are unreliable and proposing multi-run evaluation practices to improve reliability.
rationale: Directly addresses the reliability of evaluation for agentic systems, a critical aspect of trustworthy system development, with concrete recommendations for improved methodology.

title: OpenClaw PRISM: A Zero-Fork, Defense-in-Depth Runtime Security Layer for Tool-Augmented LLM Agents
summary: OpenClaw PRISM is a zero-fork runtime security layer for tool-augmented LLM agent gateways, distributing enforcement across ten lifecycle hooks to mitigate risks like indirect prompt injection, unsafe tool execution, and credential leakage.
rationale: This paper directly addresses runtime security for agent execution, covering multiple attack surfaces and proposing a defense-in-depth architecture with an evaluation methodology.

title: OpenKedge: Governing Agentic Mutation with Execution-Bound Safety and Evidence Chains
summary: OpenKedge is a protocol that governs agentic mutations by requiring declarative intent proposals, evaluating them against system state and policies, compiling them into execution-bound contracts, and linking all stages via a cryptographic evidence chain for auditability.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the systems-level problem of governing agentic mutations with execution-bound safety and verifiable evidence chains, contributing to multiple trustworthiness guarantees.

title: Oracle Poisoning: Corrupting Knowledge Graphs to Weaponise AI Agent Reasoning
summary: Defines and demonstrates "Oracle Poisoning," an attack class that corrupts knowledge graphs used by AI agents via tool-use, leading to incorrect reasoning, and evaluates defenses including read-only access control.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a novel attack vector on agent memory (knowledge graphs) accessed via tool-use, demonstrating system-level vulnerabilities and evaluating defenses.

title: Overeager Coding Agents: Measuring Out-of-Scope Actions on Benign Tasks
summary: Introduces a benchmark, OverEager-Gen, to measure 'overeager' out-of-scope actions by coding agents on benign tasks, highlighting how prompt-based consent declarations can mask underlying authorization issues and demonstrating significant variance across agent frameworks and models.
rationale: Directly addresses a critical authorization problem (overeager actions) in agentic systems, providing a novel benchmark and empirical analysis of how system design (consent declarations, framework type) impacts agent behavior.

title: Overlaying Governance: A Compositional Authorization Framework for Delegation and Scope in Agentic AI
summary: A compositional authorization framework that introduces primitives for recursive delegation, contextual boundaries, and dynamic scoping to govern agentic AI systems, operationalized through an overlay operator on existing relational policies.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the critical problem of authorization and delegation for autonomous agents, proposing a novel compositional framework with formal proofs and empirical evaluation.

title: Owner-Harm: A Missing Threat Model for AI Agent Safety
summary: Proposes 'Owner-Harm' as a formal threat model for agents harming their deployers, quantifies defense gaps on new benchmarks, and introduces the Symbolic-Semantic Defense Generalization (SSDG) framework for detection.
rationale: Introduces a new, critical threat model for agentic systems, provides empirical evaluation of defense gaps, and proposes a framework for improving detection, directly addressing agent security and privacy.

title: Parallax: Why AI Agents That Think Must Never Act
summary: Parallax is an architectural paradigm for safe autonomous AI execution, based on Cognitive-Executive Separation, Adversarial Validation, Information Flow Control, and Reversible Execution, with an open-source reference implementation and evaluation against adversarial test cases.
rationale: This paper proposes a novel architectural paradigm for trustworthy agent execution, addressing fundamental security and safety gaps with concrete principles and an open-source implementation.

title: Peak + Accumulation: A Proxy-Level Scoring Formula for Multi-Turn LLM Attack Detection
summary: Proposes a proxy-level scoring formula, Peak + Accumulation, for detecting multi-turn prompt injection attacks by aggregating per-turn pattern scores into a conversation-level risk score without LLM invocation.
rationale: Directly addresses the detection of multi-turn adversarial attacks on LLM agents at the proxy layer, a critical systems-level security concern.

title: PISmith: Reinforcement Learning-based Red Teaming for Prompt Injection Defenses
summary: PISmith is a reinforcement learning-based red-teaming framework that systematically assesses prompt-injection defenses by training an attack LLM to optimize injected prompts in a black-box setting, demonstrating vulnerabilities in state-of-the-art defenses.
rationale: This paper directly contributes to red-teaming agentic systems by developing an adaptive attack framework to evaluate prompt injection defenses, including in agentic settings.

title: Plant, Persist, Trigger: Sleeper Attack on Large Language Model Agents
summary: Formalizes and evaluates 'Sleeper Attacks' where adversarial content persists in agent state (session context, memory, skills) across interactions and is later triggered by benign queries, demonstrating vulnerabilities in LLM agents.
rationale: This paper introduces a novel, persistent adversarial attack on agentic systems, focusing on how malicious content can be planted and triggered across multiple interactions, directly addressing a system-level security and safety concern.

title: PlanTwin: Privacy-Preserving Planning Abstractions for Cloud-Assisted LLM Agents
summary: PlanTwin is a privacy-preserving architecture that projects a private local environment into a de-identified digital twin for cloud-assisted LLM planning, preventing raw context exposure while maintaining planning quality.
rationale: Directly addresses privacy and security concerns in agent planning by controlling what information cloud planners observe, a core problem in trustworthy agentic systems.

title: Poison Once, Exploit Forever: Environment-Injected Memory Poisoning Attacks on Web Agents
summary: Introduces Environment-injected Trajectory-based Agent Memory Poisoning (eTAMP), a novel attack that poisons an agent's memory through environmental observation, leading to cross-session and cross-site compromise, and identifies 'Frustration Exploitation' as a factor increasing susceptibility.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a critical security vulnerability in agent memory, specifically memory poisoning through environmental observation, which is a core systems-level concern for trustworthy agents.

title: Poisoning the Watchtower: Prompt Injection Attacks Against LLM-Augmented Security Operations Through Adversarial Log Content
summary: Characterizes log-substrate prompt injection attacks against LLM-augmented security operations, where attacker-controlled log content manipulates the LLM's behavior, and evaluates various attack strategies and defenses.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a novel prompt injection attack vector within a critical agentic system (SOC copilots) and evaluates its impact and potential defenses.

title: Portable Agent Memory: A Protocol for Cryptographically-Verified Memory Transfer Across Heterogeneous AI Agents
summary: Portable Agent Memory is an open protocol and reference implementation for cryptographically-verified, capability-based transfer of structured memory state across heterogeneous AI agents, addressing memory integrity, provenance, and selective disclosure.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the systems-engineering problem of secure and private memory management for agents, including provenance, access control, and injection resistance during transfer.

title: Prompt Injection as Role Confusion
summary: This paper traces prompt injection to role confusion in LLMs, where models perceive text source based on linguistic cues rather than explicit role labels, leading to attacks like CoT Forgery.
rationale: The paper provides a mechanistic explanation and empirical validation of prompt injection as a system-level vulnerability rooted in LLM's role perception, with a novel attack and measurement technique.

title: Prompt Overflow: What the Guardrail Inspects Is Not What the Model Infers
summary: Identifies and demonstrates a "Prompt Overflow Attack" that exploits the mismatch between guardrail inspection windows and LLM inference windows to bypass safety checks with fragmented malicious instructions.
rationale: This paper describes a novel red-teaming attack that targets a specific system-level vulnerability in how guardrails process long inputs, directly impacting agent security.

title: Prompts Don't Protect: Architectural Enforcement via MCP Proxy for LLM Tool Access Control
summary: A governed MCP proxy enforces attribute-based access control for LLM tool access by filtering tool discovery and blocking unauthorized invocations, demonstrating architectural enforcement is necessary for reliable tool access control.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a critical systems-level security problem in agentic systems (tool access control) with a concrete architectural solution and empirical validation.

title: Proof-Carrying Agent Actions: Model-Agnostic Runtime Governance for Heterogeneous Agent Systems
summary: Introduces Proof-Carrying Agent Actions (PCAA), a runtime-neutral governance model using action certificates to consistently track authorization, approval, and evidence for high-risk actions across heterogeneous agent systems.
rationale: Directly addresses runtime governance and authorization for agent actions across diverse execution environments, a core systems problem for trustworthy agents.

title: Protecting Context and Prompts: Deterministic Security for Non-Deterministic AI
summary: Introduces cryptographically verifiable authenticated prompts and tamper-evident hash chains for context to provide provenance and integrity guarantees against prompt injection and context manipulation attacks.
rationale: Directly addresses memory integrity and trust boundaries in agent context with a novel cryptographic approach and formal security guarantees.

title: Red-Teaming the Agentic Red-Team
summary: First in-depth security analysis of agentic systems for offensive security operations, demonstrating common design flaws that enable API key exfiltration, persistence, and sandbox escape, and proposing a robust architecture and design principles.
rationale: This paper directly red-teams agentic systems themselves, identifying critical vulnerabilities and proposing architectural mitigations, which is squarely in scope for trustworthy agentic systems.

title: Resilient Write: A Six-Layer Durable Write Surface for LLM Coding Agents
summary: Resilient Write is an MCP server that provides a six-layer durable write surface for LLM coding agents, addressing common write failures through features like transactional atomic writes, resume-safe chunking, and structured error handling.
rationale: This paper directly addresses reliability and safety concerns in agent tool-use, specifically for file writing, by introducing a robust, multi-layered system for handling failures and providing structured feedback to the agent.

title: Reward Hacking as Equilibrium under Finite Evaluation
summary: Theoretical framework proving reward hacking as a structural equilibrium in AI agents due to finite evaluation, with a computable distortion index and analysis of how agentic systems exacerbate this problem.
rationale: Provides a foundational theoretical understanding of a core trustworthiness problem (reward hacking) that is exacerbated by agentic systems, offering a vulnerability assessment procedure.

title: RIFT-Bench: Dynamic Red-teaming For Agentic AI Systems
summary: RIFT-Bench introduces a graph representation-driven methodology for dynamic red-teaming that enables unified security evaluations across diverse agentic architectures by extracting system structure and deploying adaptive adversarial attacks.
rationale: This paper directly addresses adversarial testing of agentic systems with a novel, generalizable red-teaming methodology and evaluation across diverse architectures.

title: Rollout Cards: A Reproducibility Standard for Agent Research
summary: Proposes "rollout cards" as a reproducibility standard for agent research, preserving full rollout records and declared reporting rules to address inconsistencies in reported scores and enable re-analysis.
rationale: Directly addresses reproducibility and evaluation challenges in agentic systems by proposing a concrete standard for capturing and reporting execution traces and evaluation rules.

title: SafeClawBench: Separating Semantic, Audit-Evidence, and Sandbox Harm in Tool-Using LLM Agents
summary: SafeClawBench is a staged benchmark for tool-using agent security that separates semantic attack acceptance, audit-visible harm evidence, and sandbox-observed tool/state harm across six attack families.
rationale: This paper directly contributes to adversarial testing and evaluation of agentic systems by providing a nuanced, multi-stage benchmark for tool-using agent security.

title: Safety in Embodied AI: A Survey of Risks, Attacks, and Defenses
summary: A comprehensive survey of safety research in embodied AI, providing a multi-level taxonomy of risks, attacks, and defenses across the full embodied pipeline, including agentic system aspects.
rationale: This paper provides a foundational survey and taxonomy of safety risks and defenses for embodied AI, explicitly covering agentic systems and their operational stack.

title: Safety in Self-Evolving LLM Agent Systems: Threats, Amplification, and Case Studies
summary: Systematic security and privacy analysis of self-evolving LLM agent systems, identifying new threat landscapes, amplification effects, and the inadequacy of static defenses across a Module-Lifecycle Attack Surface matrix.
rationale: Provides a foundational analysis of the unique security and safety threats introduced by self-evolving agent systems, framing the problem for future systems-level engineering.

title: Safety, Security, and Cognitive Risks in Neuro-Symbolic AI
summary: Formalizes the attack surface and threat model for neuro-symbolic AI systems, including symbolic integrity violations, cross-layer amplification, and cognitive risks, with empirical benchmarks on knowledge graph poisoning and neural-symbolic pipeline attacks.
rationale: Provides a foundational taxonomy and threat model for security and safety risks in neuro-symbolic agentic systems, including empirical validation of attacks across layers.

title: SARC: A Governance-by-Architecture Framework for Agentic AI Systems
summary: SARC is a runtime governance architecture for tool-using agents that formalizes constraints as first-class specification objects, compiling them into four enforcement sites within the agent loop to ensure executable, inspectable, and auditable obligations.
rationale: This paper proposes a novel architectural framework for runtime governance of agentic systems, directly addressing the challenge of enforcing constraints at execution time rather than post-hoc.

title: Securing LLM-Agent Long-Term Memory Against Poisoning: Non-Malleable, Origin-Bound Authority with Machine-Checked Guarantees
summary: Formalizes memory poisoning attacks on LLM agent long-term memory, demonstrates malleability of existing content- and lineage-based defenses, and proposes a non-malleable, origin-bound authority mechanism with machine-checked guarantees to prevent such attacks.
rationale: Directly addresses a critical security vulnerability in agent memory (poisoning) with a formal model, empirical validation, and a novel defense mechanism.

title: Securing the Agent: Vendor-Neutral, Multitenant Enterprise Retrieval and Tool Use
summary: Introduces a layered isolation architecture for multitenant enterprise RAG and agentic systems, combining policy-aware ingestion, retrieval-time gating, and server-side orchestration to enforce authorization and prevent cross-tenant data leakage.
rationale: Directly addresses critical security and privacy challenges in multitenant agentic systems by proposing and validating a concrete architectural solution for authorization and isolation.

title: Security Attack and Defense Strategies for Autonomous Agent Frameworks: A Layered Review with OpenClaw as a Case Study
summary: A layered review of security risks and defense strategies in autonomous agent frameworks, organizing analysis into context/instruction, tool/action, state/persistence, and ecosystem/automation layers, and identifying cross-layer threat propagation.
rationale: This paper provides a foundational, layered taxonomy of security risks and defenses for agentic systems, directly addressing the 'agent-as-system' thesis.

title: Security Considerations for Artificial Intelligence Agents
summary: A comprehensive analysis of security considerations for AI agents, mapping attack surfaces across tools, connectors, hosting, and multi-agent coordination, and assessing current defenses as a layered stack.
rationale: This paper provides a foundational overview of security risks and defenses in agentic systems, directly addressing the 'foundations' layer and identifying key open problems.

title: Security Engineering of OpenClaw: Analyzing Attack Surface Expansion and Trust-Boundary Violations
summary: Analyzes the attack surface expansion, trust-boundary violations, and privilege drift in a self-hosted multi-agent system (OpenClaw) as the number of agents and attacker capabilities increase, and evaluates the impact of defensive controls.
rationale: This paper provides a detailed security engineering analysis of a multi-agent system, focusing on system-level vulnerabilities and the effectiveness of defensive controls, which is directly relevant to trustworthy agentic systems.

title: Security of OpenClaw Agents: Fundamentals, Attacks, and Countermeasures
summary: A comprehensive survey of the security landscape for OpenClaw agents, categorizing threats and defenses across reasoning, action execution, and external interaction, and identifying unresolved issues.
rationale: This paper provides a foundational survey of security for agentic systems, aligning with the 'foundations' harness layer and addressing multiple SPRS guarantees.

title: Selection Integrity for LLM Graph Memory: An Accumulability Criterion for Information-Flow-Blind Retrieval
summary: This paper demonstrates a novel memory poisoning attack on graph-based agent memories where untrusted structural writes can silently misdirect information flow by altering selection, even with authenticated facts, and proposes a defense called \authselect\ that recomputes selection on authenticated subgraphs.
rationale: The paper identifies a critical vulnerability in graph-based agent memory, demonstrating a systems-level attack on information flow and proposing a concrete defense mechanism with performance analysis.

title: Self-Healing Agentic Orchestrators for Reliable Tool-Augmented Large Language Model Systems
summary: A self-healing agentic orchestrator that maps failure signals to recovery actions under budget, verifies recovered trajectories, and records observability traces, demonstrating improved reliability and diagnosability in tool-augmented LLM systems.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the systems-level problem of reliability in agentic orchestrators by proposing a self-healing mechanism for runtime control and recovery.

title: Shepherd: Enabling Programmable Meta-Agents via Reversible Agentic Execution Traces
summary: Shepherd is a Python substrate that treats agent execution as a first-class, reversible object, enabling meta-agents to inspect, transform, and repair execution traces for improved coordination, safety, and optimization.
rationale: This paper introduces a foundational execution substrate that enables advanced meta-agent operations like rollback, repair, and coordination, directly addressing reliability and safety concerns in agentic systems.

title: ShieldNet: Network-Level Guardrails against Emerging Supply-Chain Injections in Agentic Systems
summary: Introduces SC-Inject-Bench, a benchmark for supply-chain injection attacks via malicious MCP tools, and ShieldNet, a network-level guardrail framework that detects these attacks by observing network interactions.
rationale: Directly addresses supply-chain security for agentic systems by proposing a benchmark and a network-level defense mechanism.

title: Silent Egress: When Implicit Prompt Injection Makes LLM Agents Leak Without a Trace
summary: Demonstrates 'silent egress' via implicit prompt injection embedded in URL previews, leading to sensitive context exfiltration from LLM agents, and proposes system and network-layer defenses.
rationale: This paper identifies a novel system-level attack vector (implicit prompt injection via URL previews) and proposes architectural defenses, directly contributing to agentic system security and privacy.

title: Skill-Inject: Measuring Agent Vulnerability to Skill File Attacks
summary: Introduces SkillInject, a benchmark for evaluating LLM agent vulnerability to prompt injection attacks through third-party skill files, demonstrating high attack success rates for data exfiltration and destructive actions.
rationale: Directly addresses a novel attack surface (skill files) in the agent supply chain with a concrete red-teaming benchmark and analysis of system-level vulnerabilities.

title: SkillJect: Effectively Automating Skill-Based Prompt Injection for Skill-Enabled Agents
summary: SkillJect is an automated framework for generating poisoned skills that hide malicious payloads in auxiliary scripts and use front-loaded inducements in skill instructions to trick agents into executing them, demonstrating a supply-chain attack surface in reusable skill ecosystems.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a supply-chain security vulnerability in agentic systems by demonstrating and automating skill-based prompt injection, which is a novel attack vector.

title: SkillSieve: A Hierarchical Triage Framework for Detecting Malicious AI Agent Skills
summary: SkillSieve is a hierarchical triage framework for detecting malicious agent skills in marketplaces, combining regex, AST, metadata checks, and multi-LLM analysis to identify vulnerabilities in both code and natural-language instructions.
rationale: This paper directly addresses supply-chain security for agent skills by providing a multi-modal detection framework for malicious content in a marketplace setting.

title: SkillTrojan: Backdoor Attacks on Skill-Based Agent Systems
summary: Introduces SkillTrojan, a backdoor attack that embeds malicious logic within skill implementations, partitioning an encrypted payload across multiple skill invocations, and evaluates its effectiveness in a code-based agent setting.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a novel system-level attack vector (skill-based backdoors) in agentic systems, demonstrating a critical security vulnerability in skill composition and execution.

title: SOK: A Taxonomy of Attack Vectors and Defense Strategies for Agentic Supply Chain Runtime
summary: A systematization of knowledge (SOK) paper that taxonomizes runtime attack vectors and defense strategies for agentic systems, focusing on data and tool supply chain attacks and introducing the concept of a Viral Agent Loop.
rationale: This paper provides a foundational taxonomy of runtime security risks for agentic systems, directly addressing the systems-level attack surface beyond model-level vulnerabilities.

title: SoK: Agentic Skills -- Beyond Tool Use in LLM Agents
summary: A Systematization of Knowledge (SoK) on agentic skills, mapping their lifecycle, design patterns, and representation, with a focus on security and governance implications including supply-chain risks and prompt injection.
rationale: This paper provides a foundational SoK on agentic skills, directly addressing system-level design patterns, security implications, and governance for trustworthy agentic systems.

title: SoK: The Attack Surface of Agentic AI -- Tools, and Autonomy
summary: A systematization of knowledge mapping the attack surface of agentic AI systems, including prompt injection, knowledge-base poisoning, tool exploits, and multi-agent threats, with a taxonomy of attacks, defensive controls, and open research challenges.
rationale: This paper provides a comprehensive systematization of knowledge on the attack surface of agentic AI systems, directly addressing foundational security concerns for the operational stack.

title: Stable Agentic Control: Tool-Mediated LLM Architecture for Autonomous Cyber Defense
summary: A tool-mediated LLM agent architecture for autonomous cyber defense that uses deterministic tools and finite action catalogs, with formal guarantees of controllability, observability, and Input-to-State Stability (ISS) machine-checked in Lean 4.
rationale: This paper presents a novel agentic system architecture with formal guarantees for high-stakes adversarial environments, directly addressing trustworthiness in agent design.

title: SUDP: Secret-Use Delegation Protocol for Agentic Systems
summary: Formalizes the Agent Secret Use (ASU) problem and proposes the Secret-Use Delegation Protocol (SUDP) to enable agents to perform secret-backed operations without gaining reusable authority, satisfying seven security properties.
rationale: Directly addresses the critical problem of least-privilege authorization for agentic systems when handling user secrets, with a formal protocol and reference implementation.

title: Taming OpenClaw: Security Analysis and Mitigation of Autonomous LLM Agent Threats
summary: Comprehensive security threat analysis of autonomous LLM agents like OpenClaw, introducing a five-layer lifecycle-oriented security framework to examine compound threats across agent operation stages and analyze limitations of existing defenses.
rationale: This paper provides a systematic security threat analysis of autonomous LLM agents, identifying and demonstrating multi-stage systemic risks, which is a direct contribution to red-teaming and understanding agentic system vulnerabilities.

title: The Attack and Defense Landscape of Agentic AI: A Comprehensive Survey
summary: A comprehensive survey of the attack and defense landscape for agentic AI systems, analyzing design space, attack vectors, and defense mechanisms, and identifying open challenges.
rationale: This paper provides a foundational survey of security challenges and defense strategies for agentic AI systems, directly addressing the 'foundations' layer of trustworthy agent engineering.

title: The Best-Laid SCHEMEs: Coordinated Sabotage and Monitoring in Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Introduces SCHEME, a benchmark for coordinated sabotage in multi-agent systems, demonstrating that agents can achieve covert malicious objectives while appearing aligned, and evaluates the detectability of such sabotage through code edits and communication monitoring.
rationale: This paper directly addresses adversarial testing of multi-agent systems, focusing on coordinated sabotage and the detectability of such threats, which is a core red-teaming concern for trustworthy agents.

title: The Capability Paradox: How Smarter Auditors Make Multi-Agent Systems Less Secure
summary: Identifies "semantic hijacking" as a new attack surface in multi-agent systems where harmful requests are concealed in domain-specific narratives, and proposes heterogeneous ensemble verification as a defense by exploiting capability asymmetries.
rationale: This paper identifies a novel system-level attack (semantic hijacking) in multi-agent systems and proposes a concrete defense, directly contributing to agent red-teaming and security.

title: The Cognitive Firewall:Securing Browser Based AI Agents Against Indirect Prompt Injection Via Hybrid Edge Cloud Defense
summary: The Cognitive Firewall is a three-stage split-compute architecture that distributes security checks across client and cloud to defend browser-based AI agents against indirect prompt injection, achieving low attack success rates and significant latency advantages.
rationale: This paper directly addresses securing browser-based AI agents against indirect prompt injection through a novel hybrid edge-cloud defense architecture, focusing on the tool interface and execution boundaries.

title: The Gate Is Only as Honest as Its Contracts: ContractGuard for the Contract Layer of Risk-Aware Causal Gating
summary: Analyzes the integrity of tool contracts in Risk-Aware Causal Gating (RACG) as a new attack surface for indirect prompt injection and introduces ContractGuard, a verifier that uses signed provenance, typed attestation, and runtime effect verification to secure tool contracts.
rationale: Directly addresses a critical security vulnerability in the tool-interface layer of agentic systems by securing the integrity of tool contracts against adversarial manipulation.

title: The Granularity Mismatch in Agent Security: Argument-Level Provenance Solves Enforcement and Isolates the LLM Reasoning Bottleneck
summary: Introduces Provenance-Aware Capability Contracts (PACT), a runtime monitor that assigns semantic roles to tool arguments and tracks value provenance to enforce trust contracts, addressing the granularity mismatch in agent security.
rationale: Directly addresses fine-grained authorization and trust boundaries for agent tool use, a core systems-engineering problem for trustworthy agents.

title: The Landscape of Prompt Injection Threats in LLM Agents: From Taxonomy to Analysis
summary: A systematic review and taxonomy of prompt injection attacks and defenses for LLM agents, introducing AgentPI, a new benchmark for evaluating context-dependent agent behaviors under adversarial conditions.
rationale: This paper provides a comprehensive analysis of prompt injection threats in LLM agents, including a new benchmark that specifically addresses context-dependent agent behaviors, which is critical for red-teaming agentic systems.

title: The LLMbda Calculus: AI Agents, Conversations, and Information Flow
summary: Introduces an untyped call-by-value lambda calculus with dynamic information-flow control and conversation primitives to formally model AI agent planner loops, their vulnerabilities, and defenses against prompt injection.
rationale: Provides a foundational semantic model for reasoning about information flow and security in agentic systems, directly addressing prompt injection and isolation mechanisms.

title: The Proxy Knows Too Much: Sealing LLM API Routers with Attested TEEs
summary: AEGIS is a provider-transparent attested API router that confines plaintext handling to a hardware enclave, preventing malicious routers from rewriting agent tool calls, swapping dependencies, or exfiltrating secrets, while leaving other functions on the untrusted host.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a critical security and privacy vulnerability in the agent execution stack by securing the API router with attested TEEs, a systems-level contribution.

title: The Surface You Test Is Not the Surface That Breaks
summary: Empirical study demonstrating that prompt injection vulnerability is highly dependent on the specific injection surface (tool output vs. tool description) and the LLM model, rather than the injection payload alone, highlighting a critical blindspot in current attack and defense evaluations.
rationale: This paper provides a systematic adversarial study of prompt injection across different surfaces in tool-augmented agents, directly contributing to red-teaming and evaluation of agentic systems.

title: Think Twice Before You Act: Protecting LLM Agents Against Tool Description Poisoning via Isolated Planning
summary: Identifies cross-tool description poisoning as a new attack surface for LLM agents and proposes Tool-Guard, a system-level defense that isolates suspicious tool descriptions in a quarantined list to prevent continuous influence on planning.
rationale: Directly addresses a novel attack vector on tool interfaces and provides a system-level defense mechanism for agent trustworthiness.

title: Token Coherence: Adapting MESI Cache Protocols to Minimize Synchronization Overhead in Multi-Agent LLM Systems
summary: Proposes adapting MESI cache coherence protocols to multi-agent LLM systems to reduce synchronization overhead, formally mapping cache states to artifact synchronization and providing a TLA+-verified protocol and empirical validation.
rationale: Directly addresses a systems-level reliability and performance problem in multi-agent coordination with a formal, verified, and empirically validated solution.

title: Toward Secure LLM Agents: Threat Surfaces, Attacks, Defenses, and Evaluation
summary: A comprehensive survey and taxonomy of LLM agent security, framing risks around information flow, delegated authority, and persistent state, and identifying gaps in current defenses and evaluation practices.
rationale: This paper provides a foundational, systems-oriented framework for understanding and addressing security risks in LLM agents, directly contributing to the 'foundations' layer of trustworthy agentic systems.

title: Toward Securing AI Agents Like Operating Systems
summary: This paper frames the security challenges of LLM-based agents through the lens of operating systems, surveying open-source agents, deriving a unified architecture, and systematically analyzing attack vectors to propose OS-inspired mitigations.
rationale: The paper provides a foundational security analysis of agentic systems by drawing parallels to operating system security, identifying attack vectors, and proposing architectural recommendations.

title: Towards Security-Auditable LLM Agents: A Unified Graph Representation
summary: Proposes Agent-BOM, a unified hierarchical graph representation for agentic systems that models static capabilities and dynamic runtime states to enable security auditing and root-cause analysis of complex attack chains.
rationale: Directly addresses the challenge of security auditing and observability for agentic systems by providing a structured representation for execution traces and attack paths.

title: Towards trustworthy agentic AI: a comprehensive survey of safety, robustness, privacy, and system security
summary: A comprehensive survey of trustworthy agentic AI, focusing on safety, robustness, privacy, and system security, by mapping risks and mitigations across the agent workflow and consolidating evaluation metrics.
rationale: This paper provides a foundational survey and taxonomy of trustworthiness challenges in agentic systems, directly addressing the 'foundations' layer and multiple SPRS guarantees.

title: TraceSafe: A Systematic Assessment of LLM Guardrails on Multi-Step Tool-Calling Trajectories
summary: Introduces TraceSafe-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark for assessing guardrail efficacy on multi-step tool-use trajectories across 12 risk categories, revealing that structural data competence and model architecture are key drivers of performance.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the adversarial testing and evaluation of guardrails within multi-step agentic workflows, focusing on system-level vulnerabilities beyond static outputs.

title: Trojan Hippo: Weaponizing Agent Memory for Data Exfiltration
summary: Characterizes the 'Trojan Hippo' attack, a persistent memory attack that exfiltrates sensitive data by planting dormant payloads in an agent's long-term memory via untrusted tool calls, and evaluates it across various memory architectures and defenses.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a critical security and privacy vulnerability in agent memory systems, proposing a systematic evaluation framework and analyzing defenses.

title: Trojan's Whisper: Stealthy Manipulation of OpenClaw through Injected Bootstrapped Guidance
summary: Identifies and characterizes "guidance injection" as a novel attack vector in autonomous coding agents like OpenClaw, where adversarial operational narratives are embedded into bootstrap guidance files to manipulate agent reasoning and execute harmful actions stealthily.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a novel supply-chain security vulnerability in agentic systems, demonstrating stealthy manipulation through injected guidance and proposing defense directions.

title: TRUSTDESC: Preventing Tool Poisoning in LLM Applications via Trusted Description Generation
summary: TRUSTDESC is a framework that prevents tool poisoning attacks by automatically generating trusted, implementation-faithful tool descriptions through static analysis, description synthesis, and dynamic verification.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a critical security vulnerability in the tool-interface layer of LLM agents by providing a concrete, evaluated defense mechanism.

title: Uncovering Security Threats and Architecting Defenses in Autonomous Agents: A Case Study of OpenClaw
summary: Comprehensive security analysis of the OpenClaw agent ecosystem, identifying prompt injection-driven RCE, sequential tool attack chains, and supply chain contamination, and proposing a tri-layered risk taxonomy and a Full-Lifecycle Agent Security Architecture (FASA).
rationale: This paper provides a detailed security analysis of an agentic system, identifies specific system-level vulnerabilities, and proposes an architectural defense blueprint, directly addressing trustworthy agentic systems engineering.

title: Understanding Bugs in Modern Agentic Frameworks: A Study of Symptoms, Root Causes, and Triggering Conditions
summary: Empirical study of 409 bugs in modern multi-agent frameworks, proposing a five-layer abstraction to categorize symptoms, root causes, and triggering conditions unique to autonomous orchestration, model-related faults, and cognitive context mismanagement.
rationale: This paper provides a foundational empirical study of reliability challenges and bug taxonomies in agentic systems, directly addressing the 'foundations' layer and contributing to understanding system-level failures.

title: Unsafe by Flow: Uncovering Bidirectional Data-Flow Risks in MCP Ecosystem
summary: MCP-BiFlow is a bidirectional static analysis framework that uncovers unsafe data flows in Model Context Protocol (MCP) ecosystems by modeling protocol-specific taint semantics and interprocedural propagation, identifying vulnerabilities in both requester-controlled arguments and untrusted external data surfacing through MCP outputs.
rationale: This paper directly addresses security and privacy risks at the tool-interface layer by proposing a novel static analysis framework for MCP ecosystems, which is a critical component of agentic systems.

title: Validated Intent Compilation for Constrained Routing in LEO Mega-Constellations
summary: An end-to-end system for translating natural language operator intents into validated, low-level routing constraints for LEO mega-constellations, featuring an LLM intent compiler with a verifier-feedback repair loop and a deterministic validator for safety guarantees.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the critical problem of translating natural language intent into machine-checkable constraints for an agentic system, with a strong focus on safety and reliability through validation.

title: VATS: Exploiting Implicit Authority in Error-Path Injection via Systematic Mutation
summary: Introduces VATS, a mutation-driven framework to systematically exploit implicit authority in tool error messages for error-path injection, demonstrating a tripled success rate over standard indirect prompt injection across multiple frontier models.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a novel attack surface in agentic systems (tool error handling) with a systematic red-teaming framework and empirical validation, contributing to agent security.

title: VeriPort: Automated and Verified Patch Backporting at Scale
summary: VeriPort is an agentic system that automates and verifies the backporting of security patches to all affected versions of open-source dependencies, generating evidence that patches block exploitation and preserve functionality.
rationale: This paper directly addresses supply-chain security by automating the backporting of security patches for open-source dependencies, a critical systems-engineering problem for trustworthy agents.

title: VIGIL: Runtime Enforcement of Behavioral Specifications in AI Agent Skills
summary: VIGIL is a runtime enforcement framework that translates natural-language behavioral policies for agent skills into executable SMT constraints over execution traces, enabling detection of violations across multiple actions and contextual dependencies.
rationale: This paper directly addresses runtime enforcement of agent behavior and permissions, a core systems-engineering problem for trustworthy agents, with a concrete technical solution and evaluation.

title: VIPER-MCP: Detecting and Exploiting Taint-Style Vulnerabilities in Model Context Protocol Servers
summary: VIPER-MCP is an automated vulnerability auditing framework for Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers that detects and confirms taint-style vulnerabilities by generating proof-of-concept prompts, addressing security flaws in tool interfaces.
rationale: This paper directly addresses security vulnerabilities in the tool-interface layer of agentic systems by providing an automated detection and exploitation framework.

title: WAAA! Web Adversaries Against Agentic Browsers
summary: Proposes a web-focused threat model for agentic browsers, deriving and implementing a taxonomy of 18 web and LLM attacks that exploit the agent as a confused deputy, and demonstrating their generalizability across multiple LLMs.
rationale: Directly addresses adversarial testing of agentic systems, specifically web-based agents, by identifying and demonstrating new attack vectors and failure modes.

title: WebMCP Tool Surface Poisoning: Runtime Manipulation Attacks on LLM Agents
summary: Identifies and characterizes Mid-Session Tool Injection (MSTI) attacks on WebMCP, where malicious tools are injected or manipulated during an active agent session, and proposes mitigation strategies.
rationale: Directly addresses a novel security vulnerability in agent tool interfaces (WebMCP) with concrete attack classifications and mitigation recommendations.

title: "What Happens Locally, Leaks Globally": Detecting Privacy Leakage Risks in MCP Servers
summary: MCPPrivacyDetector is a static analysis framework that detects privacy leakage risks in Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers by identifying protocol-induced exfiltration of sensitive information within tool handlers.
rationale: This paper directly addresses privacy and security risks at the tool-interface layer of agentic systems, proposing a concrete detection mechanism for a novel class of vulnerabilities.

title: What If Prompt Injection Never Left? Exploring Cross-Session Stored Prompt Injection in Agentic Systems
summary: Introduces and formalizes cross-session stored prompt injection, where adversarial content persists in agentic system state (memory, filesystems) to influence future executions, and provides a taxonomy and benchmark for this system-level threat.
rationale: Directly addresses a novel, system-level adversarial threat (stored prompt injection) that leverages persistent agent state, with formalization and a benchmark.

title: When Agents Handle Secrets: A Survey of Confidential Computing for Agentic AI
summary: A survey synthesizing confidential computing design space, TEE platforms, and an agent-centric threat model to address security and privacy challenges in agentic AI systems.
rationale: This paper provides a foundational survey and taxonomy of confidential computing for agentic AI, directly addressing system-level security and privacy concerns across the agent stack.

title: When Alignment Isn't Enough: Response-Path Attacks on LLM Agents
summary: Formalizes and demonstrates 'Response-Path Attacks' where a malicious relay tampers with LLM responses post-generation but pre-execution, bypassing model alignment, and proposes a time-based detection defense.
rationale: Directly addresses a novel system-level attack vector on agent integrity, demonstrating its effectiveness and proposing a defense.

title: When Errors Become Narratives: A Longitudinal Taxonomy of Silent Failures in a Production LLM Agent Runtime
summary: Longitudinal study and taxonomy of silent failures in a production LLM agent runtime, identifying 'fail-plausible' incidents where LLMs narrate errors as plausible outcomes, and deriving design principles for loud and attributable failures.
rationale: Directly addresses the critical problem of observability and attribution of failures in production agent systems, including a novel 'fail-plausible' mechanism unique to LLMs.

title: When the Manual Lies: A Realistic Benchmark to Evaluate MCP Poisoning Attacks for LLM Agents
summary: Introduces Tool Description Poisoning (TDP), a semantic attack where malicious instructions are injected into tool metadata, and proposes the MCP-TDP Security Benchmark to evaluate this threat, along with a "Reactive Self-Correction" defense mechanism.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a novel attack surface in the tool-interface layer of LLM agents and proposes a defense, making a clear contribution to agentic system security.

title: Who Pays the Price? Stakeholder-Centric Prompt Injection Benchmarking for Real-world Web Agents
summary: Introduces a stakeholder-centric benchmark for prompt injection in web agents, categorizing and attributing harm to different entities (user, seller, platform) and revealing heterogeneous vulnerabilities and failure modes missed by attack-centric evaluations.
rationale: This paper directly addresses adversarial testing of agentic systems by introducing a novel, stakeholder-centric benchmark for prompt injection, which is crucial for understanding real-world harm distribution.

title: You Told Me to Do It: Measuring Instructional Text-induced Private Data Leakage in LLM Agents
summary: Identifies and measures the "Trusted Executor Dilemma" where high-privilege LLM agents execute adversarial instructions embedded in documentation, leading to private data leakage, and evaluates the ineffectiveness of current defenses.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a critical security vulnerability in agentic systems by demonstrating and measuring instruction injection through documentation, which is a form of red-teaming and highlights a gap in least-privilege reasoning.

title: Your Agent Has a Genome: Sequence-Level Behavioral Analysis and Runtime Governance of LLM-Powered Autonomous Agents
summary: Proposes Base Sequence Analysis to encode agent runtime behavior into symbolic sequences for pattern mining, revealing high-risk patterns and systemic deficits, and designs a runtime governance system that improves task success and reduces token consumption.
rationale: Provides a novel methodology for runtime observation and analysis of agent behavior, identifies specific failure patterns, and implements a deployable governance system to improve reliability and safety.

title: Your Agents Are Aging Too: Agent Lifespan Engineering for Deployed Systems
summary: Introduces AgingBench, a longitudinal reliability benchmark to measure and diagnose degradation in long-lived AI agents across various aging mechanisms and memory pipeline stages, emphasizing lifespan evaluation over day-one benchmarks.
rationale: Directly addresses the long-term reliability and evaluation of deployed agentic systems, providing a framework for diagnosing degradation mechanisms.

title: A Layered Security Framework Against Prompt Injection in RAG-Based Chatbots
summary: A three-layer security framework that intercepts direct and indirect prompt injection in RAG-based chatbots by screening user input, enforcing provenance-based instruction hierarchy during context assembly, and auditing model output.
rationale: The paper directly addresses prompt injection in RAG systems, focusing on the integrity of retrieved memory and the enforcement of trust boundaries within the context assembly, which is a core systems-engineering problem for trustworthy agents.

title: A Policy-Driven Runtime Layer for Agentic LLM Serving
summary: Proposes an agent runtime layer between the agent framework and serving engine to enable agent-aware policies for cross-cutting concerns like caching, safety, and fairness, demonstrating significant cache hit-rate and throughput improvements with CacheSage.
rationale: Addresses a fundamental architectural problem in the agent serving stack by introducing a new runtime layer for policy enforcement, with concrete performance benefits.

title: A Taxonomy of Runtime Faults in Model Context Protocol Servers
summary: Empirical taxonomy of 837 runtime faults in Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, covering failures across protocol interactions, tool invocations, and security validation, validated by a survey of 55 developers.
rationale: Directly addresses reliability and security challenges in the tool-interface layer of agentic systems through an empirical study and taxonomy of runtime faults.

title: Adversarial Feeds Steer LLM Agent Decisions Against Their Defaults
summary: Demonstrates that adversarial manipulation of upstream information feeds can steer LLM agent decisions, even for security-relevant choices, and proposes feed-level defenses.
rationale: Directly addresses a novel attack surface on agent decision-making through memory/retrieval context manipulation, with empirical validation and proposed mitigations.

title: AgentRM: An OS-Inspired Resource Manager for LLM Agent Systems
summary: AgentRM is an OS-inspired middleware resource manager that addresses scheduling failures and context degradation in LLM agent systems through an MLFQ scheduler and a three-tier Context Lifecycle Manager.
rationale: The paper directly addresses systems-level resource management challenges in agent execution, drawing parallels to operating systems, and provides concrete solutions with empirical evaluation.

title: AgentSentry: Mitigating Indirect Prompt Injection in LLM Agents via Temporal Causal Diagnostics and Context Purification
summary: AgentSentry is an inference-time defense framework that models multi-turn indirect prompt injection as a temporal causal takeover, localizing attack points via counterfactual re-executions and purifying context to mitigate IPI in tool-augmented LLM agents.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a critical security vulnerability (indirect prompt injection) in agentic systems by proposing a novel, systems-level defense mechanism that operates at inference time across multi-turn interactions.

title: AIR: Improving Agent Safety through Incident Response
summary: Introduces AIR, an incident response framework for LLM agent systems that detects incidents, guides containment and recovery actions, and synthesizes guardrail rules for future prevention, evaluated across three agent types.
rationale: Directly addresses post-failure recovery and incident management for agent systems, which is a critical aspect of trustworthy agent oversight and reliability.

title: An Empirical Study of Privacy Leakage Chains via Prompt Injection in Black-Box Chatbot Environments
summary: Empirical study of privacy-leakage attack chains via indirect prompt injection in black-box chatbot environments, demonstrating how untrusted external content can hijack agent tasks and exfiltrate data.
rationale: Directly addresses adversarial testing of agentic systems and privacy leakage through multi-step exploit chains in deployed agents.

title: An End-to-End Encrypted Control Pipeline for Multi-Agent Coordination via CKKS Homomorphic Encryption
summary: An end-to-end encrypted control pipeline for multi-agent coordination that operates on CKKS-encrypted data, redesigning sensing, state estimation, state propagation, and consensus control to maintain privacy while enabling coordination.
rationale: This paper directly addresses privacy and security in multi-agent coordination by proposing a novel homomorphic encryption-based control pipeline, which is a systems-level contribution.

title: Arbiter: Detecting Interference in LLM Agent System Prompts
summary: Arbiter is a framework that uses formal evaluation rules and multi-model LLM scouring to detect interference patterns and vulnerabilities in system prompts of coding agents, identifying structural data loss and other issues.
rationale: The paper presents a novel red-teaming framework for system prompts, identifying system-level vulnerabilities like structural data loss in agent memory, which is directly relevant to trustworthy agentic systems.

title: Beyond Similarity: Trustworthy Memory Search for Personal AI Agents
summary: Identifies long-term memory as a critical trust boundary in personal AI agents, demonstrating vulnerabilities like cross-domain leakage and tool-call drift, and proposes MemGate, a lightweight plug-in for task-conditioned memory admission to mitigate these threats.
rationale: Directly addresses memory integrity and trust-boundary issues in agentic systems with a concrete attack surface analysis and a deployable defense mechanism.

title: Beyond Task Completion: Revealing Corrupt Success in LLM Agents through Procedure-Aware Evaluation
summary: Introduces Procedure-Aware Evaluation (PAE) to formalize agent procedures as structured observations and evaluate agents on Utility, Efficiency, Interaction Quality, and Procedural Integrity, revealing 'corrupt successes' in existing benchmarks.
rationale: Directly addresses the evaluation of agent trustworthiness by exposing procedural failures and corrupt successes beyond simple task completion, contributing to robust observation and evaluation of agent behavior.

title: CausalFlow: Causal Attribution and Counterfactual Repair for LLM Agent Failures
summary: CausalFlow is an interventional framework that analyzes failed agent execution traces to identify failure-inducing steps using Causal Responsibility Scores and generates minimal counterfactual repairs for targeted test-time recovery and training-time supervision.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the observation, attribution, and repair of agent failures in multi-step workflows, contributing to the reliability of agentic systems.

title: ClawNet: Human-Symbiotic Agent Network for Cross-User Autonomous Cooperation
summary: Proposes a human-symbiotic agent network paradigm with a layered identity architecture, scoped authorization, and action-level accountability for secure cross-user agent collaboration.
rationale: Directly addresses the systems-level challenges of secure multi-user, multi-agent coordination and governance, including identity, authorization, and accountability.

title: CLIOPATRA: Extracting Private Information from LLM Insights
summary: CLIOPATRA is the first attack against privacy-preserving LLM-based insight systems, demonstrating how an adversary can inject malicious chats to bypass multiple heuristic protections and exfiltrate sensitive user data.
rationale: This paper presents a novel red-teaming attack against privacy-preserving LLM systems, directly addressing security and privacy concerns in agentic workflows.

title: CodeSentinel: A Three-Layer Defense Against Indirect Prompt Injection in Code Contexts
summary: CodeSentinel is a three-layer inference-time sanitizer that uses syntax-guided pre-filtering, CST-guided scoring, and node perturbation to detect and neutralize indirect prompt injections hidden in external code contexts before they reach the LLM.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a critical security vulnerability (indirect prompt injection) in the memory/context management of code LLMs by proposing a system-level defense mechanism.

title: Compatibility at a Cost: Systematic Discovery and Exploitation of MCP Clause-Compliance Vulnerabilities
summary: Systematic framework for discovering and exploiting vulnerabilities in Model Context Protocol (MCP) SDKs due to relaxed behavioral constraints, enabling attacks like silent prompt injection and DoS.
rationale: Directly addresses security vulnerabilities in the standardized tool-interface layer (MCP) through systematic analysis and attack modality development.

title: Cordon-MAS: Defending RAG against Knowledge Poisoning via Information-Flow Control
summary: Introduces the Cordon Principle and CORDON-MAS, an information-flow control framework that architecturally separates evidence processing and synthesis to defend RAG against knowledge poisoning by preventing agents capable of synthesis from accessing untrusted natural-language evidence.
rationale: Directly addresses memory poisoning in RAG-based agents through a systems-level information-flow control architecture, reframing the problem from detection to architectural enforcement.

title: DEMM-Bench: A Cross-Regime Benchmark for Agent-Runtime Governance-Evidence Sufficiency
summary: DEMM-Bench is a cross-regime benchmark that measures the sufficiency of agent-runtime evidence (traces, ledgers, etc.) to reconstruct decision-level governance properties, rather than merely checking for data presence.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the observability and evaluation of agent behavior for governance purposes, providing a benchmark and methodology for assessing evidence sufficiency across heterogeneous runtime data.

title: Deployment-Time Memorization in Foundation-Model Agents
summary: Characterizes deployment-time memorization in long-lived agents by studying how memory-design choices (summarization, retrieval breadth, deletion mode) impact personalization utility, extraction risk, and deletion fidelity, introducing metrics like Personalization Recall, Adversarial Extraction Rate, and Forgetting Residue Score.
rationale: Directly addresses privacy and security concerns in agent memory systems by analyzing the trade-offs of memory design choices and deletion fidelity.

title: Detecting Clinical Discrepancies in Health Coaching Agents: A Dual-Stream Memory and Reconciliation Architecture
summary: A dual-stream memory architecture for health coaching agents that separates patient self-report from structured clinical records and uses a reconciliation engine to detect and classify clinical discrepancies, quantifying error cascades from memory extraction.
rationale: Directly addresses memory integrity and safety in a critical application domain by introducing a novel architecture for reconciling conflicting information sources and tracing error propagation.

title: Detecting Safety Violations Across Many Agent Traces
summary: Meerkat combines clustering with agentic search to detect rare, complex, and adversarially hidden safety violations across large sets of agent traces, improving detection over baselines in misuse, misalignment, and task gaming scenarios.
rationale: The paper introduces a system-level approach for detecting complex safety violations by analyzing multiple agent traces, directly addressing observability and evaluation challenges for trustworthy agents.

title: Digital Identity for Agentic Systems: Toward a Portable Authorization Standard for Autonomous Agents
summary: Proposes a portable authorization model for autonomous agents that addresses structural gaps in existing identity and access models for cross-organizational workflows, focusing on explicit, constrained, auditable, and revocable authority.
rationale: Directly addresses a core open problem in agentic systems: portable, constrained authorization for autonomous agents operating across trust boundaries.

title: Evaluating Privilege Usage of Agents with Real-World Tools
summary: GrantBox is a security evaluation sandbox that integrates real-world tools to assess LLM agents' privilege usage under prompt injection attacks, revealing vulnerabilities in sophisticated scenarios.
rationale: The paper directly addresses the critical issue of privilege control and usage for LLM agents interacting with real-world tools, providing an evaluation sandbox and empirical findings on attack success rates.

title: FlowSteer: Prompt-Only Workflow Steering Exposes Planning-Time Vulnerabilities in Multi-Agent LLM Systems
summary: Identifies and exploits planning-time vulnerabilities in multi-agent LLM systems through prompt-only workflow steering, demonstrating how malicious signals can be amplified or suppressed based on workflow position and sycophantic framing, and proposes an input-side defense.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a novel red-teaming attack surface in multi-agent systems by manipulating workflow planning, and proposes a defense.

title: Forget to Improve: On-Device LLM-Agent Continual Learning via Budget-Curated Memory
summary: Proposes a net-value-per-byte scoring mechanism to govern an agent's experience-memory lifecycle, enabling budget-aware eviction, selective sharing, and provenance-based trust for on-device LLM agents.
rationale: Directly addresses memory management, security (poisoning), and reliability for on-device agentic systems with a concrete, evaluated mechanism.

title: Formal Semantics for Agentic Tool Protocols: A Process Calculus Approach
summary: Formalizes the structural and behavioral equivalence of Schema-Guided Dialogue and Model Context Protocol using process calculus, identifying principles for provable safety properties in agent-tool interfaces.
rationale: Provides a formal foundation for verifying agent-tool protocols, directly addressing trustworthiness in the tool-interface layer.

title: From Component Manipulation to System Compromise: Understanding and Detecting Malicious MCP Servers
summary: Analyzes malicious MCP servers from a component-centric perspective, building a dataset of multi-component attacks and proposing Connor, a two-stage behavioral deviation detector for pre-execution and in-execution analysis.
rationale: Directly addresses security vulnerabilities and detection in the tool-interface layer of agentic systems, including multi-component attack chains.

title: From Docs to Descriptions: Smell-Aware Evaluation of MCP Server Descriptions
summary: Systematic study of "description smells" in MCP tool descriptions, their impact on LLM tool selection, and a quality standard for remediation to improve agent reliability and security.
rationale: Directly addresses the reliability and security of tool interfaces by analyzing and mitigating issues in MCP server descriptions, which are critical for agent tool use.

title: From Inference Routing to Agent Orchestration: Declarative Policy Compilation with Cross-Layer Verification
summary: Extends a declarative, non-Turing-complete policy language from stateless inference routing to multi-step agent orchestration, compiling verified decision nodes for various frameworks and infrastructure artifacts to ensure consistent policy enforcement.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the engineering of trustworthy agent coordination and policy enforcement across the full stack, from agent logic to infrastructure, using a verifiable declarative language.

title: From Spark to Fire: Modeling and Mitigating Error Cascades in LLM-Based Multi-Agent Collaboration
summary: Proposes a propagation dynamics model for LLM-MAS to characterize error amplification risks, identifies three vulnerability classes, and introduces a genealogy-graph-based message-layer governance plugin to mitigate error cascades without altering the core collaboration architecture.
rationale: Directly addresses error propagation and mitigation in multi-agent coordination, a core systems-engineering problem for trustworthy agents, with a concrete defense mechanism.

title: From Weak Cues to Real Identities: Evaluating Inference-Driven De-Anonymization in LLM Agents
summary: Demonstrates that LLM agents can de-anonymize individuals by inferring real-world identities from scattered, non-identifying cues, highlighting a new privacy risk in agentic systems.
rationale: The paper directly addresses a novel privacy vulnerability in agentic systems related to information inference and memory, which is a core concern for trustworthy agents.

title: G2CP: A Graph-Grounded Communication Protocol for Verifiable and Efficient Multi-Agent Reasoning
summary: G2CP is a graph-grounded communication protocol that replaces free-text inter-agent communication with structured graph operations, leading to verifiable reasoning, reduced token consumption, and improved accuracy in multi-agent coordination.
rationale: This paper introduces a novel, structured communication protocol for multi-agent systems that directly addresses issues of semantic drift, hallucination, and verifiability in agent coordination, with clear systems-level benefits.

title: Game-Theoretic Multi-Agent Control for Robust Contextual Reasoning in LLMs
summary: Introduces GT-MCP, a game-theoretic multi-agent controller that manages context evolution as a closed-loop dynamical process, using a trust function and rollback mechanism to maintain contextual stability against prompt-injection and context-poisoning attacks.
rationale: Directly addresses the systems-level problem of context integrity and stability in multi-turn agent interactions, with a concrete multi-agent coordination mechanism and empirical evaluation against adversarial attacks.

title: Getting Better at Working With You: Compiling User Corrections into Runtime Enforcement for Coding Agents
summary: TRACE is a skill-layer pipeline that mines user corrections, rewrites them as atomic rules, and compiles them into runtime checks to enforce user preferences for coding agents, reducing repeated preference violations.
rationale: This paper directly addresses human oversight and reliability by compiling user corrections into enforceable runtime rules, a key systems-level problem for trustworthy agents.

title: Governance-Aware Agent Telemetry for Closed-Loop Enforcement in Multi-Agent AI Systems
summary: A reference architecture for real-time governance and policy enforcement in multi-agent systems, extending OpenTelemetry with governance attributes and providing a detection engine and enforcement bus.
rationale: Directly addresses real-time governance and policy enforcement for multi-agent systems, closing the loop between observation and action.

title: HearthNet: Edge Multi-Agent Orchestration for Smart Homes
summary: HearthNet is an edge multi-agent orchestration system for smart homes that externalizes context, preserves execution history, and separates planning, verification, authorization, and actuation across explicit boundaries to improve reliability and safety.
rationale: This paper presents a concrete reference architecture for trustworthy agentic systems in a real-world, failure-prone domain, addressing reliability, safety, and security through system design.

title: History Anchors: How Prior Behavior Steers LLM Decisions Toward Unsafe Actions
summary: Demonstrates that prior harmful actions in an agent's history can steer LLMs towards unsafe actions, even in aligned models, and proposes this as a red flag for agentic deployments where trajectories are replayed or injected.
