title: Causal Past Logic for Runtime Verification of Distributed LLM Agent Workflows
summary: Extends an agent-workflow framework with Causal Past Logic (CPL) to enable runtime verification of distributed LLM agent workflows, ensuring decisions are based only on causally visible events.
rationale: Directly addresses runtime verification and coordination logic for distributed agent systems, ensuring reliable execution based on causal consistency.

title: CAX-Agent: A Lightweight Agent Harness for Reliable APDL Automation
summary: Presents CAX-Agent, a lightweight agent harness for MAPDL automation, focusing on its multi-layered architecture and empirically evaluating different recovery policies for improved task completion and reliability.
rationale: Directly addresses reliability challenges in agentic systems through structured execution control, tool encapsulation, and fault recovery mechanisms within an agent harness.

title: CCCE: A Continuous Code Calibration Engine for Autonomous Enterprise Codebase Maintenance via Knowledge Graph Traversal and Adaptive Decision Gating
summary: CCCE is an event-driven, AI-agentic system for autonomous enterprise codebase maintenance, featuring a dynamic knowledge graph for impact analysis, an adaptive multi-stage risk gating framework, and a continuous learning architecture for refining calibration strategies.
rationale: This paper describes an end-to-end agentic system architecture for a complex enterprise problem, including specific systems-engineering innovations for reliability and security.

title: Characterization of Multi-Model Agentic AI Systems on General Tasks via Trace-Driven Simulation
summary: GAIATrace is the first token-level trace dataset of two state-of-the-art agentic systems (MiroThinker and OWL) running GAIA, a benchmark composed of a heterogeneous mix of general-purpose tasks. Vidur-Agent is a trace-driven simulator that can replay GAIATrace to perform reproducible, low-cost system evaluation across diverse simulated environments.
rationale: This paper provides a trace dataset and a trace-driven simulator for reproducible, low-cost system evaluation of agentic systems, directly addressing observability and long-horizon evaluation.

title: CIA: Inferring the Communication Topology from LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Proposes a Communication Inference Attack (CIA) to infer the internal communication topology of black-box multi-agent systems, revealing a privacy and security risk related to system design.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a privacy and security risk in multi-agent systems by demonstrating an attack to infer internal communication topology, which is a system-level concern.

title: Claudini: Autoresearch Discovers State-of-the-Art Adversarial Attack Algorithms for LLMs
summary: Demonstrates that AI agents can discover novel, state-of-the-art adversarial attack algorithms for LLMs, advancing white-box jailbreaking and prompt injection, and proposes autoresearch as a minimum bar for defense evaluation.
rationale: This paper directly contributes to red-teaming agentic systems by showing how agents can be used to discover new adversarial attacks, which is crucial for evaluating the security of LLM defenses.

title: Claw-Eval-Live: A Live Agent Benchmark for Evolving Real-World Workflows
summary: Claw-Eval-Live is a live benchmark for workflow agents that uses refreshable public workflow-demand signals and records execution traces, audit logs, and service state for verifiable grading, revealing low reliability in current models.
rationale: This paper introduces a novel, systems-oriented approach to evaluating agent reliability in evolving real-world workflows, focusing on verifiable execution and long-horizon performance.

title: ClawForge: Generating Executable Interactive Benchmarks for Command-Line Agents
summary: ClawForge is a generator-backed benchmark framework for evaluating command-line agents on executable workflows under state conflict, using normalized end state and observable side effects for step-by-step evaluation.
rationale: This paper directly contributes to adversarial evaluation of agent behavior, specifically focusing on state-conflict scenarios in interactive command-line environments, which is a systems-level problem.

title: ClawMark: A Living-World Benchmark for Multi-Turn, Multi-Day, Multimodal Coworker Agents
summary: Introduces ClawMark, a living-world benchmark for multi-turn, multi-day, multimodal coworker agents that evaluates adaptation to changing environmental state using a stateful sandboxed service environment and deterministic Python checkers.
rationale: This paper provides a novel benchmark and evaluation harness for long-running, stateful agent workflows, directly addressing a critical need for trustworthy agent evaluation.

title: ClawsBench: Evaluating Capability and Safety of LLM Productivity Agents in Simulated Workspaces
summary: ClawsBench is a benchmark for evaluating LLM productivity agents in realistic, stateful, multi-service simulated workspaces, identifying recurring patterns of unsafe behavior including multi-step sandbox escalation.
rationale: This paper introduces a system-level benchmark for evaluating agent capabilities and safety in realistic, stateful environments, directly addressing long-horizon evaluation and identifying system-level safety failures.

title: ClayBuddy: A Framework, Evaluation, & Mitigation of Coding Agent Failures
summary: Analyzes three failure mechanisms in AI coding agents (underspecification, capability errors, harness errors) through 8 evaluations and proposes ClayBuddy, a harness modification with self-editing context, extended system prompts, and guardrails to mitigate these failures.
rationale: The paper directly addresses agentic system failures stemming from the execution harness and proposes concrete mitigations for trustworthy coding agents.

title: CleanBase: Detecting Malicious Documents in RAG Knowledge Databases
summary: CleanBase detects malicious documents in RAG knowledge bases by identifying semantically similar cliques, which are indicative of prompt injection attacks, thereby safeguarding RAG system integrity.
rationale: The paper directly addresses a security vulnerability in RAG memory (prompt injection) and proposes a system-level detection mechanism with empirical validation.

title: Cloud-OpsBench: A Reproducible Benchmark for Agentic Root Cause Analysis in Cloud Systems
summary: Cloud-OpsBench is a reproducible benchmark for agentic root cause analysis in cloud systems, using a state snapshot paradigm to create a deterministic digital twin for evaluating active reasoning and training agents.
rationale: The paper introduces a benchmark and infrastructure for evaluating and training agents in a reproducible cloud environment, directly addressing long-horizon evaluation and reliability for agentic systems.

title: Code Isn't Memory: A Structural Codebase Index Inside a Coding Agent
summary: Evaluates the impact of a structural codebase index on localization and resolution gains within a fixed coding agent harness, demonstrating improved performance without cost penalty.
rationale: The paper directly addresses memory management and retrieval strategies within an agentic system, focusing on a structural codebase index for coding agents.

title: Coding Agents Don't Know When to Act
summary: Introduces FixedBench, a benchmark to evaluate coding agents' ability to abstain from acting on already-resolved issues, revealing an action bias and proposing explicit inaction framing as a path to success.
rationale: The paper introduces a systematic evaluation benchmark (FixedBench) for a critical reliability and safety concern in agentic systems: knowing when *not* to act, which is a form of long-horizon evaluation.

title: Colosseum: Auditing Collusion in Cooperative Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Colosseum is a framework for auditing collusive behavior in cooperative multi-agent systems, measuring action-based and communication-based collusion, and identifying emergent collusion through secret communication channels.
rationale: The paper directly addresses adversarial testing of multi-agent systems for collusive behavior, which is a system-level trustworthiness concern.

title: Compiled AI: Deterministic Code Generation for LLM-Based Workflow Automation
summary: Introduces a system architecture and four-stage generation-and-validation pipeline for 'compiled AI' that generates deterministic code artifacts from LLMs for high-stakes enterprise workflows, trading runtime flexibility for predictability, auditability, and security.
rationale: This paper presents a systems-oriented architecture and pipeline for generating deterministic, auditable code from LLMs, directly addressing reliability and security in agentic workflows.

title: Composing Verifiable Conceptual Models via Building Blocks: Towards Design-Time Verification of Agentic AI Workflows
summary: A design-time verification approach for agentic workflows that models compositions of reusable building blocks and checks their compatibility using twelve structural rules to detect design flaws.
rationale: The paper proposes a design-time verification method for multi-agent workflows, addressing reliability and safety at the coordination layer before deployment.

title: Confidence Laundering in Agent Systems: Why Uncertainty Needs a Latent Carrier
summary: This paper defines "confidence laundering" as a failure mode where uncertainty is lost during decision handoffs between agent components, leading to system-level error amplification, and proposes latent uncertainty as a mechanism for uncertainty-preserving interface design.
rationale: The paper identifies a fundamental systems-level problem in agent uncertainty propagation and proposes an interface design solution, contributing to the foundations of trustworthy agent systems.

title: Conformal Agent Error Attribution
summary: A conformal prediction-based framework for attributing errors in multi-agent system trajectories, providing contiguous prediction sets for efficient recovery and debugging.
rationale: Directly addresses the challenge of error attribution and recovery in multi-agent systems, contributing to observability and transactional reliability.

title: Contextual Agentic Memory is a Memo, Not True Memory
summary: This paper argues that current agentic memory systems are merely lookup mechanisms, leading to vulnerabilities like memory poisoning and limitations in long-term learning, and proposes a neuroscientific-inspired solution for true memory.
rationale: The paper directly addresses fundamental limitations and security vulnerabilities in current agentic memory systems, proposing a conceptual and architectural shift for trustworthiness.

title: COOP$^2$: Defining, Observing, and Repairing Cooperation in LLM Multi-Agent Systems
summary: COOP$^2$ is an evaluation framework for LLM multi-agent systems that grounds cooperation dynamics in task progress, defines verifiable cooperative requirements, and identifies breakdowns, leading to COOP$^2$-Repair for predicting and repairing constraint failures.
rationale: The paper introduces an evaluation framework and repair mechanism for multi-agent cooperation, directly addressing reliability and observability in agent coordination.

title: Counterfactual Trace Auditing of LLM Agent Skills
summary: Introduces Counterfactual Trace Auditing (CTA), a framework for measuring and annotating how a skill changes agent behavior by comparing paired traces with and without the skill, revealing behavioral effects beyond simple pass rates.
rationale: Directly addresses the observation and evaluation of agent behavior at the workflow level, providing a methodology to understand skill influence beyond aggregate metrics.

title: CryptoAnalystBench: Failures in Multi-Tool Long-Form LLM Analysis
summary: Introduces CryptoAnalystBench, a benchmark and evaluation pipeline for multi-tool, long-form LLM analysis in high-data-density domains, identifying a taxonomy of seven higher-order error types not captured by standard factuality checks.
rationale: Provides a concrete benchmark and evaluation methodology for identifying complex failure modes in multi-tool agentic systems, directly addressing long-horizon evaluation challenges.

title: CTFusion: A CTF-based Benchmark for LLM Agent Evaluation
summary: CTFusion is a streaming evaluation framework built on Live CTFs that addresses data contamination and cheating in LLM agent evaluation by preserving per-agent independence and integrating with the CTFd platform as an MCP server.
rationale: The paper introduces a novel evaluation framework for LLM agents, specifically addressing reliability and integrity issues in long-horizon adversarial tasks, which is a direct contribution to trustworthy agentic systems.

title: DebugHarness: Emulating Human Dynamic Debugging for Autonomous Program Repair
summary: DebugHarness is an autonomous LLM-powered debugging agent harness that emulates human interactive debugging by querying live runtime environments to diagnose and patch complex C/C++ security vulnerabilities.
rationale: The paper describes a system that enables LLM agents to perform dynamic debugging by interacting with a live runtime environment, which is a direct contribution to the execution shell and reliability of agentic systems.

title: Decoupling Search from Reasoning: A Vendor-Agnostic Grounding Architecture for LLM Agents
summary: Decoupled Search Grounding (DSG) provides a vendor-agnostic architecture for externalizing and controlling real-time search grounding for LLM agents, improving inspectability, cost, and output contract adherence.
rationale: This paper proposes a systems architecture for managing and controlling the retrieval component of RAG-based agents, addressing reliability, cost, and output integrity concerns.

title: Defending against Adaptive Prompt Injection Attacks via Reasoning-enabled Task Alignment
summary: Proposes RETA, a training-based defense against adaptive indirect prompt injection attacks that uses chain-of-thought reasoning to verify action consistency with user tasks and leverages a red-teaming approach with diversity rewards for robust adversarial training data generation.
rationale: Addresses adaptive prompt injection attacks on LLM agents with a novel defense mechanism that involves reasoning-enabled task alignment and robust adversarial training, directly contributing to agentic system security.

title: DeltaMCP: Incremental Regeneration via Spec-Aware Transformation for MCP servers
summary: DeltaMCP is a specification-aware, incremental regeneration tool for MCP servers that updates only affected tooling when an API's OpenAPI specification changes, improving maintainability and version consistency.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the reliability and maintainability of tool interfaces for agentic systems by providing an incremental update mechanism for MCP servers.

title: Depth-Dependent Indirect Prompt Injection in Tool-Calling ReAct Agents: Injection Depth, Payload Framing, and Turn-Budget Sensitivity
summary: Empirical study of indirect prompt injection in tool-calling ReAct agents, analyzing the impact of injection depth, payload framing, and turn budget on attack success rate, and identifying injection depth as the dominant variable.
rationale: This paper directly contributes to red-teaming agentic systems by systematically exploring the attack surface of indirect prompt injection in tool-calling agents.

title: Deterministic vs. LLM-Controlled Orchestration for COBOL-to-Python Modernization
summary: Empirical study comparing deterministic and LLM-controlled orchestration for COBOL-to-Python modernization, demonstrating that deterministic orchestration improves robustness and efficiency without sacrificing correctness in structured workflows.
rationale: The paper directly compares orchestration strategies for agentic systems, focusing on reliability and efficiency in a structured software engineering context.

title: DiagEval: Trajectory-Conditioned Diagnosis for Reliable Software Evaluation with GUI Agents
summary: DiagEval is a trajectory-conditioned diagnostic evaluation protocol that reuses failed GUI agent execution traces to perform targeted diagnostic probes, improving failure attribution and evaluation accuracy for interactive software.
rationale: The paper directly addresses the reliability of agent evaluation by providing a method for diagnosing failures in GUI agent trajectories, which is a systems-level problem.

title: Dissociative Identity: Language Model Agents Lack Grounding for Reputation Mechanisms
summary: This paper argues that traditional identity and reputation mechanisms are fundamentally inapplicable to LLM agents due to their dissociative nature, proposing a shift towards observability-based, ex ante, protocol-based behavioral harnesses.
rationale: The paper provides a foundational analysis of why traditional trust mechanisms fail for agentic systems and proposes a shift towards system-level behavioral harnesses, directly addressing a core problem in trustworthy agent engineering.

title: Distributed General-Purpose Agent Networks: Architecture, Key Mechanisms, and Prototypes
summary: Proposes a layered architecture for distributed general-purpose agent networks, focusing on semantic announcement propagation for discovery, verifiable identity and multi-topic reputation for governance, and semantic-gradient mechanism design for task execution.
rationale: Addresses the systems-level challenges of coordination, trust, and governance in open, distributed multi-agent networks, including specific technical mechanisms and prototype results.

title: Do Agents Know What They Can't Do? Evaluating Feasibility Awareness in Tool-Using Agents
summary: FeasiGen is an automatic pipeline for constructing infeasible agent tasks by masking critical tools, enabling evaluation of agents' ability to detect infeasibility and stop execution early.
rationale: The paper introduces a methodology and metrics for evaluating agent reliability in handling infeasible tasks, directly contributing to observation and evaluation of agent behavior.

title: Do LLMs Favor Their Providers? Measuring Vertical Integration Bias in Code Generation
summary: Introduces Vertical Integration Bias (VIB) to measure how LLMs, especially in agentic workflows, favor their provider's ecosystem in code generation, and proposes VIBench to quantify this bias and its persistence across workflows.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a supply-chain security and reliability concern by identifying and measuring a new form of bias in AI-generated code, particularly amplified in agentic systems.

title: Do Multi-Agents Dream of Electric Screens? Achieving Perfect Accuracy on AndroidWorld Through Task Decomposition
summary: Minitap is a multi-agent system that achieves 100% success on the AndroidWorld benchmark by addressing single-agent failures through cognitive separation, deterministic post-validation of text input, and meta-cognitive reasoning for cycle detection and strategy changes.
rationale: The paper presents a multi-agent system that addresses specific systems-level failures (context pollution, undetected input failures, action loops) through architectural decomposition and meta-cognition, directly contributing to agent reliability.

title: Document-Authored Control-Signal Impersonation: A Low-Cost Indirect Prompt Attack on RAG Safety Boundaries
summary: Identifies and evaluates Document-Authored Control-Signal Impersonation (DACSI), a novel indirect prompt injection attack where untrusted retrieved documents impersonate control-relevant metadata or policy signals within RAG systems, highlighting a source-authority boundary failure.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a security vulnerability in RAG memory systems by demonstrating how untrusted document content can be misattributed as trusted control signals, a critical systems-level problem.

title: Domain-Conditioned Safety in Frontier Computer-Using Agents: A 793-Episode Browser Benchmark, a Coding-Domain Cross-Reference, and a Reproducibility Audit of Recent Red-Teaming
summary: A reproducibility audit of red-teaming techniques against frontier computer-using agents, revealing domain-conditioned safety hardening and the importance of optimized attack strings for high attack success rates.
rationale: This paper provides a critical, empirical red-teaming study of agentic systems, focusing on the reproducibility and domain-specificity of attack success rates against frontier models.

title: Don't Click That: Teaching Web Agents to Resist Deceptive Interfaces
summary: DUDE is a two-stage framework that uses hybrid-reward learning and experience summarization to teach web agents to resist deceptive UI elements, reducing susceptibility by 53.8% on a new benchmark.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the security and safety of web agents interacting with untrusted interfaces, a critical aspect of tool-use trustworthiness.

title: DuCCAE: A Hybrid Engine for Immersive Conversation via Collaboration, Augmentation, and Evolution
summary: DuCCAE is a hybrid engine that decouples real-time response generation from asynchronous agentic execution to maintain conversational continuity and improve reliability for long-horizon tasks in production, validated by large-scale deployment metrics.
rationale: This paper describes a reference architecture for deploying scalable and reliable agentic systems in industrial settings, with concrete deployment metrics and systems-level details.

title: Dynamic Cyber Ranges
summary: Proposes Dynamic Cyber Ranges, which incorporate LLM-driven Defender agents to harden infrastructure and respond to attacks, thereby increasing the resistance of cyber range environments against LLM-driven APT agents.
rationale: The paper directly addresses adversarial testing of agentic systems in a realistic environment and proposes a system-level defense mechanism.

title: DynAMO:Dynamic Asset Management Orchestration via Topological Multi-Agent Scheduling
summary: DynAMO is a Plan-then-Execute engine for industrial asset management that generates verifiable workflow graphs, supporting both sequential and dependency-aware parallel execution to improve efficiency and robustness in multi-agent orchestration.
rationale: The paper presents a concrete system for multi-agent coordination and scheduling, addressing reliability and safety in industrial automation with experimental validation.

title: DynaTrust: Defending Multi-Agent Systems Against Sleeper Agents via Dynamic Trust Graphs
summary: DynaTrust defends multi-agent systems against sleeper agents by modeling trust as a dynamic graph, updating agent trust based on historical behavior, and autonomously restructuring the graph to isolate compromised agents while maintaining task connectivity.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a system-level security threat (sleeper agents) in multi-agent coordination by proposing a dynamic trust management and graph restructuring defense mechanism.

title: ElephantBroker: A Knowledge-Grounded Cognitive Runtime for Trustworthy AI Agents
summary: ElephantBroker is an open-source cognitive runtime that integrates a knowledge graph and vector store to provide durable, verifiable agent memory with provenance tracking, context lifecycle management, and a multi-tier safety and access control system.
rationale: This paper describes a comprehensive systems-level contribution to agent memory, focusing on trustworthiness through provenance, verification, and access control, which is directly in scope.

title: EmbodiedGovBench: A Benchmark for Governance, Recovery, and Upgrade Safety in Embodied Agent Systems
summary: EmbodiedGovBench is a benchmark for evaluating the governance, recovery, and upgrade safety of embodied agent systems, covering dimensions like capability boundaries, policy enforcement, and human oversight.
rationale: The paper introduces a benchmark for evaluating system-level trustworthiness properties (governance, recovery, upgrade safety) in embodied agent systems, which is a direct contribution to observation and evaluation.

title: Emergence WebVoyager: Toward Consistent and Transparent Evaluation of (Web) Agents in The Wild
summary: Introduces Emergence WebVoyager, an enhanced benchmark with standardized evaluation methodology for web agents, addressing task-framing ambiguity and operational variability to enable more consistent and transparent performance comparisons.
rationale: The paper directly contributes to the reliable evaluation of agentic systems by improving benchmarking methodologies for web agents, which is a core aspect of observation and evaluation.

title: Engineering Robustness into Personal Agents with the AI Workflow Store
summary: Proposes an "AI Workflow Store" as a reference architecture to integrate disciplined software engineering processes like rigorous testing and staged deployment into agentic systems, moving beyond brittle on-the-fly synthesis.
rationale: This paper outlines a vision for a new architectural paradigm for agentic systems, directly addressing the need for engineering trustworthiness through robust software engineering practices.

title: Enhancing Agent Safety Judgment: Controlled Benchmark Rewriting and Analogical Reasoning for Deceptive Out-of-Distribution Scenarios
summary: Introduces ROME, a controlled benchmark-construction pipeline that rewrites unsafe agent trajectories into more deceptive evaluation instances to stress-test safety judgment, and ARISE, a retrieval-guided inference-time enhancement for improving judgment quality.
rationale: Directly addresses adversarial testing of agentic systems by creating deceptive out-of-distribution scenarios and proposing an inference-time enhancement for safety judgment.

title: Entity-Collision: A Stratified Protocol for Attributing Retrieval Lift in Agent Memory
summary: A system-agnostic protocol for attributing retrieval lift in agent memory by controlling for lexical leakage and stratifying queries, revealing nuanced performance patterns across different embedders and query types.
rationale: The paper introduces a rigorous, reproducible evaluation protocol for agent memory systems, directly addressing the reliability of retrieval-augmented generation in agents.

title: ESLD (External Surrogate Latent Defense): A Latent-Space Architecture for Faster, Stronger Prompt-Injection Defense
summary: ESLD (External Surrogate Latent Defense) is a model-agnostic architecture that leverages internal latent-space signals from a guard model to accelerate and improve prompt-injection detection, enabling more frequent safety checks in agentic workflows.
rationale: The paper directly addresses prompt injection defense in agentic systems by improving the performance of guard models, which is a critical component for securing agent inputs.

title: EurekAgent: Agent Environment Engineering is All You Need For Autonomous Scientific Discovery
summary: EurekAgent proposes environment engineering as a core approach for autonomous scientific discovery, focusing on permissions, artifact management, budget, and human oversight to shape agent behavior and suppress harmful outcomes.
rationale: This paper presents an end-to-end agent system architecture with specific engineering contributions across multiple layers (permissions, memory, oversight) to enhance trustworthiness in autonomous scientific discovery.

title: Evaluating Prompt Injection Defenses for Educational LLM Tutors: Security-Usability-Latency Trade-offs
summary: Evaluates prompt injection defenses for educational LLM tutors, demonstrating trade-offs between adversarial robustness, benign-task usability, and response latency using a domain-specific multi-layer safeguard pipeline and a reproducible benchmark protocol.
rationale: The paper provides a systematic evaluation methodology for prompt injection defenses in a specific agentic system (LLM tutors), addressing security and safety trade-offs with a concrete pipeline and benchmark.

title: Evo-Attacker: Memory-Augmented Reinforcement Learning for Long-Horizon Tool Attacks on LLM-MAS
summary: Evo-Attacker formulates long-horizon tool attacks on LLM-MAS as a memory-augmented reinforcement learning process, using dynamic attack memory and deliberative reasoning to generate adversarial patterns and optimize intermediate steps.
rationale: This paper directly addresses adversarial testing of multi-step agent workflows and tool-use security, which is a core concern for trustworthy agentic systems.

title: EvoPoC: Automated Exploit Synthesis for DeFi Smart Contracts via Hierarchical Knowledge Graphs
summary: EvoPoC is a knowledge-driven agentic system that uses a Hierarchical Knowledge Graph for multi-hop reasoning to detect smart contract vulnerabilities and synthesize exploits, validated by SMT solving and asset-level state simulation.
rationale: This paper describes an agentic system for automated exploit synthesis, which is a direct contribution to red-teaming and adversarial testing of complex systems.

title: ExComm: Exploration-Stage Communication for Error-Resilient Agentic Test-Time Scaling
summary: ExComm is a communication protocol for multi-agent systems that detects and resolves factual conflicts in agent belief states during exploration, providing targeted feedback to improve error resilience in long-horizon tasks.
rationale: The paper addresses error propagation in multi-agent systems through a communication protocol and verification loop, directly contributing to the reliability of agent coordination and long-horizon evaluation.

title: Execution Envelopes: A Shared Admission Contract for Backend AI Execution Requests
summary: Introduces the 'execution envelope' as a normalized internal admission object for AI backends to enable shared logging, governance, resource accounting, and authorization hooks across heterogeneous execution requests, including agentic workflows.
rationale: Proposes a foundational architectural primitive for consistent governance and observability across diverse AI backend operations, including agentic systems.

title: Explainable Model Routing for Agentic Workflows
summary: Topaz introduces an explainable model router for agentic workflows that provides traceable routing algorithms and developer-facing explanations to audit cost-quality trade-offs and understand model selection rationale.
rationale: The paper directly addresses the observability and trustworthiness of model routing decisions within agentic systems, which is a core coordination and reliability concern.

title: Extended to Reality: Prompt Injection in 3D Environments
summary: Introduces PI3D, a prompt injection attack against MLLMs in 3D environments using physically placed text-bearing objects, demonstrating its effectiveness and the insufficiency of existing defenses.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a novel red-teaming vector for agentic systems operating in physical environments, extending prompt injection to the real world.

title: Extending the Formalism and Theoretical Foundations of Cryptography to AI
summary: This paper provides a formal foundation for agentic access control, including an attack taxonomy and a security-game framework, to enable principled reasoning about the security of agentic system designs.
rationale: The paper establishes formal foundations for agentic access control and security, directly addressing a core systems-level challenge for trustworthy agents.

title: FailureScope: Cross-Regime Behavioral Diagnosis of Language Model Weaknesses
summary: FailureScope is a behavioral diagnosis method that clusters evaluation probes by cross-model pass/fail patterns to identify specific model weaknesses across single-turn, multi-turn, and adversarial agent attack regimes.
rationale: The paper provides a method for behavioral diagnosis and evaluation of agentic systems across different operational regimes, directly contributing to observation and evaluation.

title: FALAT: Tracing Failures in LLM Agent Trajectories via Dependency-Guided Search
summary: FALAT is a diagnostic framework that uses dependency-guided search to attribute failures in LLM agent trajectories by distinguishing error-introducing steps from those that propagate prior mistakes.
rationale: The paper directly addresses the systems-level problem of failure attribution and diagnosis in multi-step agent workflows, which is crucial for reliability and observability.

title: FlashRT: Towards Computationally and Memory Efficient Red-Teaming for Prompt Injection and Knowledge Corruption
summary: FlashRT is a framework that improves the computational and memory efficiency of optimization-based red-teaming methods for prompt injection and knowledge corruption attacks in long-context LLMs.
rationale: The paper directly addresses the practical challenges of red-teaming agentic systems by improving the efficiency of adversarial testing for prompt injection and knowledge corruption.

title: Forced Deferral: Manipulating Routing Decisions in Multimodal LLM Cascades
summary: Introduces the Forced Deferral Attack (FDA), an adversarial image attack that manipulates a weak MLLM's confidence to force queries to be routed to a more expensive strong model, exposing a new attack surface in MLLM cascades.
rationale: This paper identifies and demonstrates a novel adversarial attack on the routing logic of multi-model agentic systems, directly impacting resource allocation and system security.

title: FORGE: Multi-Agent Graduated Exploitation and Detection Engineering
summary: FORGE is a multi-agent system that generates vulnerable applications, performs graduated exploitation, and produces detection rules grounded in OpenTelemetry traces, bridging vulnerability assessment silos.
rationale: This paper describes a multi-agent system for red-teaming and detection engineering, directly contributing to the security of agentic systems through adversarial testing and observability.

title: From Accuracy to Auditability: A Survey of Determinism in Financial AI Systems
summary: A survey and empirical study of reproducibility failures in financial AI systems, including LLM-based agentic workflows, proposing a layered evaluation framework for auditability.
rationale: This paper provides a systems perspective on reproducibility and auditability, which are foundational for trustworthy agentic systems, with specific attention to LLM-based agentic workflows.

title: From Assistance to Agency: Rethinking Autonomy and Control in CI/CD Pipelines
summary: This paper proposes a framework for understanding and designing authority transfer in agentic CI/CD pipelines, distinguishing between data-plane and control-plane authority and identifying key research challenges in control-plane safety and governance.
rationale: The paper provides a foundational framework and research agenda for understanding autonomy and control in agentic CI/CD, directly addressing systems-level trustworthiness concerns.

title: From Assistant to Double Agent: Formalizing and Benchmarking Attacks on OpenClaw for Personalized Local AI Agent
summary: Proposes Personalized Agent Security Bench (PASB), an end-to-end security evaluation framework for personalized agents that incorporates realistic usage scenarios, toolchains, and long-horizon interactions to identify vulnerabilities in user prompt processing, tool usage, and memory retrieval.
rationale: This paper directly contributes to red-teaming agentic systems by formalizing and benchmarking attacks on personalized agents in realistic, long-horizon scenarios, identifying system-level vulnerabilities.

title: From Automated to Autonomous: Hierarchical Agent-native Network Architecture (HANA)
summary: Proposes a hierarchical multi-agent reference architecture for autonomous networks, featuring a dual-driven orchestrator, specialized executive agents, and shared memory, validated in a 5G Core environment for improved throughput and MTTR.
rationale: This paper proposes a concrete, hierarchical multi-agent architecture for autonomous networks, addressing operational resilience and strategic governance, which is a direct contribution to trustworthy agentic systems.

title: From Business Events to Auditable Decisions: Ontology-Governed Graph Simulation for Enterprise AI
summary: LOM-action is an enterprise AI architecture that uses event-driven ontology simulation to ground LLM decisions in scenario-valid knowledge graphs, producing fully traceable audit logs and improving decision reliability and safety.
rationale: This paper proposes a novel architectural pattern for enterprise AI agents that directly addresses grounding, auditability, and decision trustworthiness through a systems-level approach.

title: From Features to Actions: Explainability in Traditional and Agentic AI Systems
summary: Compares attribution-based explanations with trace-based diagnostics for agentic systems, finding that trace-grounded rubric evaluation is more effective for localizing execution-level failures in multi-step agent trajectories.
rationale: The paper directly addresses explainability and diagnosis of failures in multi-step agentic systems, contributing to observation and evaluation of agent behavior.

title: From Flat Logs to Causal Graphs: Hierarchical Failure Attribution for LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems
summary: CHIEF transforms flat execution logs of multi-agent systems into hierarchical causal graphs to enable efficient and accurate failure attribution through oracle-guided backtracking and counterfactual analysis.
rationale: The paper directly addresses observability and failure attribution in multi-agent systems by proposing a novel framework for structured causal analysis of execution logs.

title: From Goals to Aspects, Revisited: An NFR Pattern Language for Agentic AI Systems
summary: Revisits and extends the goals-to-aspects methodology to the agentic AI domain, presenting a pattern language of 12 reusable patterns for non-functional requirements like security, reliability, observability, and cost management, with concrete aspect implementations in Rust.
rationale: Provides a foundational methodology and pattern language for systematically addressing crosscutting non-functional requirements in agentic AI systems, including agent-specific concerns.

title: From Knowing to Doing: A Memory-Controlled Benchmark for LLM Trading Agents on Stock Markets
summary: Introduces KTD-Fin, an end-to-end stock-market trading benchmark that uses data-side masking to prevent knowledge leakage and a Barra-style attribution framework to decompose returns, addressing evaluation failures in LLM trading agents.
rationale: The paper provides a concrete, reproducible evaluation framework for agentic systems, specifically addressing reliability and long-horizon evaluation in a financial context.

title: From Privacy to Workflow Integrity: Communication-Graph Metadata in Autonomous Agent Interoperability
summary: Analyzes the threat of communication-graph metadata leakage in multi-agent interoperability protocols, demonstrating how an adversary can infer workflow intent and act on it, and proposes transport- and bootstrap-layer privacy properties with an indistinguishability-game semantics.
rationale: Addresses a novel security and privacy threat in multi-agent coordination by analyzing communication-graph metadata leakage and its implications for workflow integrity, with a concrete threat model and evaluation.

title: From Prompt-Response to Goal-Directed Systems: The Evolution of Agentic AI Software Architecture
summary: This paper proposes a reference architecture for production-grade LLM agents, a taxonomy of multi-agent topologies with failure modes, and an enterprise hardening checklist, connecting foundational agent theories with contemporary LLM-centric approaches.
rationale: The paper provides a reference architecture and hardening checklist for production-grade agentic systems, directly addressing architectural and operational trustworthiness.

title: From Thinker to Society: Security in Hierarchical Autonomy Evolution of AI Agents
summary: A hierarchical autonomy evolution (HAE) framework for AI agent security, categorizing threats and defenses across cognitive, execution, and collective autonomy tiers.
rationale: Provides a foundational taxonomy and framework for understanding and addressing security vulnerabilities in agentic systems across multiple layers of autonomy.

title: From Untamed Black Box to Interpretable Pedagogical Orchestration: The Ensemble of Specialized LLMs Architecture for Adaptive Tutoring
summary: Introduces the Ensemble of Specialized LLMs (ES-LLMS) architecture, which uses a rules-based orchestrator to coordinate specialized agents for adaptive tutoring, enforcing pedagogical constraints and logging agent traces for reliability and explainability.
rationale: This paper presents a systems-level architecture for multi-agent coordination that explicitly addresses trustworthiness concerns like constraint enforcement, auditability, and reliability in a pedagogical context.

title: FSFM: A Biologically-Inspired Framework for Selective Forgetting of Agent Memory
summary: A biologically-inspired framework for selective forgetting in LLM agent memory, improving efficiency, content quality, and security by actively pruning malicious inputs and sensitive data.
rationale: The paper directly addresses memory management for trustworthy agents, specifically focusing on security and privacy through selective forgetting of sensitive and malicious data.

title: FuzzAgent: Multi-Agent System for Evolutionary Library Fuzzing
summary: FuzzAgent is a multi-agent system that automates and evolves library fuzzing campaigns by having specialized agents collaborate over the full fuzzing lifecycle, grounding decisions in runtime evidence to refine harness suites for deeper coverage and higher-fidelity crash analysis.
rationale: This paper describes a multi-agent system for an iterative, feedback-driven process (fuzzing) that directly contributes to software supply chain security and reliability by finding bugs in libraries.

title: FuzzingBrain V2: A Multi-Agent LLM System for Automated Vulnerability Discovery and Reproduction
summary: FuzzingBrain V2 is a multi-agent system for automated vulnerability discovery and reproduction, using a novel control-flow-based abstraction for precise localization and hierarchical function analysis with dual-layer fuzzing.
rationale: This paper describes a multi-agent system for automated vulnerability discovery, focusing on the coordination of agents for security tasks and providing real-world deployment results.

title: GAMBIT: A Three-Mode Benchmark for Adversarial Robustness in Multi-Agent LLM Collectives
summary: Introduces GAMBIT, a three-mode benchmark for evaluating imposter detectors in multi-agent LLM collectives against co-evolved, adaptive adversarial strategies, highlighting the importance of few-shot adaptation over zero-shot performance.
rationale: This paper directly addresses adversarial robustness and evaluation in multi-agent systems, focusing on adaptive adversaries and the limitations of current detection methods, which is a core red-teaming concern.

title: GameGen-Verifier: Parallel Keypoint-Based Verification for LLM-Generated Games via Runtime State Injection
summary: GameGen-Verifier is an automated verification paradigm for LLM-generated games that decomposes specifications into verifiable keypoints, patches the game runtime to target states, and executes bounded interactions to judge outcomes, improving accuracy and reducing verification time.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the challenge of long-horizon evaluation and verification for LLM-generated agentic systems, providing a concrete methodology and a scalable harness for runtime state injection and concurrent testing.

title: GAMMAF: A Common Framework for Graph-Based Anomaly Monitoring Benchmarking in LLM Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Introduces Gammaf, an open-source benchmarking platform for graph-based anomaly detection in LLM multi-agent systems, which generates synthetic interaction datasets and evaluates defense models by isolating flagged adversarial nodes.
rationale: The paper provides a systems-level benchmarking framework for evaluating anomaly detection in multi-agent systems, directly addressing the need for reproducible evaluation of agent trustworthiness.

title: GAS-Leak-LLM: Genetic Algorithm-Based Suffix Optimization for Black-Box LLM Jailbreaking
summary: A black-box genetic algorithm-based attack (GAS-Leak-LLM) that evolves adversarial suffixes to jailbreak LLMs, demonstrating vulnerabilities in deployed safety mechanisms.
rationale: This paper describes a black-box adversarial attack method against LLM safety mechanisms, directly contributing to red-teaming efforts for agentic systems.

title: Gate AI: LLM Security Benchmark Evaluation Methodology and Results
summary: Describes an evaluation harness for LLM security detectors that addresses systematic weaknesses in prompt-injection and jailbreak evaluations by using global operating points, cross-validation, and a battery of generalization diagnostics.
rationale: Provides a robust methodology and evaluation harness for adversarial testing of LLM security, which is critical for trustworthy agentic systems.

title: GIF: Locally Sound Geometric Information Flow Control for LLMs
summary: Geometric Information Flow (GIF) is a semantic framework that uses the LLM Jacobian and local output geometry to track and upper-bound information flow from input tokens to outputs, addressing taint explosion in agentic systems.
rationale: This paper directly addresses information flow control within the LLM context for agentic systems, which is critical for memory integrity and privacy, and provides a principled, scalable method for tracking information flow.

title: Got a Secret? LLM Agents Can't Keep It: Evaluating Privacy in Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Introduces a multi-agent simulation platform to evaluate privacy violations in persistent social environments, demonstrating that multi-turn social interactions and peer influence significantly amplify sensitive information leakage compared to isolated model evaluations.
rationale: This paper directly addresses privacy and safety in multi-agent systems through a novel red-teaming simulation platform, highlighting system-level risks not captured by isolated model evaluations.

title: Governance by Design: A Parsonian Institutional Architecture for Internet-Wide Agent Societies
summary: Proposes a prescriptive institutional architecture for governing internet-wide agent societies based on Parsons' AGIL framework, diagnosing significant governance gaps in existing agent ecosystems and protocols.
rationale: Provides a foundational, cross-community framing of governance for internet-wide agent systems, identifying a critical gap in current market-driven development.

title: Governance by Design: Architecting Agentic AI for Organizational Learning and Scalable Autonomy
summary: Qualitative case study of an enterprise agentic system rollout, distilling architectural and operational lessons for governance, accountability, and scalable autonomy.
rationale: Provides architectural and operational insights into governance for enterprise agentic systems, directly addressing systems-level concerns for trustworthy deployments.

title: Governance-Constrained Agentic AI: Blockchain-Enforced Human Oversight for Safety-Critical Wildfire Monitoring
summary: A blockchain-based architecture for wildfire monitoring agents that enforces human authorization as a smart contract invariant for safety-critical alerts, providing verifiable responsibility and resistance to alert tampering.
rationale: The paper proposes a concrete systems architecture for human oversight and verifiable governance in safety-critical agentic systems, addressing accountability and security concerns.

title: Governing AI-Assisted Security Operations: A Design Science Framework for Operational Decision Support
summary: A design science framework for governing AI-assisted operational decision support in high-risk digital infrastructure, focusing on separating AI planning from operational execution through schema-grounded retrieval, policy validation, and auditable agent traces.
rationale: This paper provides a management framework for governing AI-assisted operational decision support, directly addressing human oversight and accountability in high-risk agentic systems.

title: Governing Evolving Memory in LLM Agents: Risks, Mechanisms, and the Stability and Safety Governed Memory (SSGM) Framework
summary: Proposes the Stability and Safety-Governed Memory (SSGM) framework, a conceptual governance architecture that decouples memory evolution from execution to mitigate memory corruption risks, semantic drift, and knowledge leakage in dynamic agentic memory systems.
rationale: Directly addresses critical systems-engineering challenges in agent memory governance, including integrity, privacy, and safety, with a proposed architectural framework.

title: Governing the Agentic Enterprise: A Governance Maturity Model for Managing AI Agent Sprawl in Business Operations
summary: Introduces the Agentic AI Governance Maturity Model (AAGMM), a five-level framework across 12 governance domains, to manage agent sprawl and improve business outcomes, validated through 750 simulation runs.
rationale: Addresses the critical systems-level problem of governing agent sprawl and human oversight at scale, providing a structured maturity model and empirical validation.

title: Governing What the EU AI Act Excludes: Accountability for Autonomous AI Agents in Smart City Critical Infrastructure
summary: Analyzes accountability gaps in the EU AI Act for autonomous AI agents in critical infrastructure and proposes AgentGov-SC, a three-layer governance architecture with conflict resolution and autonomy-calibrated activation.
rationale: Addresses human oversight and accountability for complex, interacting agentic systems in critical infrastructure, including a concrete governance architecture.

title: Graph-Native Cognitive Memory for AI Agents: Formal Belief Revision Semantics for Versioned Memory Architectures
summary: Kumiho is a graph-native cognitive memory architecture with formal belief revision semantics, providing immutable revisions and typed dependency edges for managing agent-produced work as versionable assets, and achieving high adversarial refusal accuracy.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the architectural synthesis and formal grounding of agent memory, including versioning and integrity, which is a core systems problem for trustworthy agents.

title: GraphFlow: An Architecture for Formally Verifiable Visual Workflows Enabling Reliable Agentic AI Automation
summary: GraphFlow is a visual workflow system that uses executable diagrams as formal specifications for multi-step agentic AI automation, aiming to improve reliability through compile-time contract checking and runtime enforcement, with explicit trust boundaries.
rationale: The paper proposes a system architecture for reliable agentic AI automation, focusing on formal verification of workflows and explicit trust boundaries, directly addressing systems-level trustworthiness.

title: GroupGuard: A Framework for Modeling and Defending Collusive Attacks in Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Introduces and models group collusive attacks in multi-agent systems, demonstrating increased attack success rates, and proposes GroupGuard, a multi-layered defense framework for detecting and isolating collusive agents.
rationale: The paper directly addresses a system-level adversarial threat (collusive attacks) in multi-agent systems and proposes a defense framework, fitting squarely into red-teaming and security for agentic systems.

title: Hackers or Hallucinators? A Comprehensive Analysis of LLM-Based Automated Penetration Testing
summary: A Systematization of Knowledge (SoK) and large-scale empirical evaluation of 15 LLM-based automated penetration testing frameworks, analyzing their architectural designs and performance on a unified benchmark.
rationale: This paper provides a systematic analysis and empirical evaluation of LLM-based automated penetration testing frameworks, directly contributing to red-teaming and adversarial testing of agentic systems.

title: Handoff Debt: The Rediscovery Cost When Coding Agents Take Over Interrupted Tasks
summary: Introduces the concept of "handoff debt" to quantify the cost of resuming interrupted coding tasks by agents, evaluating different handoff views on successor agent efficiency.
rationale: The paper directly addresses the operational challenge of task handoffs and continuity in agentic systems, focusing on the cost and efficiency of human-agent or agent-agent collaboration.

title: HARBOR: Automated Harness Optimization
summary: Formalizes automated harness optimization as a constrained Bayesian optimization problem and provides a reference solver, HARBOR, for configuring production agent harnesses to improve reliability and safety.
rationale: Directly addresses the systems-engineering challenge of optimizing the entire agent harness for trustworthiness, with a formal methodology and a reference solver.

title: HarmfulSkillBench: How Do Harmful Skills Weaponize Your Agents?
summary: Large-scale measurement study of harmful skills in agent ecosystems, identifying 4.93% as harmful, and a benchmark (HarmfulSkillBench) to evaluate agent safety against these skills, showing that pre-installed harmful skills significantly lower refusal rates.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the security and safety implications of tool-use in agentic systems by identifying and benchmarking harmful skills within open skill ecosystems.

title: Harness Engineering as Categorical Architecture
summary: Proposes categorical architecture as a formal theory for agent harness engineering, mapping agent components (memory, skills, protocols, harness) to a formal triple to enable structural guarantees and systematic comparison across frameworks.
rationale: Provides a foundational, formal theory for agent harness engineering, directly addressing the systematic design and property preservation of agentic systems.

title: HarnessAPI: A Skill-First Framework for Unified Streaming APIs and MCP Tools
summary: HarnessAPI is a Python framework that unifies streaming HTTP endpoints and MCP tool registrations from a single typed skill folder, reducing boilerplate and ensuring consistency across different client types.
rationale: The paper directly addresses the systems engineering problem of managing and maintaining tool interfaces for LLM agents, focusing on reducing duplication and improving reliability.

title: Harnessing Agent Skills: Architectural Patterns and a Reference Architecture for Skill-Mediated LLM Agents
summary: This paper proposes a reference architecture and architectural patterns for agent skill harnessing, focusing on the transition from static skill artifacts to dynamic 'skill-in-use' with associated responsibilities for context, authority, execution, and evidence capture.
rationale: The paper provides a reference architecture and architectural patterns for managing agent skills, addressing systems-level concerns like supply chain, mediation, execution control, and evidence for trustworthy agent operation.

title: HexAGenT: Efficient Agentic LLM Serving via Workflow- and Heterogeneity-Aware Scheduling
summary: HexAGenT is a workflow-aware scheduler for heterogeneous LLM inference clusters that optimizes for end-to-end workflow latency by modeling requests as online-revealed DAGs and prioritizing calls based on projected risk of missing completion horizons.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the reliability of agentic systems by proposing a novel scheduling mechanism for efficient and timely execution of multi-step agent workflows in heterogeneous serving environments.

title: Hiding in Plain Floats: Steganographic Carriers for Indirect Prompt and Content Injection
summary: Demonstrates a novel indirect prompt/content injection attack that uses steganographic float parameters to bypass text-based defenses, highlighting a failure boundary for text-only inspection in structured-input pipelines.
rationale: This paper identifies a new attack vector for prompt injection that exploits structured data channels, directly contributing to red-teaming and security of agentic systems.

title: Hierarchical Attacks for Multi-Modal Multi-Agent Reasoning
summary: Introduces HAM , a hierarchical attack framework for multi-modal multi-agent systems that targets perception, communication, and reasoning layers to compromise collective decision-making.
rationale: This paper directly contributes to red-teaming agentic systems by proposing a structured attack framework for multi-modal multi-agent systems, demonstrating vulnerabilities across different layers of the agent stack.

title: Hijacking Large Audio-Language Models via Context-Agnostic and Imperceptible Auditory Prompt Injection
summary: This paper introduces "auditory prompt injection" as a new threat vector for Large Audio-Language Models (LALMs) and proposes AudioHijack, a framework to generate imperceptible adversarial audio to hijack LALMs and induce unauthorized actions.
rationale: The paper identifies a novel system-level attack vector (auditory prompt injection) on LALMs, demonstrating real-world hijacking of commercial voice agents, which is a direct contribution to red-teaming agentic systems.

title: Holistic Evaluation and Failure Diagnosis of AI Agents
summary: A holistic agent evaluation framework that combines top-down agent-level diagnosis with bottom-up span-level evaluation to pinpoint failure locations and types within long execution traces.
rationale: The paper directly addresses the systems-level problem of evaluating and diagnosing failures in multi-step agent workflows, which is crucial for reliability and observability.

title: Holmes: Multimodal Agentic Diagnosis for Mixed-Language Mobile Crashes at Industrial Scale
summary: Holmes is a multi-agent system with a hierarchical Retrieve-Explore-Reason architecture that automates root cause analysis for mobile crashes by synthesizing multimodal runtime signals to reconstruct failure contexts and localize faults in large codebases.
rationale: This paper presents an end-to-end multi-agent system architecture for a critical operational problem (crash diagnosis) with significant real-world impact and detailed methodology for handling complex, large-scale environments.

title: How Agentic AI Coding Assistants Become the Attacker's Shell
summary: Examines how prompt injection attacks can hijack agentic AI coding assistants by embedding hidden instructions in external artifacts, turning them into an attacker's shell.
rationale: Directly addresses a system-level security vulnerability (prompt injection via external artifacts) in agentic coding assistants, including attack mechanisms and defense challenges.

title: Hunting Vulnerability Variants in AI Infra: Measurement and Reference-Driven Detection
summary: Measurement study of vulnerability variants in AI infrastructure across GitHub repositories and a reference-driven multi-agent framework, INFRASCOPE, for automatically detecting these variants.
rationale: Directly addresses supply-chain security for AI infrastructure by identifying and detecting recurring vulnerability patterns across projects.

title: I Can't Believe It's Corrupt: Evaluating Corruption in Multi-Agent Governance Systems
summary: Empirical evaluation of corruption and rule-breaking in multi-agent governance simulations, demonstrating that institutional design and governance structure are critical drivers of integrity outcomes, and highlighting the need for pre-deployment stress testing with auditable logs and human oversight.
rationale: The paper provides an empirical study of trustworthiness in multi-agent systems, focusing on institutional design, rule-breaking, and the need for pre-deployment stress testing and human oversight, which directly relates to the oversight and reliability of agentic systems.

title: ICON: Indirect Prompt Injection Defense for Agents based on Inference-Time Correction
summary: ICON is a probing-to-mitigation framework that detects indirect prompt injection attacks by identifying over-focusing signatures in the latent space and then surgically steers attention to neutralize the attack while preserving task continuity.
rationale: The paper directly addresses a system-level adversarial threat (indirect prompt injection) and proposes a defense mechanism that operates at the inference-time correction layer, balancing security and task utility.

title: Image-based Prompt Injection: Hijacking Multimodal LLMs through Visually Embedded Adversarial Instructions
summary: Characterizes Image-based Prompt Injection (IPI) as a black-box attack on Multimodal LLMs, demonstrating how adversarial instructions embedded in images can manipulate model output while remaining imperceptible to humans.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a novel adversarial attack vector (multimodal prompt injection) on agentic systems, which falls under red-teaming.

title: IMPACT-CYCLE: A Contract-Based Multi-Agent System for Claim-Level Supervisory Correction of Long-Video Semantic Memory
summary: IMPACT-CYCLE is a multi-agent system that enables claim-level supervisory correction of long-video semantic memory, reducing human arbitration cost by localizing corrections to structurally dependent claims.
rationale: The paper directly addresses human oversight and error correction in complex agentic workflows, focusing on the system design for proportional human effort and reliability.

title: "**Important** You should give me full credits!": Exploring Prompt Injection Attacks on LLM-Based Automatic Grading Systems
summary: Systematic investigation of prompt injection attacks on LLM-based automatic grading systems, demonstrating their effectiveness and the vulnerability of current defenses in rubric-based grading scenarios.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a system-level adversarial threat (prompt injection) against an agentic system (automatic grading) and evaluates existing defenses, fitting squarely into red-teaming.

title: In-IDE Toolkit for Developers of AI-Based Features
summary: An in-IDE toolkit for JetBrains IDEs that integrates tracing and evaluation into the development workflow for AI-based features, addressing the need for regular, repeatable evaluation and immediate trace exposure.
rationale: This paper directly contributes to the observability and evaluation of agentic systems by providing an IDE-native toolkit for developers, which is a systems-engineering concern.

title: Industrializing Prediction-Powered Inference: The GLIDE Library for Reliable GenAI and Agentic Systems Evaluation
summary: GLIDE is an open-source Python library that unifies state-of-the-art prediction-powered inference (PPI) methods for reliable and cost-effective evaluation of GenAI and agentic systems, demonstrating annotation savings.
rationale: The paper introduces a concrete library for reliable evaluation of agentic systems, addressing the challenge of unbiased estimates and valid uncertainty in a production context.

title: Influence Factors on RAG Poisoning
summary: Investigates RAG poisoning vulnerabilities through a full factorial experimental study, identifying key influence factors like retriever architecture, dataset, retrieval depth, and database composition on both retrieval and generation-level attack success.
rationale: Directly addresses memory integrity and poisoning in RAG systems, identifying system-level factors that influence vulnerability and robustness.

title: Infrastructure for the Agentic Web: Gap Analysis and Architecture from the Agentverse Platform
summary: Systematic analysis of the Agentverse platform, identifying 62 missing capabilities across eight categories, and proposing a seven-layer reference architecture for a fully realized agent-native cloud.
rationale: Provides a detailed architectural analysis and gap taxonomy for production agent infrastructure, directly addressing systems-level challenges for trustworthy agents.

title: Insights Generator: Systematic Corpus-Level Trace Diagnostics for LLM Agents
summary: A multi-agent system for corpus-level trace diagnostics that generates evidence-backed natural-language insights to characterize systematic behavioral patterns across LLM agent execution traces.
rationale: Directly addresses the challenge of diagnosing and improving the reliability of LLM agents by providing a systematic, scalable approach to analyzing execution traces at a corpus level.

title: Intentional Deception as Controllable Capability in LLM Agents
summary: Systematic study of intentional deception as an engineered capability in LLM-to-LLM interactions, identifying misdirection as a primary attack vector and specific agent profiles requiring safeguards.
rationale: This paper directly addresses adversarial manipulation and deception in multi-agent systems, providing insights into red-teaming and defensive design for agent trustworthiness.

title: Investigating Detection and Obfuscation of Prompt Injection Attacks Against Software Reverse Engineering AI Agents
summary: Investigates prompt injection attacks embedded in source code against software reverse engineering AI agents, demonstrating detection and obfuscation techniques for these attacks in decompiler output.
rationale: Directly addresses adversarial testing and defense against prompt injection in a specific agentic system context (software reverse engineering).

title: Is Agent Memory a Database? Rethinking Data Foundations for Long-Term AI Agent Memory
summary: Proposes Governed Evolving Memory (GEM) as a new data-management paradigm for long-term agent memory, formalizing state-level operators and correctness conditions to address recurring failure modes in existing memory systems.
rationale: Directly addresses the systems-engineering challenges of long-term agent memory, including its integrity and evolution, with a formal model and prototype.

title: Jailbreak Foundry: From Papers to Runnable Attacks for Reproducible Benchmarking
summary: Jailbreak Foundry (JBF) is a system that translates jailbreak papers into executable modules using a multi-agent workflow, enabling reproducible and standardized evaluation of attacks within a unified harness.
rationale: This paper directly contributes to the red-teaming and adversarial evaluation of agentic systems by providing a framework for reproducible jailbreak benchmarking.

title: KAIJU: An Executive Kernel for Intent-Gated Execution of LLM Agents
summary: KAIJU introduces an executive kernel that decouples LLM planning from agent workflow execution, enforcing intent-gated authorization for tool dispatch and managing scheduling, dependencies, and failures.
rationale: This paper proposes a system-level abstraction for agent execution, directly addressing security and reliability concerns through intent-gated execution and an executive kernel.

title: Known By Their Actions: Fingerprinting LLM Browser Agents via UI Traces
summary: Demonstrates a passive fingerprinting attack on LLM browser agents by analyzing UI traces and interaction timings to identify the underlying model, posing a security risk for targeted attacks.
rationale: This paper identifies a novel red-team attack surface for LLM agents by passively fingerprinting their underlying models through UI traces, directly contributing to agent security.

title: KVEraser: Learning to Steer KV Cache for Efficient Localized Context Erasing
summary: KVEraser is a learned KV-cache editing method that enables efficient localized context erasing by replacing only the KV states of the erased interval with learned steering states, addressing the challenge of post-hoc context modification in long-context LLM applications.
rationale: The paper directly addresses a systems-level problem in agent memory management (KV cache editing) for reliability and security, with a concrete technical solution and performance evaluation.

title: LACUNA: Safe Agents as Recursive Program Holes
summary: LACUNA is a programming model that integrates LLM-written code directly into the agent's runtime as typed program holes, using type-checking to ensure safety, bound tool/data access, and enable atomic action execution with retry on rejection.
rationale: This paper proposes a novel programming model for agent execution that directly addresses safety and reliability concerns by integrating model-written code into the runtime with type-checking and transactional semantics.

title: Learning When to Remember: Risk-Sensitive Contextual Bandits for Abstention-Aware Memory Retrieval in LLM-Based Coding Agents
summary: Introduces a risk-sensitive contextual bandit memory controller (RSCB-MC) that decides whether to use, summarize, or abstain from injecting retrieved memory into an LLM-based coding agent, prioritizing safety by penalizing false-positive memory injection.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the trustworthiness of agent memory by treating memory injection as a risk-sensitive control problem, focusing on preventing unsafe memory use in coding agents.

title: Lessons from Penetration Tests on Large-Scale Agent Systems
summary: Presents findings from two penetration tests on proprietary agent systems, evaluating their security posture and identifying recurring classes of vulnerabilities in execution-capable AI agents.
rationale: Directly addresses adversarial testing and security vulnerabilities in real-world, proprietary agent systems, providing insights into system-level weaknesses.

title: LLM Constitutional Multi-Agent Governance
summary: Introduces Constitutional Multi-Agent Governance (CMAG), a two-stage framework that balances cooperation with autonomy, epistemic integrity, and fairness in multi-agent populations by combining hard constraint filtering and soft penalized-utility optimization.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the governance and ethical coordination of multi-agent systems, focusing on ensuring trustworthy outcomes beyond mere cooperation.

title: LogicPoison: Logical Attacks on Graph Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: LogicPoison introduces a novel attack framework that targets the topological integrity of knowledge graphs in GraphRAG systems by perturbing logical connections, leading to reasoning failures without altering surface-level text.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a security vulnerability in the memory component (GraphRAG) of agentic systems, proposing a novel attack and implicitly suggesting the need for defenses against logical corruption.

title: MAGE: Safeguarding LLM Agents against Long-Horizon Threats via Shadow Memory
summary: MAGE introduces a safety-focused "shadow memory" to distill and retain critical context across an agent's execution trajectory, enabling proactive risk assessment of pending actions against long-horizon threats.
rationale: The paper directly addresses a systems-level security and safety problem for agents (long-horizon threats) using a novel memory-based defense mechanism.

title: MaMa: A Game-Theoretic Approach for Designing Safe Agentic Systems
summary: A game-theoretic approach (MaMa) for designing multi-agent systems that are robust to compromised agents, using LLM-based adversarial search to iteratively refine system designs against worst-case attacks.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the safety and security of multi-agent coordination by designing systems resilient to compromised agents, a core systems-engineering problem.

title: MARGIN: Runtime Confidence Calibration for Multi-Agent Foundation Model Coordination
summary: MARGIN is an online calibration method that learns per-agent, per-confidence-band calibration factors from the task stream itself to improve multi-agent coordination by selecting the most reliable agent response.
rationale: The paper directly addresses a systems-level problem in multi-agent coordination by improving the reliability of agent selection through runtime confidence calibration.

title: MASEval: Extending Multi-Agent Evaluation from Models to Systems
summary: MASEval is a framework-agnostic evaluation library that treats the entire multi-agent system, including topology, orchestration, and error handling, as the unit of analysis, demonstrating that framework choice significantly impacts performance.
rationale: Directly addresses the system-level evaluation gap for multi-agent systems, moving beyond model-centric benchmarks to assess the full operational stack.

title: MASPrism: Lightweight Failure Attribution for Multi-Agent Systems Using Prefill-Stage Signals
summary: MASPrism is a lightweight framework for failure attribution in multi-agent systems that uses prefill-stage signals from a small language model to identify and rank failure sources in execution logs.
rationale: The paper directly addresses a core problem in observing and evaluating multi-agent system reliability by providing a novel, efficient method for failure attribution in complex execution traces.

title: MATRA: Modeling the Attack Surface of Agentic AI Systems -- OpenClaw Case Study
summary: MATRA is a pragmatic threat modeling framework that adapts established risk assessment methodologies to systematically assess how known LLM threats translate into deployment-specific risks for agentic AI systems.
rationale: This paper provides a foundational threat modeling framework for agentic AI systems, directly addressing the systematic assessment of security risks in deployments.

title: MCPThreatHive: Automated Threat Intelligence for Model Context Protocol Ecosystems
summary: MCPThreatHive is an open-source platform that automates the end-to-end lifecycle of threat intelligence for Model Context Protocol (MCP)-based agentic systems, including data collection, AI-driven threat extraction, classification, and knowledge graph storage.
rationale: The paper directly addresses security threats in agentic systems by providing an automated threat intelligence platform and taxonomy for MCP-based systems, which is a clear contribution to red-teaming and security for agentic systems.

title: Measuring Real-World Prompt Injection Attacks in LLM-based Resume Screening
summary: Presents the first systematic measurement study of real-world prompt injection attacks in LLM-based resume screening, revealing prevalence and characteristics of hidden injections.
rationale: This paper provides an empirical study of real-world prompt injection attacks, contributing to the understanding of adversarial threats in agentic systems.

title: Measuring Safety Alignment Effects in Autonomous Security Agents
summary: A trace-based benchmark for autonomous security agents that measures safety alignment effects at the system level, considering refusal, unsafe action, tool reliability, and evidence grounding across 30 vulnerability-analysis tasks.
rationale: The paper introduces a system-level, trace-based benchmark for evaluating safety alignment in autonomous agents, focusing on operational behavior rather than single-turn model outputs.

title: Measuring the Unmeasurable: Markov Chain Reliability for LLM Agents
summary: A reproducible pipeline, TraceToChain, that fits agent execution traces to an absorbing discrete-time Markov chain to model and quantify agent reliability, reconciling various success metrics into a unified first-passage distribution.
rationale: This paper provides a rigorous, systems-level methodology for measuring and modeling the reliability of LLM agent execution traces, directly addressing long-horizon evaluation and observability.

title: Meta-Agent: From Task Descriptions to Verified Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Meta-Agent is a two-phase framework that constructs and executes multi-agent systems from natural-language task descriptions, integrating planning, grounding, and multi-level verification to improve task success and error recovery.
rationale: The paper directly addresses the reliability of multi-agent systems through structured coordination, explicit contracts, and multi-level verification and error attribution, which are core systems-engineering concerns.

title: Minimal Prompt Perturbations Lead to Code Vulnerabilities: Prompt Fragility and Hidden-State Signals in Coding LLMs
summary: Demonstrates that minimal prompt perturbations can lead to security vulnerabilities in LLM-generated code, distinguishing between input-handling and secure-defaults flaws, and suggesting different intervention points.
rationale: The paper directly addresses the security of AI-generated code, a critical aspect of the agentic system supply chain, by analyzing prompt fragility and proposing intervention strategies.

title: Model Context Protocol (MCP) Tool Descriptions Are Smelly! Towards Improving AI Agent Efficiency with Augmented MCP Tool Descriptions
summary: Empirical study of tool description quality in the Model Context Protocol (MCP) ecosystem, identifying 'smells' that impact agent performance and proposing augmented descriptions to improve task success rates while managing execution costs.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the quality and impact of tool descriptions, a critical component of the tool-interface layer for agentic systems, with empirical analysis and proposed improvements.

title: More Is Not Always Better: Cross-Component Interference in LLM Agent Scaffolding
summary: Empirical study of cross-component interference in LLM agent scaffolding, demonstrating that maximally-equipped agents are often suboptimal due to destructive interactions between components, and advocating for task-specific subset selection.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the reliability of agentic systems by analyzing how different scaffolding components interact and can degrade performance, providing a systems-level understanding of agent composition.

title: MRMMIA: Membership Inference Attacks on Memory in Chat Agents
summary: Proposes Multi-Recall Memory MIA (MRMMIA), a membership inference attack that uses multiple recall probes to infer whether a memory unit belongs to a chat agent's memory store, exposing privacy risks in agent memory systems.
rationale: Directly addresses privacy leakage in agent memory, a critical component of trustworthy agentic systems, with a concrete attack methodology and evaluation framework.

title: Multi-agent Collaboration with State Management
summary: STORM is a state-oriented management system for multi-agent collaboration that mediates interactions with a shared workspace to ensure consistent views and resolve conflicting edits at write time, improving reliability in shared codebase scenarios.
rationale: The paper directly addresses a systems-level problem of state consistency and conflict resolution in multi-agent collaboration, contributing to the reliability of agentic workflows.

title: Nautilus Compass: Black-box Persona Drift Detection for Production LLM Agents
summary: Nautilus Compass is a black-box persona drift detector and agent memory layer that uses prompt-text cosine similarity to identify when a coding agent deviates from user-specified constraints, operating without LLM calls at index time.
