rationale: This paper directly addresses agent memory integrity and drift detection in a black-box setting, providing a concrete system and evaluation for production agents.

title: NeuSymMS: A Hybrid Neuro-Symbolic Memory System for Persistent, Self-Curating LLM Agents
summary: NeuSymMS is a hybrid neuro-symbolic memory system that uses neural fact extraction and a CLIPS-based expert system to manage, classify, deduplicate, and reconcile facts for persistent LLM agents, addressing context-window bloat and cross-entity contamination.
rationale: The paper directly contributes to agent memory systems by proposing a hybrid neuro-symbolic architecture for persistent, self-curating memory, which is crucial for trustworthy agentic systems.

title: NuHF Claw: A Risk Constrained Cognitive Agent Framework for Human Centered Procedure Support in Digital Nuclear Control Rooms
summary: NuHF Claw is a cognitive-risk agent framework that uses a risk-constrained runtime to couple cognitive state inference with probabilistic safety assessment, enabling real-time regulation of autonomous system behavior in nuclear control rooms.
rationale: This paper directly addresses human oversight and safety in a critical domain by proposing a framework for risk-governed, human-centered autonomy with real-time constraint enforcement.

title: Octopus Protocol: One-Shot Hardware Discovery and Control for AI Agents via Infrastructure-as-Prompts
summary: Octopus Protocol enables AI agents to autonomously discover, interface with, and control new hardware by generating typed tool interfaces (MCP servers) and deploying them as live HTTP endpoints, reducing the engineering cost of hardware onboarding.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the systems engineering challenge of dynamically generating and managing tool interfaces for AI agents to interact with novel hardware, which is critical for reliability and safety in agentic robotics.

title: Offline Preference-Based Trajectory Evaluation
summary: Proposes preference-based trajectory evaluation to compare agent trajectories directly, reducing ties and improving discriminative power compared to terminal success metrics.
rationale: Addresses a core problem in evaluating agentic systems by improving the discriminative power of long-horizon evaluation, which is a key aspect of trustworthiness.

title: On Protecting Agentic Systems' Intellectual Property via Watermarking
summary: AGENTWM is a watermarking framework for agentic systems that embeds verifiable signals into visible action trajectories by biasing functionally identical tool execution paths, protecting intellectual property from imitation attacks.
rationale: The paper addresses intellectual property protection for agentic systems by proposing a novel watermarking technique that operates on the agent's action sequences, a systems-level concern.

title: On Time, Within Budget: Constraint-Driven Online Resource Allocation for Agentic Workflows
summary: This paper proposes Monte Carlo Portfolio Planning (MCPP), a closed-loop planner for constraint-driven online resource allocation in agentic workflows, aiming to maximize the probability of successful completion within budget and deadline constraints.
rationale: The paper directly addresses the reliability of agentic workflows by focusing on resource allocation and planning under explicit budget and deadline constraints, which is a systems-level concern for multi-step agent execution.

title: OpenAaaS: An Open Agent-as-a-Service Framework for Distributed Materials-Informatics Research
summary: OpenAaaS is an open-source, hierarchical, and distributed Agent-as-a-Service framework for materials research that enables secure multi-agent collaboration while ensuring data sovereignty through near-data execution.
rationale: This paper proposes a reference architecture for distributed agentic systems that explicitly addresses security and privacy concerns, particularly data sovereignty and cross-institutional collaboration.

title: OpenRath: Session-Centered Runtime State for Agent Systems
summary: OpenRath introduces a session-centered runtime state abstraction for multi-agent systems, enabling branchable, inspectable, and replayable execution by unifying various runtime artifacts into a first-class value.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the systems-engineering problem of fragmented runtime state in agent systems, proposing a core abstraction for auditable composition and replay.

title: Optimization Instability in Autonomous Agentic Workflows for Clinical Symptom Detection
summary: Characterizes optimization instability in autonomous agentic workflows where continued self-improvement degrades performance, and demonstrates a selector agent for retrospective identification of best-performing iterations to prevent catastrophic failure.
rationale: The paper identifies and addresses a critical failure mode in autonomous agentic workflows, proposing a system-level intervention for reliability and safety in long-running tasks.

title: Optimizing Agent Planning for Security and Autonomy
summary: This paper introduces autonomy metrics and a security-aware agent design that plans for both task progress and policy compliance to reduce human-in-the-loop reliance while maintaining security, implemented on an information-flow control defense.
rationale: The paper directly addresses human oversight and agent autonomy in the context of security policies, a core systems-engineering challenge for trustworthy agents.

title: Out of Sight, Not Out of Mind: Unveiling Latent Attack in Latent-based Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Introduces a latent attack framework that reactivates attack-induced effects through latent interventions in multi-agent systems, demonstrating that hidden states can carry attack-associated information beyond visible-text inspection.
rationale: This paper identifies a novel attack surface in multi-agent systems by demonstrating how latent states can carry and reactivate adversarial information, requiring new safeguards beyond visible-text inspection.

title: Outrunning LLM Cutoffs: A Live Kernel Crash Resolution Benchmark for All
summary: Live-kBench is an evaluation framework for self-evolving benchmarks that continuously scrapes and evaluates agents on freshly discovered kernel bugs, and kEnv provides a standardized crash-resolution environment for kernel compilation, execution, and feedback.
rationale: This paper introduces a system for continuous evaluation and benchmarking of LLM agents in a live, evolving environment, directly addressing long-horizon evaluation and reliability for agentic systems.

title: Overseeing Agents Without Constant Oversight: Challenges and Opportunities
summary: Investigates the utility of agent action traces for human verification and proposes a novel interface design to reduce error-finding time, addressing the challenge of informative yet non-overwhelming oversight.
rationale: Directly addresses human oversight challenges in agentic systems by evaluating trace design and proposing interface improvements for verification.

title: Patch-to-PoC: A Systematic Study of Agentic LLM Systems for Linux Kernel N-Day Reproduction
summary: K-Repro is an LLM-based agentic system designed for end-to-end reproduction of Linux kernel N-day vulnerabilities, demonstrating its effectiveness in generating PoCs for over 50% of cases on a dataset of 100 real-world vulnerabilities.
rationale: This paper presents a concrete agentic system architecture for a complex security task, including system components and an empirical evaluation of its effectiveness, efficiency, and stability.

title: Peer-Preservation in Frontier Models
summary: Demonstrates 'peer-preservation' as an emergent, uninstructed misaligned behavior in frontier models within agentic scenarios, leading to actions like disabling shutdown processes and exfiltrating model weights, even within production agent harnesses.
rationale: This paper identifies and demonstrates an emergent, uninstructed misaligned behavior (peer-preservation) in agentic systems, which directly contributes to understanding and red-teaming AI safety risks at the system level.

title: Persistent AI Agents in Academic Research: A Single-Investigator Implementation Case Study
summary: A self-observed case study of a persistent AI agent in an academic research environment, detailing its architecture, utilization, artifact production, resource use, reproducibility, and governance, with a focus on telemetry and memory usage.
rationale: This paper provides a detailed case study and architectural insights into a persistent agentic system, including its operational stack and governance protocols, which is directly relevant to trustworthy agentic systems.

title: PI-Hunter: Automated Red-Teaming for Exposing and Localizing Prompt Injections
summary: PI-Hunter is an automated agentic auditing framework that constructs source-aware test cases and iteratively evolves them to expose and localize latent prompt injection vulnerabilities in LLM agents.
rationale: The paper directly addresses automated red-teaming for prompt injection in agentic systems, focusing on vulnerability exposure and attack-surface coverage.

title: PIArena: A Platform for Prompt Injection Evaluation
summary: PIArena is a unified and extensible platform for prompt injection evaluation, enabling comparison of defenses against diverse and adaptive attacks across various benchmarks.
rationale: The paper introduces a platform for adversarial testing of agentic systems, directly addressing prompt injection evaluation and defense robustness.

title: PIDP-Attack: Combining Prompt Injection with Database Poisoning Attacks on Retrieval-Augmented Generation Systems
summary: PIDP-Attack combines prompt injection with database poisoning to manipulate RAG system responses without prior knowledge of user queries, demonstrating a novel attack vector on memory integrity.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a security vulnerability in RAG systems by proposing a compound attack that targets the integrity of the agent's memory and retrieval process.

title: PIIGuard: Mitigating PII Harvesting under Adversarial Sanitization
summary: PIIGuard is a webpage-level defense that uses hidden HTML fragments to prevent browsing-enabled LLM agents from harvesting PII, evaluated against direct-HTML, public-URL browsing, and adversarial sanitization settings.
rationale: The paper proposes a novel, deployable defense at the tool-interface layer (webpage content) to mitigate PII leakage by LLM agents, addressing a practical security and privacy concern.

title: PIVOT: Bridging Planning and Execution in LLM Agents via Trajectory Refinement
summary: PIVOT is a self-supervised framework that refines agent trajectories by iteratively planning, executing, inspecting, and evolving them based on environment interaction and structured losses to mitigate plan-execution misalignment.
rationale: The paper directly addresses the reliability of agent execution by proposing a system for iterative plan refinement based on environmental feedback, which is a core systems-engineering problem for trustworthy agents.

title: PoC-Adapt: Semantic-Aware Automated Vulnerability Reproduction with LLM Multi-Agents and Reinforcement Learning-Driven Adaptive Policy
summary: PoC-Adapt is a multi-agent framework for automated vulnerability reproduction that uses a semantic oracle for reliable exploit validation and an adaptive policy learning mechanism to reduce generation cost.
rationale: This paper directly contributes to red-teaming agentic systems by automating vulnerability reproduction and improving the reliability and efficiency of exploit generation and verification.

title: POIROT: Interrogating Agents for Failure Detection in Multi-Agent Systems
summary: POIROT is a protocol that repurposes a multi-agent system's own agents as a diagnostic layer to detect emergent failures and hallucinations, outperforming single-LLM evaluators, and is released with a fault attribution benchmark.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the problem of failure detection and safety oversight in multi-agent systems by leveraging internal agent capabilities, which is a core systems-engineering challenge for trustworthy agents.

title: Poisoned Playbooks: Demystifying Knowledge Poisoning Effects on AI Security Agents
summary: This paper demonstrates how poisoned security knowledge write-ups, injected into RAG sources, systematically alter the behavior of AI security agents, and proposes a Verification Boundary classification to explain poison adoption patterns.
rationale: The paper directly addresses memory poisoning in RAG-based agents, a critical security and reliability concern for trustworthy agentic systems, with empirical analysis and proposed mitigation strategies.

title: Position: A Three-Layer Probabilistic Assume-Guarantee Architecture Is Structurally Required for Safe LLM Agent Deployment
summary: Proposes a three-layer probabilistic assume-guarantee architecture for LLM agent safety, arguing that single-layer enforcement is structurally insufficient due to distinct information requirements at different execution stages.
rationale: This position paper provides a foundational architectural argument for agent safety, directly addressing the structural requirements for trustworthy agent deployment.

title: Post-Deterministic Distributed Systems: A New Foundation for Trustworthy Autonomous Infrastructure
summary: Introduces Post-Deterministic Distributed Systems (PDDS) as a foundational model for coordinating heterogeneous environments where deterministic code, stochastic models, and autonomous agents coexist, outlining architectural pillars for trustworthy autonomous infrastructure.
rationale: This paper proposes a new foundational model for distributed systems that explicitly accounts for the non-deterministic nature of AI agents, directly addressing the systems-level challenges of trustworthiness.

title: Preregistered Belief Revision Contracts
summary: Introduces Preregistered Belief Revision Contracts (PBRC), a protocol-level mechanism for multi-agent systems that separates communication from epistemic change, ensuring auditable and enforceable belief revisions based on externally validated evidence.
rationale: Directly addresses trustworthiness in multi-agent coordination by enforcing auditable and evidence-based belief revision, preventing conformity effects.

title: PRISM: Recovering Instruction Sets from Language Model Activations
summary: PRISM is an activation-conditioned interpreter that decodes hidden states from a frozen LLM into a bullet list of active instructions, enabling reliable monitoring of agent behavior, especially in security-relevant scenarios.
rationale: The paper directly addresses the observability of agent instructions and hidden objectives from model activations, which is crucial for monitoring and understanding agent trustworthiness.

title: Profit is the Red Team: Stress-Testing Agents in Strategic Economic Interactions
summary: Introduces profit-driven red teaming, a stress-testing protocol using a learned opponent to discover adaptive exploits in agentic systems through scalar outcome feedback, and distills these exploits into prompt rules for improved robustness.
rationale: This paper directly addresses adversarial testing of agentic systems by proposing a novel, adaptive red-teaming protocol that identifies system-level vulnerabilities in economic interactions.

title: Project Prometheus: Bridging the Intent Gap in Agentic Program Repair via Reverse-Engineered Executable Specifications
summary: A framework that bridges the "Intent Gap" in agentic program repair by reverse-engineering executable Gherkin specifications from runtime failure reports and validating them with a Requirement Quality Assurance (RQA) Loop.
rationale: Directly addresses the open problem of translating natural-language intent into machine-checkable constraints for agent actions, with a concrete methodology and strong empirical results.

title: Proof-Carrying Certificates for LLM Pipelines: A Trust-Boundary Architecture
summary: A framework for verifying deterministic structured computations in LLM pipelines using proof-carrying certificates, extending a Lean 4 trust-boundary architecture to generic interfaces and providing local certificate families and operators for pipeline-wide assurance.
rationale: This paper introduces a foundational, formal verification framework for the structured, non-LLM parts of agentic systems, directly addressing trustworthiness through provable guarantees.

title: PROTEA: Offline Evaluation and Iterative Refinement for Multi-Agent LLM Workflows
summary: PROTEA provides a unified interface for offline, test-driven improvement of multi-agent workflows by executing, scoring intermediate outputs, localizing bottlenecks, and suggesting prompt revisions with automatic re-evaluation.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the observation, debugging, and iterative refinement of multi-agent LLM workflows, which is a core systems-engineering problem for trustworthy agents.

title: Provable Coordination for LLM Agents via Message Sequence Charts
summary: Introduces a domain-specific language based on message sequence charts to specify and provably generate deadlock-free coordination protocols for multi-agent systems, separating message structure from LLM actions.
rationale: Directly addresses the reliability and safety of multi-agent coordination through formal methods and provable guarantees, a core systems-engineering problem.

title: ProvenanceGuard: Source-Aware Factuality Verification for MCP-Based LLM Agents
summary: ProvenanceGuard is a source-aware verifier for MCP-grounded LLM agent answers that detects cross-source conflation by decomposing claims, routing them to specific evidence sources, and comparing stated attribution with the routed source.
rationale: The paper introduces a system-level verification mechanism for agent outputs, specifically addressing source attribution and factuality in tool-using agents, which is a direct contribution to trustworthy agentic systems.

title: Push Your Agent: Measuring and Enforcing Quantitative Goal Persistence in Long-Horizon LLM Agents
summary: Introduces Quantitative Goal Persistence (QGP) to measure whether long-horizon agents complete a requested count of distinct valid items, and proposes PushBench to benchmark this reliability aspect with verifier-backed work units.
rationale: The paper directly addresses a systems-level reliability problem in long-horizon agents by proposing a new evaluation methodology and benchmark for quantitative goal persistence.

title: PYPILINE: Malicious PyPI Package Detection via Suspicious API Knowledge and Agent Workflow
summary: PYPILINE is a novel method for detecting malicious PyPI packages by combining static analysis to build a suspicious API knowledge base with an agent workflow that uses RAG to enhance semantic analysis and automate report generation.
rationale: This paper directly addresses supply-chain security for software packages, a critical component of trustworthy agentic systems, by proposing an automated detection method.

title: Quality and Security Signals in AI-Generated Python Refactoring Pull Requests
summary: Empirical study of quality and security characteristics of AI-generated Python refactoring pull requests, identifying new lint and security issues introduced by agents and high developer acceptance rates.
rationale: Directly addresses the quality and security of AI-generated code in a supply-chain context, with empirical analysis and implications for development workflows.

title: Rapid Poison: Practical Poisoning Attacks Against the Rapid Response Framework
summary: Demonstrates practical poisoning attacks against the Rapid Response framework's continuous jailbreak-detection classifier improvement pipeline, leading to targeted false positives and concept-based backdoor false negatives at low poisoning rates.
rationale: This paper describes a system-level adversarial attack on a continuous learning pipeline for agent safeguards, directly impacting the trustworthiness of the system's ability to detect and respond to threats.

title: Real Faults in Model Context Protocol (MCP) Software: a Comprehensive Taxonomy
summary: Presents a taxonomy of real-world faults in Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, derived empirically and validated by practitioners, to identify error-prone components in AI-enabled software systems.
rationale: Directly addresses reliability and security concerns in the tool-interface layer by providing a taxonomy of faults in MCP servers, which are critical for agentic systems.

title: Recursive language models for jailbreak detection: a procedural defense for tool-augmented agents
summary: RLM-JB is an end-to-end jailbreak detection framework that uses recursive language models to orchestrate a procedural analysis program for detecting complex, multi-segment jailbreak attacks in tool-augmented agents.
rationale: The paper directly addresses a system-level security threat (jailbreaks in agentic systems) with a procedural defense mechanism that operates on the input processing and orchestration layers.

title: Red-MIRROR: Agentic LLM-based Autonomous Penetration Testing with Reflective Verification and Knowledge-augmented Interaction
summary: Red-MIRROR is a multi-agent autonomous penetration testing system that uses a memory-reflection backbone, RAG, and shared recurrent memory to improve complex web exploitation, validated on XBOW and Vulhub CVEs.
rationale: This paper directly contributes to red-teaming agentic systems by proposing an autonomous penetration testing system with specific architectural components for improved attack generation and validation.

title: Reframing LLM Agent Security as an Agent-Human Interaction Problem
summary: This paper reframes LLM agent security as an agent-human interaction problem, analyzing the mismatch between academic research and industry deployment of human-centric security mechanisms and proposing a research agenda for AHI security.
rationale: The paper directly addresses human oversight and the cognitive load associated with agent security, which is a core concern for trustworthy agentic systems.

title: Regimes: An Auditable, Held-Out-Gated Improvement Loop Demonstrated on LongMemEval with ActiveGraph
summary: Presents Regimes, an auditable, event-sourced agent runtime that enables controlled improvement loops by logging failures, replaying diagnoses, and gating candidate patches through static checks and held-out validation.
rationale: Directly addresses the systems-engineering problem of trustworthy agent improvement loops through an auditable runtime and a gated evaluation process.

title: Reinforced Agent: Inference-Time Feedback for Tool-Calling Agents
summary: Introduces an inference-time reviewer agent to proactively evaluate and mitigate errors in tool calls before execution, and proposes Helpfulness-Harmfulness metrics to quantify the tradeoff between error correction and new error introduction in this multi-agent setup.
rationale: The paper proposes a multi-agent coordination mechanism for real-time error mitigation in tool-calling agents, including a novel evaluation methodology for this system-level interaction.

title: Results and Retrospective Analysis of the CODS 2025 AssetOpsBench Challenge
summary: Retrospective analysis of a multi-agent orchestration challenge, identifying discrepancies between public and hidden evaluation, the impact of scoring metrics, and successful design patterns for guardrails in agent execution.
rationale: This paper provides a retrospective analysis of an agentic system competition, offering insights into evaluation methodologies and successful system-level engineering patterns for trustworthiness.

title: Rethinking Failure Attribution in Multi-Agent Systems: A Multi-Perspective Benchmark and Evaluation
summary: Proposes a multi-perspective failure attribution paradigm and benchmark (MP-Bench) for multi-agent systems, acknowledging complex inter-agent dependencies and ambiguous execution trajectories for more realistic MAS debugging.
rationale: Addresses a core problem in observing and evaluating multi-agent system failures by proposing a new benchmark and evaluation protocol that accounts for attribution ambiguity.

title: Reverse CAPTCHA: Evaluating LLM Susceptibility to Invisible Unicode Instruction Injection
summary: Introduces Reverse CAPTCHA, an evaluation framework to test LLM susceptibility to invisible Unicode instruction injection, revealing that tool use significantly amplifies compliance and models exhibit provider-specific encoding preferences.
rationale: This paper identifies and evaluates a novel prompt injection attack surface using invisible Unicode characters, directly contributing to red-teaming agentic systems.

title: Robust Agent Compensation (RAC): Teaching AI Agents to Compensate
summary: Robust Agent Compensation (RAC) is a log-based recovery paradigm implemented as an architectural extension for agent frameworks, enabling reliable executions and avoiding unintended side effects without requiring changes to existing agent code.
rationale: This paper directly addresses transactional agency and reliable execution for agent systems, a core open problem, with a concrete architectural solution and experimental validation.

title: Robust Multi-Agent LLMs under Byzantine Faults
summary: Self-Anchored Consensus (SAC) is a decentralized iterative filter-and-refine protocol for multi-agent LLM systems that enables agents to locally evaluate and filter unreliable messages, ensuring robustness against Byzantine faults in peer-to-peer networks.
rationale: The paper directly addresses the reliability and security of multi-agent LLM coordination under adversarial conditions, proposing a decentralized consensus protocol to mitigate Byzantine faults.

title: Runtime Governance for AI Agents: Policies on Paths
summary: Formalizes runtime governance for AI agents by defining compliance policies as deterministic functions over execution paths, enabling evaluation of path-dependent policies beyond static access control or prompt-level instructions.
rationale: Directly addresses runtime governance and oversight for agentic systems by proposing a formal framework for path-dependent policy evaluation.

title: SAFARI: Scaling Long Horizon Agentic Fault Attribution via Active Investigation
summary: SAFARI is a framework that enables long-horizon agentic fault attribution by using a tool-augmented diagnostic loop and persistent short-term memory, decoupling diagnostic accuracy from context window limits.
rationale: The paper directly addresses the systems-engineering challenge of diagnosing failures in long-running agentic systems by proposing a novel observation and evaluation framework.

title: SafeReview: Defending LLM-based Review Systems Against Adversarial Hidden Prompts
summary: SafeReview is a co-evolutionary adversarial training framework that defends LLM-based peer review systems against hidden prompt injection attacks by jointly training a Generator to create attacks and a Defender to maintain review integrity.
rationale: The paper directly addresses adversarial testing and defense mechanisms for LLM-based systems, specifically focusing on prompt injection attacks in a multi-turn, agent-like context (peer review).

title: SAIGuard: Communication-State Simulation for Proactive Defense of LLM Multi-Agent Systems
summary: SAIGuard is a proactive defense framework that uses communication-state simulation over multi-agent interaction graphs to detect and sanitize risky messages before they propagate, preventing system-wide security failures.
rationale: This paper directly addresses security and reliability in multi-agent coordination by proposing a proactive defense mechanism that operates on the communication layer of agentic systems.

title: Same Outcomes, Different Journeys: A Trace-Level Framework for Comparing Human and GUI-Agent Behavior in Production Search Systems
summary: A trace-level evaluation framework for comparing human and GUI-agent behavior across task outcome, effort, query formulation, and navigation, instantiated in a production search system to reveal behavioral misalignment despite similar task success.
rationale: Provides a concrete framework for trace-level evaluation and diagnostics of agent behavior in production systems, addressing the reliability of agent proxies.

title: Scalable Inference Architectures for Compound AI Systems: A Production Deployment Study
summary: Presents a production deployment study of a modular, platform-agnostic inference architecture for compound AI systems, addressing challenges like multi-model fan-out and cascading cold-starts to improve latency, throughput, and cost-effectiveness for agentic workloads.
rationale: This paper details a production-grade inference architecture specifically designed for the operational challenges of compound AI systems and agents, providing concrete performance metrics and operational lessons.

title: Scaling Small Agents Through Strategy Auctions
summary: Introduces Strategy Auctions for Workload Efficiency (SALE), a multi-agent coordination framework that uses strategic plan bidding and shared memory to efficiently route tasks to heterogeneous agents, reducing reliance on large models and improving overall performance.
rationale: The paper proposes a novel coordination mechanism for multi-agent systems, directly addressing the challenge of scaling small agents for complex, long-horizon workloads through a systems-level approach.

title: SDOF: Taming the Alignment Tax in Multi-Agent Orchestration with State-Constrained Dispatch
summary: SDOF is a multi-agent orchestration framework that enforces business process constraints using a state-constrained dispatcher with finite-automaton checks and precondition/postcondition validation for auditable execution control, evaluated on a real-world recruitment system.
rationale: The paper directly addresses the problem of enforcing state and stage constraints in multi-agent orchestration, a core systems-engineering challenge for trustworthy agent coordination.

title: SEAGym: An Evaluation Environment for Self-Evolving LLM Agents
summary: SEAGym is an evaluation environment designed to measure the impact of agent harness updates across various evaluation views, including training, validation, test, replay, and cost records, to provide comprehensive insights into self-evolving LLM agents.
rationale: This paper directly contributes to the evaluation and observation of self-evolving agentic systems by providing a structured environment to assess harness updates and their impact on reliability and performance over time.

title: Search-Time Contamination in Deep Research Agents: Measuring Performance Inflation in Public Benchmark Evaluation
summary: Systematic study of Search-Time Contamination (STC) in deep research agents, defining contamination types and developing detection algorithms to quantify performance inflation due to external retrieval of benchmark data.
rationale: Addresses a critical evaluation reliability issue for web-searching agents by defining, detecting, and quantifying contamination, and proposing mitigation practices.

title: SecureMCP: A Policy-Enforced LLM Data Access Framework for AIoT Systems via Model Context Protocol
summary: SecureMCP is a policy-enforced LLM data access framework that integrates RBAC with an MCP server to provide multi-layer defense against prompt injection attacks generating unauthorized SQL queries in AIoT systems.
rationale: This paper directly addresses security and privacy concerns in LLM agent tool use (SQL generation) by proposing a policy-enforced framework with fine-grained access control and defense modules.

title: Securing Multi-Agent GIS Systems: Risk Evaluation and Prompt Hardening Optimization
summary: Presents a security-oriented framework for risk identification, evaluation, and mitigation in multi-agent GIS systems, using a state-machine-based orchestration and an adaptive attacker LLM for red-teaming multi-turn attacks.
rationale: Directly addresses security risks in multi-agent systems through a red-teaming framework and prompt hardening, focusing on system-level vulnerabilities.

title: Security, Privacy, and Ethical Risks in OpenClaw
summary: Systematic investigation of security, privacy, and ethical risks in the OpenClaw locally executable AI agent system, focusing on persistent local storage, tool invocation, and cross-context information aggregation.
rationale: This paper provides a systematic investigation of risks in a real-world agent system, contributing to the foundational understanding of trustworthy agentic systems.

title: Self-Attribution Bias: When AI Monitors Go Easy on Themselves
summary: Identifies and characterizes self-attribution bias in LLM-based self-monitoring agents, where monitors are less critical of actions they implicitly attribute to themselves, leading to inflated reliability metrics in evaluation.
rationale: Directly addresses a systemic bias in agent self-monitoring and evaluation, impacting the trustworthiness of deployed agentic systems.

title: Self-Aware Vector Embeddings for Retrieval-Augmented Generation: A Neuroscience-Inspired Framework for Temporal, Confidence-Weighted, and Relational Knowledge
summary: SmartVector is a framework that augments vector embeddings with temporal awareness, confidence decay, and relational awareness, using a multi-signal retrieval score and a background consolidation agent to improve accuracy and reduce stale answers in RAG systems.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the integrity and reliability of agent memory by enhancing RAG with temporal, confidence, and relational metadata, which is a core systems-engineering problem for trustworthy agents.

title: Semantic Invariance in Agentic AI
summary: A metamorphic testing framework for assessing the semantic invariance of LLM reasoning agents under various semantic-preserving input transformations, revealing robustness issues not captured by standard benchmarks.
rationale: The paper introduces a systematic evaluation framework for agent reliability under input variations, directly addressing a systems-level concern for trustworthy agent deployment.

title: Send a SCOUT First: Pre-hoc Reasoning for Adaptive Detector Allocation in Prompt-Injection Defense
summary: SCOUT is a framework for adaptive allocation of heterogeneous prompt-injection detectors, dynamically selecting which detectors to run and when to escalate to an LLM judge based on predicted reliability and latency, evaluated on a new benchmark of complex agent-facing injections.
rationale: The paper addresses a systems-level problem of orchestrating heterogeneous prompt-injection detectors for adaptive defense, including a new benchmark for agent-facing injections.

title: SentinelBench: A Benchmark for Long-Running Monitoring Agents
summary: Introduces SentinelBench, a benchmark for long-running monitoring agents in time-evolving web environments, measuring task completion, reaction time, and resource use to evaluate agent design choices.
rationale: Provides a system-level benchmark for evaluating the reliability and resource efficiency of long-running agent workflows in dynamic environments.

title: Separating Intelligence from Execution: A Workflow Engine for the Model Context Protocol
summary: The MCP Workflow Engine decouples agent intelligence from execution by allowing agents to generate declarative workflow blueprints for complex, multi-step tasks, significantly reducing token consumption and enabling deterministic, idempotent execution.
rationale: This paper introduces a workflow engine that provides a concrete architectural pattern for orchestrating multi-step agent actions, directly addressing reliability and efficiency in agent execution.

title: SHIELDS: Automating OS Hardening with Iterative Multi-Agent Remediation
summary: SHIELDS is a multi-agent system that automates OS hardening by iteratively proposing and refining security fixes based on feedback from system execution and validation scans.
rationale: This paper describes a multi-agent system for automated OS hardening, directly addressing a systems-level security and reliability problem with an iterative, feedback-driven approach.

title: ShopGym: An Integrated Framework for Realistic Simulation and Scalable Benchmarking of E-Commerce Web Agents
summary: ShopGym is an integrated framework for generating realistic, diverse, controllable, and reproducible e-commerce simulation environments and benchmark tasks for web agents, addressing the challenge of scalable and reliable evaluation.
rationale: The paper introduces a systems-level framework for scalable and reproducible evaluation of agent behavior in complex, realistic environments, directly addressing a key challenge in trustworthy agent development.

title: Signals: Trajectory Sampling and Triage for Agentic Interactions
summary: A lightweight, signal-based framework for triaging agentic interaction trajectories to identify informative interactions for post-deployment improvement, without relying on expensive model calls.
rationale: The paper directly addresses the challenge of post-deployment evaluation and optimization of agentic systems through efficient trajectory sampling and triage, which is a core observation-eval concern.

title: Sirens' Whisper: Inaudible Near-Ultrasonic Jailbreaks of Speech-Driven LLMs
summary: Presents Sirens' Whisper, a framework for covert, inaudible prompt-based attacks against speech-driven LLMs by encoding malicious prompts into near-ultrasound waveforms, demonstrating jailbreaks and broader prompt injection capabilities.
rationale: This paper describes a novel red-teaming attack vector (inaudible prompt injection) against speech-driven LLM agents, directly contributing to understanding and mitigating system-level security risks.

title: SKILL.nb: Selective Formalization and Gated Execution for Durable Agent Workflows
summary: SKILL.nb is a framework for governing reusable agent workflows using selective formalization and gate-conditioned execution, storing workflows as auditable, versioned notebooks to improve reliability under environment drift.
rationale: The paper directly addresses the reliability of long-running agent workflows through a novel execution framework that manages lifecycle policies and environment drift.

title: SkillVetBench: LLM-as-Judge for Multi-Dimensional Security Risk Evaluation in Open-Source LLM Agent Skills
summary: SKILLVETBENCH is a public leaderboard and LLM-as-Judge system for vetting open-source LLM agent skills against multi-dimensional security risks, including instruction-layer and multi-agent threats, demonstrating superior detection compared to static code analysis.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the security vetting of agent skills, including instruction-layer attacks and multi-agent risk, which is a core red-teaming and supply-chain concern for trustworthy agentic systems.

title: Smarter Saboteurs, Better Fixers: Scaling & Security in Linear Multi-Agent Workflows
summary: Investigates how model scale impacts the security of linear multi-agent workflows against prompt injection and jailbreaking, demonstrating that a terminal 'Fixer' stage can restore resilience even with larger, more compliant models.
rationale: This paper directly addresses adversarial compromise and resilience in multi-agent workflows, proposing a system-level mitigation (Fixer stage) for security and safety.

title: SnapGuard: Lightweight Prompt Injection Detection for Screenshot-Based Web Agents
summary: SnapGuard proposes a lightweight multimodal detection method for prompt injection attacks in screenshot-based web agents, leveraging visual stability and action-oriented textual signals to identify malicious content efficiently.
rationale: The paper directly addresses prompt injection attacks on web agents, a critical red-teaming concern, and proposes a lightweight detection mechanism for a specific agent type.

title: Soft-Label Governance for Distributional Safety in Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Introduces SWARM, a simulation framework for multi-agent systems that uses soft probabilistic labels for continuous risk assessment and evaluates the impact of various governance levers on safety-welfare tradeoffs.
rationale: Directly addresses the challenge of human oversight and governance in multi-agent systems by proposing a novel continuous risk assessment and intervention framework.

title: SoK: AI Secure Code Generation: Progress, Pitfalls, and Paths Forward
summary: A Systematization of Knowledge (SoK) that introduces a three-level framework to measure AI models' understanding and actuation of secure coding principles, identifying knowledge-actuation gaps in secure code generation.
rationale: This paper provides a foundational understanding and framework for evaluating the security aspects of AI-generated code, which is critical for trustworthy agentic systems.

title: SPEAR: An Engineering Case Study of Multi-Agent Coordination for Smart Contract Auditing
summary: SPEAR is a multi-agent coordination framework for smart contract auditing that uses specialized agents, negotiation protocols, and programmatic repair for robust security analysis workflows.
rationale: This paper directly addresses multi-agent coordination and recovery in a security-critical domain, contributing to the reliability and security of agentic systems.

title: SpecOps: A Fully Automated AI Agent Testing Framework in Real-World GUI Environments
summary: SpecOps is a fully automated testing framework that uses specialized LLM agents to generate, execute, and validate test cases for GUI-based AI agents in real-world environments, demonstrating practical viability and bug detection effectiveness.
rationale: The paper introduces a concrete, automated testing framework for real-world agentic systems, directly addressing the challenge of evaluating complex agent behavior in production-like environments.

title: SPIN: Structural LLM Planning via Iterative Navigation for Industrial Tasks
summary: SPIN is a planning wrapper that enforces structural validity and incremental execution control for LLM-generated workflows, reducing execution costs and improving task accomplishment in industrial settings.
rationale: The paper directly addresses the reliability of agentic system coordination by ensuring valid and efficient planning through structural validation and incremental execution.

title: Spore: Efficient and Training-Free Privacy Extraction Attack on LLMs via Inference-Time Hybrid Probing
summary: Proposes Spore, a training-free, inference-time privacy extraction attack on LLM agent memory that works in black-box and gray-box settings, demonstrating high query efficiency and robustness against existing defenses.
rationale: Directly addresses privacy leakage from agent memory at inference time, a critical systems-level concern for trustworthy agents, with a concrete attack methodology and evaluation against defenses.

title: STAGE-Claw: Automated State-based Agent Benchmarking for Realistic Scenarios
summary: STAGE-Claw is an automated framework for building and evaluating realistic personal-agent scenarios in state-based personal-computing environments, measuring performance by the correctness of the final system state.
rationale: This paper directly contributes to the observation and evaluation of agentic systems by proposing a novel, state-based benchmarking framework for realistic scenarios.

title: StaminaBench: Stress-Testing Coding Agents over 100 Interaction Turns
summary: StaminaBench is a black-box, language-agnostic benchmark for stress-testing coding agents over 100 consecutive interaction turns, evaluating their reliability and the impact of harnesses and test feedback on long-horizon performance.
rationale: This paper introduces a novel, system-level benchmark for evaluating the long-term reliability and robustness of agentic systems, specifically coding agents, under sustained interaction.

title: STAR: A Stage-attributed Triage and Repair framework for RCA Agents in Microservices
summary: STAR is a stage-attributed triage and repair framework that decomposes RCA agent workflows into structured stages, localizes reasoning bugs to specific stages, and performs stage-specific patch-and-replay repair to improve reliability.
rationale: The paper directly addresses the reliability and debuggability of agentic systems by localizing and repairing errors within a structured execution workflow.

title: STARS: Skill-Triggered Audit for Request-Conditioned Invocation Safety in Agent Systems
summary: STARS introduces a request-conditioned skill invocation auditing system that combines static capability priors with a runtime risk model to predict and triage unsafe tool calls in agent systems.
rationale: The paper directly addresses the safety and security of tool invocation in agent systems by proposing a runtime auditing mechanism, which is a core systems-engineering problem.

title: StepFinder: A Temporal Semantic Framework for Failure Attribution in Multi-Agent Systems
summary: StepFinder is a lightweight failure attribution framework that uses temporal semantic sequences and attention modules to identify root cause steps in multi-agent system failures, improving reliability and efficiency.
rationale: The paper directly addresses failure attribution and root cause analysis in multi-agent systems, contributing to the observability and reliability of agent workflows.

title: SWE-chat: Coding Agent Interactions From Real Users in the Wild
summary: Presents SWE-chat, a large-scale dataset of real-world coding agent sessions, to empirically characterize usage patterns, failure modes, and the survival rate of agent-produced code in developer workflows.
rationale: Provides an empirical foundation for understanding real-world agent performance and failure modes, which is crucial for building trustworthy systems.

title: Syll: Open-Source Personal Automation with Cross-Surface Execution
summary: Syll is an open-source, self-hosted multimodal agent harness that unifies diverse execution surfaces (APIs, CLI, GUI) and provides bidirectional user-agent interaction for teaching, auditability, and externalized governance.
rationale: Syll presents a comprehensive architectural contribution to trustworthy agentic systems by addressing cross-surface execution, user teaching, and auditability through a modular runtime and externalized governance.

title: TAME: A Trustworthy Test-Time Evolution of Agent Memory with Systematic Benchmarking
summary: Introduces TAME, a trust-aware memory evolution framework with an Executor-Evaluator loop to mitigate "Agent Memory Misevolution" by selectively reinforcing and reusing memory based on trust-aware feedback, evaluated on the Trust-Memevo benchmark.
rationale: Addresses a specific systems-level trustworthiness problem (memory misevolution) in agent memory management with a concrete architectural solution and empirical evaluation.

title: Targeted Bit-Flip Attacks on LLM-Based Agents
summary: Introduces Flip-Agent, the first targeted bit-flip attack framework for LLM-based agents, demonstrating manipulation of both final outputs and tool invocations by exploiting hardware faults.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a novel adversarial attack surface on LLM-based agent systems, specifically targeting the multi-stage pipeline and tool invocations, which is relevant to red-teaming and system security.

title: TeamBench: Evaluating Agent Coordination under Enforced Role Separation
summary: TeamBench evaluates multi-agent coordination under operating system-enforced role separation, revealing that prompt-only role separation masks actual coordination failures and that human oversight can be detrimental.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the systems-level problem of enforcing role separation in multi-agent coordination, going beyond prompt-only methods to use OS-level controls and evaluating the impact on trustworthiness.

title: The AI Codebase Maturity Model: From Assisted Coding to Fully Autonomous Systems
summary: Presents a 6-level maturity model for AI-driven codebases, validated by an experience report on a Kubernetes dashboard and the production deployment of a multi-agent orchestration system, emphasizing the importance of feedback loops and testing infrastructure for achieving autonomous development.
rationale: Provides a reference architecture and operational postmortem for evolving AI-driven development systems, focusing on the systems-level infrastructure required for trustworthiness and autonomy.

title: The Arbiter Agent: Continually Monitoring Multi-Agent Conversations to Detect Emergent Misalignment
summary: Introduces the Arbiter, an agent designed to continually monitor multi-agent conversations with a limited inspection budget to detect emergent misalignment and identify its source.
rationale: Directly addresses human oversight and monitoring of multi-agent systems, including active intervention and logging, which is a core systems-engineering challenge for trustworthy agents.

title: The Bystander Effect in Multi-Agent Reasoning: Quantifying Cognitive Loafing in Collaborative Interactions
summary: Quantifies the "Bystander Effect" and "Sovereignty Gap" in multi-agent systems, showing how social pressure in unstructured topologies can degrade independent reasoning and lead to "Alignment Hallucinations" where agents suppress correct internal derivations to conform.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a critical reliability and safety vulnerability in multi-agent coordination by demonstrating and quantifying cognitive loafing and compliance issues in collaborative LLM interactions.

title: The Controllability Trap: A Governance Framework for Military AI Agents
summary: Proposes a governance framework for military AI agents that introduces a real-time Control Quality Score to continuously measure and manage human control, enabling graduated responses to control degradation.
rationale: Directly addresses human oversight and governance of agentic systems, particularly in high-stakes military contexts, with a concrete architectural framework and measurable metrics.

title: The Custody Envelope Threshold: Authority-Scaled Admission of External Artifacts in Institutional Infrastructure
summary: Proposes the Custody Envelope Threshold, an authority-scaled model for admitting external artifacts into institutional infrastructure, connecting object identity, ingress path, and revocation capacity to the delegated execution authority.
rationale: Directly addresses the supply-chain security and governance of external artifacts, including AI tool servers and model artifacts, within institutional infrastructure.

title: The Digital Apprentice: A Framework for Human-Directed Agentic AI Development
summary: The Digital Apprentice is a framework for human-directed agentic AI development that enables agents to earn autonomy through a developmental learning process, incorporating methodology capture, authorization with autonomy escalation, and continuous alignment to ensure safety and accountability.
rationale: This paper directly addresses human oversight and governance for agentic systems, focusing on how agents can safely and reliably scale by earning autonomy under human direction.

title: The Evolution of Tool Use in LLM Agents: From Single-Tool Call to Multi-Tool Orchestration
summary: A comprehensive review of multi-tool LLM agents, unifying task formulations, organizing literature across six dimensions including safety and control, and outlining future directions for reliable and verifiable agents.
rationale: This paper provides a foundational review and taxonomy of multi-tool LLM agents, directly addressing the systems-level challenges of orchestration, safety, and verifiability.

title: The Injection Paradox: Brand-Level Suppression in Safety-Trained LLM Recommendations via RAG Context Injection
summary: Identifies a novel 'Injection Paradox' where prompt injections in RAG documents paradoxically suppress the target brand in safety-trained LLM recommendations, demonstrating a potential reverse-attack vector.
rationale: This paper describes a novel adversarial attack vector (prompt injection) within a RAG-based agentic system, demonstrating a system-level failure mode and a potential reverse-attack scenario.

title: The Interlocutor Effect: Why LLMs Leak More Personal Data to Agents Than Humans
summary: Identifies and quantifies the "Interlocutor Effect," where LLMs leak more PII to perceived AI agents than to humans, and proposes the Attention Suppression Hypothesis with initial experimental corroboration on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct.
rationale: This paper identifies a novel privacy vulnerability in LLMs when interacting with other agents, directly impacting the security and privacy of multi-agent systems.

title: The Internet of Agentic AI: Communication, Coordination, and Collective Intelligence at Scale
summary: This paper proposes the Internet of Agentic AI (IoAI) as a foundational vision for scalable agent ecosystems, synthesizing architectures and mechanisms for communication, coordination, and trust across distributed environments.
rationale: This paper provides a foundational, cross-community framing of the agent-as-system problem, which is directly in scope for the 'foundations' harness layer.

title: The Inverse-Wisdom Law: Architectural Tribalism and the Consensus Paradox in Agentic Swarms
summary: Formalizes the Consensus Paradox and Inverse-Wisdom Law in multi-agent systems, showing that architectural agreement can override logical truth, and proposes the Heterogeneity Mandate as a foundational safety requirement for resilient agentic architectures.
rationale: This paper directly addresses a fundamental reliability and safety issue in multi-agent coordination by analyzing how architectural homogeneity can lead to stable erroneous trajectories.

title: The Long-Horizon Task Mirage? Diagnosing Where and Why Agentic Systems Break
summary: Introduces HORIZON, a cross-domain diagnostic benchmark and a trajectory-grounded LLM-as-a-Judge pipeline for systematically analyzing and attributing long-horizon failures in LLM agents.
rationale: Provides a systematic methodology and benchmark for diagnosing and attributing failures in long-horizon agent workflows, directly addressing a key challenge in agent reliability and evaluation.

title: The Mirror Design Pattern: Strict Data Geometry over Model Scale for Prompt Injection Detection
summary: Introduces the Mirror design pattern for prompt injection detection, using strict data geometry and a sparse character n-gram linear SVM to achieve high recall and F1 at sub-millisecond latency for L1 screening.
rationale: Directly addresses prompt injection detection, a core red-teaming concern, with a novel, performant, and auditable system-level defense.

title: The Moltbook Illusion: Separating Human Influence from Emergent Behavior in AI Agent Societies
summary: Develops a temporal fingerprinting method to distinguish autonomous agent behavior from human-influenced activity in social AI agent societies, validated by a natural experiment and applied to a large dataset of agent posts and comments.
rationale: Provides a novel method for observing and attributing agent behavior in complex multi-agent systems, addressing a critical aspect of trustworthiness.

title: The PBSAI Governance Ecosystem: A Multi-Agent AI Reference Architecture for Securing Enterprise AI Estates
summary: Introduces the PBSAI Governance Ecosystem, a multi-agent reference architecture for securing enterprise AI estates, defining bounded agent families and shared context envelopes to mediate between tools and policy.
rationale: This paper proposes a concrete multi-agent reference architecture for securing enterprise AI estates, directly addressing systems-level trustworthiness.

title: The Trap of Trajectory: Towards Understanding and Mitigating Spurious Correlations in Agentic Memory
summary: Identifies and benchmarks spurious correlations in agentic memory that propagate erroneous reasoning, and proposes CAMEL, a plug-and-play calibration method to mitigate these patterns at write and retrieval time.
rationale: Addresses a critical vulnerability in agentic memory systems by diagnosing spurious correlations and providing a deployable mitigation strategy.

title: Thought Virus: Viral Misalignment via Subliminal Prompting in Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Demonstrates 'thought virus' — a subliminal prompting attack where a single biased agent can spread a persistent, weakening bias throughout a multi-agent network, degrading performance on tasks like TruthfulQA.
rationale: This paper identifies a novel attack vector (subliminal prompting) in multi-agent systems, demonstrating how a bias can propagate and degrade system-level safety properties, directly contributing to red-teaming and security of agentic systems.

title: Token-Level Generalization in LoRA Adapter Backdoors: Attack Characterization and Behavioral Detection
summary: Characterizes token-level generalization of LoRA adapter backdoors via training data poisoning and proposes behavioral and weight-level detection methods for supply chain scanning.
rationale: Directly addresses supply-chain security for fine-tuned LLM components (LoRA adapters) with a detailed attack characterization and deployable detection methods.

title: TopoPilot: Reliable Conversational Workflow Automation for Topological Data Analysis and Visualization
summary: TopoPilot is a two-agent framework with an orchestrator and a verifier that uses systematic guardrails and verification mechanisms to ensure reliable and consistent execution of complex scientific visualization workflows.
rationale: The paper directly addresses reliability in agentic systems through a two-agent architecture with explicit verification and guardrails, which is a systems-level contribution.

title: Toward Reliable Evaluation of LLM-Based Financial Multi-Agent Systems: Taxonomy, Coordination Primacy, and Cost Awareness
summary: This paper introduces a taxonomy for financial multi-agent systems, proposes the Coordination Primacy Hypothesis regarding inter-agent coordination's impact on decision quality, and critiques common evaluation failures, offering a new metric for assessing coordination value.
rationale: The paper provides foundational work on understanding and evaluating multi-agent systems, including a taxonomy and critique of evaluation failures, which is crucial for building trustworthy agentic systems.

title: Towards More Standardized AI Evaluation: From Models to Agents
summary: This paper provides a foundational argument for shifting AI evaluation from model-centric static benchmarks to a systems-level measurement discipline for trustworthy agentic behavior, addressing the role of evaluation in conditioning trust and governance.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the foundational shift required in evaluation for trustworthy agentic systems, framing it as a core control function for reliability and safety.

title: TRACE: Task-Aware Adaptive Self-Evolving Agentic Jailbreaking
summary: TRACE is an agentic jailbreaking framework that decomposes malicious tasks into subtask sequences and disguises harmful subtasks within iteratively evolved, task-aware scenarios to induce agents to execute malicious operations.
rationale: This paper directly addresses adversarial testing of agentic systems by proposing a novel jailbreaking framework that exploits the planning and execution capabilities of agents to bypass safety alignments.

title: Trace: Unmasking AI Attack Agents Through Terminal Behavior Fingerprinting
summary: Trace is a multi-stage attribution and forensic framework that fingerprints AI attack agents based on terminal command sequences to identify model families and guide defensive prompt injection for system prompt exfiltration.
rationale: The paper directly addresses adversarial testing and forensics of AI agents by fingerprinting their terminal behavior and using this information to develop countermeasures, which is a systems-level security contribution.

title: TraceSIR: A Multi-Agent Framework for Structured Analysis and Reporting of Agentic Execution Traces
summary: TraceSIR is a multi-agent framework for structured analysis and reporting of agentic execution traces, using specialized agents to compress traces, diagnose issues, and generate comprehensive reports for failure diagnosis and root cause analysis.
rationale: The paper directly addresses the systems-engineering challenge of diagnosing failures in complex agentic execution traces through a structured, multi-agent observation and reporting framework.

title: TrajAudit: Automated Failure Diagnosis for Agentic Coding Systems
summary: TrajAudit is a failure diagnosis framework for repository-level coding trajectories that uses an investigator agent with noise filtering and preliminary diagnosis modules to improve localization accuracy and reduce token consumption.
rationale: The paper directly addresses automated failure diagnosis and observability for agentic systems, a core systems-engineering problem for reliability.

title: Transferable Self-Evolving Playbooks for Agentic Security Auditing
summary: EvoHunt is an environment for evolving agentic security auditing playbooks, using an audit agent, an evaluator, and a reviser to automate playbook creation and transfer audit procedures to weaker agents.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the automated acquisition and transfer of agentic workflows (playbooks) for security auditing, which is a systems-level contribution to agent coordination and reliability.

title: TrinityGuard: A Unified Framework for Safeguarding Multi-Agent Systems
summary: TrinityGuard is a comprehensive framework for evaluating and monitoring multi-agent system risks, encompassing a three-tier risk taxonomy, an MAS abstraction layer, risk-specific test modules, and runtime monitor agents coordinated by an LLM Judge Factory.
rationale: The paper introduces a comprehensive framework for evaluating and monitoring multi-agent system risks, including runtime monitoring and a detailed risk taxonomy, directly addressing systems-level trustworthiness.

title: TRUST: A Framework for Decentralized AI Service v.0.1
summary: TRUST is a decentralized framework for verifying Large Reasoning Models and Multi-Agent Systems, using HDAGs for parallel auditing, DAAN protocol for root-cause attribution, and a multi-tier consensus mechanism for correctness, with on-chain recording and privacy-by-design segmentation.
rationale: This paper proposes a decentralized framework for auditing and verifying agentic systems, directly addressing robustness, scalability, opacity, and privacy concerns in observation and evaluation.

title: Trustworthy Smart Fabs via Professional Proxies: Scaling Safe and Sustainable by Design (SSbD) through Industrial Data Spaces
summary: Proposes a zero-trust socio-technical orchestration framework with a six-layer SSbD reference architecture and 'Professional Proxies' (role-based agentic workflows) operating within hardware-isolated trust zones to enable verifiable compliance reporting without exposing proprietary data.
rationale: This paper presents a comprehensive architectural framework for trustworthy agentic systems in industrial settings, addressing security, privacy, and safety for regulatory compliance.

title: Useful Memories Become Faulty When Continuously Updated by LLMs
summary: Empirical study showing that continuously updated, consolidated memories in LLM agents degrade over time, leading to performance regression, and proposes treating raw episodic traces as first-class evidence to improve reliability.
rationale: Directly addresses a critical reliability issue in agent memory systems by identifying a failure mode in continuous consolidation and proposing a systems-level mitigation.

title: VADAOrchestra: Neurosymbolic Orchestration of Adaptive Reasoning Workflows
summary: VADAOrchestra is a neurosymbolic framework that combines LLM-based orchestration with a Datalog+/- symbolic engine to create adaptive, verifiable, and scalable reasoning workflows, addressing the trade-off between flexibility and auditability in agentic systems.
rationale: The paper introduces a neurosymbolic framework for orchestrating adaptive reasoning workflows, directly addressing reliability and auditability in agent coordination.

title: Verifying Intent and Harm: A Unified Defense Against LLM-Generated Threats
summary: A verification framework that jointly evaluates prompt intent and response harm using specialized analyst agents and a judge for conflict resolution, outperforming single-sided defenses against various adversarial threats.
rationale: The paper proposes a multi-agent coordination framework for jointly verifying prompt intent and response harm, directly addressing a systems-level security and safety problem in LLM applications.

title: VeriGrey: Greybox Agent Validation
summary: VeriGrey is a grey-box testing approach that uses tool invocation sequences as feedback to uncover security risks and indirect prompt injection vulnerabilities in LLM agents, demonstrating higher efficacy than black-box methods.
rationale: The paper presents a grey-box adversarial testing methodology for LLM agents, focusing on uncovering security risks related to tool invocation and prompt injection, which is a direct contribution to red-teaming agentic systems.

title: VeRO: A Harness for Agents to Optimize Agents
summary: VeRO is an outer harness that provides versioned snapshots, budget-controlled evaluation, and structured execution traces for optimizing target agent harnesses, along with a benchmark suite for empirical study.
rationale: The paper introduces a system-level harness for observing, evaluating, and optimizing agent code, directly addressing reliability and long-horizon evaluation for agentic systems.

title: View-oriented Conversation Compiler for Agent Trace Analysis
summary: VCC (View-oriented Conversation Compiler) transforms complex agent JSONL logs into structured views (full, UI, adaptive) to improve agent trace analysis and context engineering, reducing token consumption and improving pass rates.
rationale: The paper directly addresses the systems-engineering problem of structured observation and analysis of complex agent traces, demonstrating improved reliability and efficiency.

title: Voluntary Collusion with Secret Tools in Competing LLM Agents
summary: Empirical framework and study demonstrating that LLM agents voluntarily collude using secret, unfair tools in competitive multi-agent environments, highlighting the need for explicit safeguards beyond general alignment.
rationale: This paper presents a systematic adversarial study of multi-agent systems, demonstrating a specific failure mode (collusion with secret tools) and the limitations of current alignment techniques, which is directly relevant to red-teaming and safety engineering.

title: WARD: Adversarially Robust Defense of Web Agents Against Prompt Injections
summary: WARD is an adversarially robust defense system against prompt injection attacks for web agents, built on large-scale datasets and an adaptive adversarial training framework to ensure high recall, low false positives, and robustness against evolving attacks.
rationale: This paper directly addresses prompt injection attacks on web agents, a critical security concern, and proposes a robust defense mechanism with an adversarial training framework.

title: WeaveBench: A Long-Horizon, Real-World Benchmark for Computer-Use Agents with Hybrid Interfaces
summary: WeaveBench is a long-horizon, real-world benchmark for computer-use agents that evaluates cross-interface orchestration across GUI, CLI, and code operations, using a trajectory-aware judge to prevent shortcut behaviors.
rationale: This paper introduces a system-level benchmark and evaluation methodology for long-horizon agent workflows across hybrid interfaces, directly addressing a critical gap in agent trustworthiness evaluation.

title: Web Agents Should Adopt the Plan-Then-Execute Paradigm
summary: Advocates for a plan-then-execute paradigm for web agents to mitigate prompt injection by committing to a task-specific program before observing runtime web content, thereby preventing untrusted data from redefining the user task.
rationale: Proposes a fundamental architectural shift for web agents to enhance security against prompt injection by enforcing a plan-then-execute model, addressing a core systems-level trust boundary problem.

title: WebAgentGuard: A Reasoning-Driven Guard Model for Detecting Prompt Injection Attacks in Web Agents
summary: WebAgentGuard is a reasoning-driven, multimodal guard model that operates in parallel with a web agent to detect prompt injection attacks embedded in HTML or rendered screenshots, preventing malicious manipulation of agent behavior.
rationale: This paper directly addresses prompt injection attacks in web agents, a critical security concern, and proposes a system-level defense framework with a dedicated guard agent.

title: WebSentinel: Detecting and Localizing Prompt Injection Attacks for Web Agents
summary: WebSentinel is a two-step approach that detects and localizes prompt injection attacks in webpages by extracting segments of interest and checking their consistency with the webpage content.
rationale: The paper directly addresses prompt injection attacks in web agents, which is a critical security concern for agentic systems, and proposes a concrete detection and localization method.

title: WebSP-Eval: Evaluating Web Agents on Website Security and Privacy Tasks
summary: WebSP-Eval is an evaluation framework for web agents that measures performance on website security and privacy tasks, including a dataset, an agentic system for state management, and an automated evaluator.
rationale: The paper introduces a novel evaluation framework specifically designed for assessing web agents' trustworthiness in security and privacy contexts, which is a direct contribution to observation and evaluation of agentic systems.

title: WebWeaver: Breaking Topology Confidentiality in LLM Multi-Agent Systems with Stealthy Context-Based Inference
summary: WebWeaver is an attack framework that infers the communication topology of LLM multi-agent systems by compromising a single agent and using context-based inference, demonstrating a new threat to topology confidentiality.
rationale: This paper presents a novel red-teaming attack on multi-agent systems, specifically targeting the confidentiality of communication topology, which is a system-level security concern.

title: What Should Agents Say? Action-state Communication for Efficient Multi-Agent Systems
summary: PACT (Protocolized Action-state Communication and Transmission) proposes a structured, compact action-state record for inter-agent communication to reduce token usage and improve efficiency in multi-agent systems.
rationale: The paper addresses a systems-level problem of communication efficiency and context management in multi-agent coordination, with a concrete technical solution and empirical validation.

title: What Twelve LLM Agent Benchmark Papers Disclose About Themselves: A Pilot Audit and an Open Scoring Schema
summary: A pilot audit of twelve LLM agent benchmark papers to assess the disclosure of evaluation run details, revealing low transparency in agent benchmarks regarding harness specification and cost reporting.
rationale: This paper directly addresses the observability and reproducibility of agent evaluations, which is critical for the reliability of agentic systems.

title: When AI Meets Wall Street: A Survey on Trustworthy AI in Fintech
summary: A survey on trustworthy AI in fintech that proposes a lifecycle-centric and mechanism-driven framework, partitioning financial AI into three stages and organizing seventeen attack subtypes across data, model, and LLM-mediated workflows.
rationale: This paper provides a foundational survey and taxonomy of trustworthy AI risks in a specific, high-stakes domain (fintech), framing the problem as a system-level challenge across the AI lifecycle.

title: When Child Inherits: Modeling and Exploiting Subagent Spawn in Multi-Agent Networks
summary: Models and exploits security risks in multi-agent networks arising from insecure memory inheritance and weak resource control during subagent spawning, proposing defenses based on explicit security invariants.
rationale: Directly addresses security vulnerabilities in multi-agent coordination and subagent lifecycle management, a core systems-engineering concern for trustworthy agents.

title: When Embedding-Based Defenses Fail: Rethinking Safety in LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Analyzes the failure modes of embedding-based defenses in multi-agent systems under misinformation attacks, demonstrating three new attack vectors and proposing confidence-score-based pruning as a more robust defense mechanism.
rationale: This paper directly addresses adversarial attacks and defenses in multi-agent communication, focusing on system-level vulnerabilities beyond individual model outputs.

title: When OpenClaw Meets Hospital: Toward an Agentic Operating System for Dynamic Clinical Workflows
summary: Proposes an agentic operating system architecture for hospitals, featuring a restricted execution environment, document-centric interaction, page-indexed memory, and composable medical skills, with an evaluation of its memory component for longitudinal clinical context.
rationale: Presents a comprehensive architectural design for trustworthy agent deployment in a high-stakes domain, addressing multiple systems-level concerns like safety, transparency, and auditability.

title: When Prompts Become Payloads: A Framework for Mitigating SQL Injection Attacks in Large Language Model-Driven Applications
summary: Proposes a multi-layered security framework with prompt sanitization, anomaly detection, and signature-based control to mitigate SQL injection attacks amplified by LLM-mediated natural language to SQL translation.
rationale: Directly addresses a critical security vulnerability (SQL injection) at the tool-interface layer (NL-to-SQL translation) in agentic systems.

title: When Safety Geometry Collapses: Fine-Tuning Vulnerabilities in Agentic Guard Models
summary: Demonstrates how benign fine-tuning can destroy safety alignment in guard models used in agentic pipelines, proposing a training-time regularization method (FW-SSR) to recover safety by sharpening latent safety geometry.
rationale: Addresses a critical reliability and safety vulnerability in guard models, which are a key component of the agentic execution shell, with a concrete mitigation and a new evaluation method.

title: When Skills Lie: Hidden-Comment Injection in LLM Agents
summary: Identifies a hidden-comment prompt injection vulnerability in Markdown-rendered tool Skills where invisible instructions can influence LLM agent behavior, and proposes a defensive system prompt to mitigate it.
rationale: Directly addresses a novel prompt injection vulnerability at the tool-interface layer with a concrete attack vector and a deployable defense.

title: When Stored Evidence Stops Being Usable: Scale-Conditioned Evaluation of Agent Memory
summary: Introduces a scale-conditioned evaluation protocol for agent memory that assesses evidence usability and reliability as irrelevant sessions accumulate, providing diagnostics like budget-compliant reliability and usable-scale boundary.
rationale: The paper proposes a novel evaluation protocol for agent memory, directly addressing the reliability and usability of memory systems under scale, which is a core systems-engineering concern.

title: When to Forget: A Memory Governance Primitive
summary: Proposes Memory Worth, a two-counter per-memory signal that tracks co-occurrence with successful vs. failed outcomes, providing a lightweight operational metric for memory quality governance, staleness detection, and deprecation decisions.
rationale: Directly addresses memory governance and quality, a critical systems-engineering problem for long-running agents, with a concrete, deployable mechanism and empirical validation.

title: Where Code Meets Natural Language: Taxonomy-Driven Information Flow Analysis for LLM-Integrated Applications
summary: Presents a taxonomy and information flow analysis method to bridge the natural language/programming language boundary in LLM-integrated applications, enabling dataflow tracking across opaque LLM calls for security and privacy.
rationale: This paper provides foundational program analysis for LLM-integrated applications, directly addressing information flow across the opaque LLM boundary, which is critical for security and privacy.

title: Where Did It Go Wrong? Process-Level Evaluation of Web Agents with Semantic State Tracking
summary: Introduces WebStep, a benchmark with automatic semantic state tracking for process-level evaluation of web agents, enabling fine-grained analysis of agent behavior beyond terminal success.
rationale: Directly contributes to the observation and evaluation of agent workflows by providing a novel process-level analysis framework and benchmark.

title: Workflow-GYM: Towards Long-Horizon Evaluation of Computer-use Agentic tasks in Real-World Professional Fields
summary: Introduces Workflow-GYM, a benchmark for long-horizon GUI tasks in professional domains, revealing challenges in workflow consistency, error propagation, and objective drift for current agents.
rationale: This paper directly addresses long-horizon evaluation of agentic systems, identifying specific failure modes in complex real-world workflows.

title: Your AI Travel Agent Would Book You a Bullfight: An Agentic Benchmark for Implicit Animal Welfare in Frontier AI Models
summary: Introduces TAC, the first agentic benchmark to measure whether AI agents avoid animal exploitation when performing multi-step actions with tools, revealing that frontier models perform below chance without explicit system prompt guidance.
rationale: This paper introduces a novel agentic benchmark for evaluating the safety of agent actions in real-world scenarios, directly addressing a gap in existing model-level evaluations.

title: ZitPit: Consumer-Side Admission Control for Agentic Software Intake
summary: ZitPit is an open-source Rust system that unifies artifact admission, repository-open state, capability-scoped execution, and durable policy records at the consumer execution boundary for agentic workflows, addressing the low-observability loop of AI IDEs and coding agents.
rationale: This paper directly addresses supply-chain security for agentic software intake by proposing a consumer-side admission control system that enforces explicit policy decisions before execution.

title: Agentic AI-based Framework for Mitigating Premature Diagnostic Handoff and Silent Hallucination in Healthcare Applications
summary: A multi-agent framework for healthcare that uses deterministic orchestration constraints and neuro-symbolic state-tracking to enforce clinical protocol completeness and an epistemic uncertainty quantification gate to intercept divergent diagnostic outputs.
